+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Every morning in the ruins began with a negotiation. Not with fate, although fate was usually lurking nearby with a brick in a sock, nor with the weather, which had long ago adopted the attitude that if people were going to survive in spite of everything, it could at least make them damp about it. No, this was a negotiation between Midas and Chewy, which was difficult because only one of them could talk properly, and that was Midas, whose proper talking had never yet improved a bad situation. Chewy sat on an upturned ammunition crate in the old signal tower, his plush fur the color of ancient dust and his plastic nose polished by decades of accidental reverence. One button eye was missing, which gave him the look of a veteran prophet or a toy that had seen too much of the market. Somewhere in his stuffing, old mechanisms clicked and thought private mechanical thoughts. Midas stood at the broken window, studying the morning through a pair of cracked field glasses that made everything look twice as ruined. “All right,” he said. “We have three immediate problems. One, we’re out of lamp oil. Two, we’re nearly out of beans. Three, Pike from the south settlement says if I don’t return his trap springs by week’s end, he’ll personally come and collect them from my ribs.” Chewy whirred. Then, in the bright, jolly voice of a dead civilization, he said, “Tomorrow’s comfort, today!” Midas turned. “That doesn’t mean anything.” Chewy gave a soft internal clunk, like a tiny bureaucrat stamping a form. “Probably beans,” said Midas. “You usually say nonsense about comfort when the answer is beans.” The mission, then, was settled according to the established procedures of the post-technological age: inadequate evidence, wild optimism, and an unwillingness to starve. They would head north to the old market district, where, rumor had it, there was still a cellar under a collapsed grocery warehouse that had not yet been discovered by raiders, scavengers, mushroom farmers, or the kind of goat that could open latches. Midas packed for the expedition with the care of a man who had once learned from experience that rope, knife, canteen, pry-bar, and a small frying pan were all essential survival items, though not necessarily in that order. He slung Chewy over one shoulder in a canvas satchel modified so the koala’s head poked out, partly because he liked the company and partly because people were less likely to stab a man who appeared to be already burdened by madness. By noon they were picking through the old streets. Cities after the Collapse had two surviving instincts: to fall down at inconvenient moments, and to remember where all the sharp bits were. Wind moved through roofless offices and roofless buses and roofless things whose previous purpose was now a matter for theology. Vines ran over everything. Moss furred the old machines. The world had not so much ended as been repossessed by something greener and much more patient. The market district lay beyond a square where a bronze founder of something important had toppled off his plinth and was now pointing permanently into a puddle. Midas took this for a bad omen, but then again in his life all omens were bad omens. Good omens tended to happen to people with family silver. They found the warehouse by following the smell. “There,” said Midas, peering over a ridge of broken concrete. “That has to be it.” It was indeed a grocery warehouse, or had been. Half the roof had collapsed inward, and one wall bulged out suspiciously, like a man pretending not to be drunk. A loading bay yawned blackly beneath a tilted sign that still faintly showed a smiling sun and the words FRESH FOR YOU! in peeling paint. Chewy crackled. “Freshness you can trust!” “That one,” said Midas, “has the ring of a lie.” The loading bay door had rusted into a single opinion, but a side entrance yielded after some labor with the pry-bar and a quantity of language not found in surviving books. Inside, the air was cool and smelled of wet paper, fungus, and old, sealed time. Midas lit a hand-lamp. Shelves leaned drunkenly out of the gloom. Crates lay burst open. There were tins here and there, all swollen or corroded or bearing labels with fruits so cheerful they seemed personally insulting. He grinned. “We may actually be lucky.” This was, in fairness, less a statement than a challenge. The first thing to go wrong was the floor. Old cellars in ruined cities had an unfortunate tendency to believe in surprise. Midas had just stepped around a pyramid of collapsed shelving when the concrete beneath him sighed and vanished. He dropped with a shout through a hail of dust and splinters, landing in darkness in a heap of bruises and vocabulary. Above him, framed by a jagged hole, Chewy peered down from the satchel, upside down. “I’m all right!” Midas shouted. Chewy emitted a triumphant chime and said, “You’re in good hands!” “Not currently!” Midas stood, wincing. He had landed in a lower storage room, remarkably intact. Better yet, around him stood stacks of tins in sealed plastic sleeves. Beans, by the look of them. More beans than he had seen in one place since Old Marta’s wedding feast, and half those had turned out to be pebbles. Then the second thing went wrong. There was a growl from the corner. It was not a large growl, as growls went, but it had confidence. Midas swung the lamp. Two pale eyes reflected back, low to the ground. Then several more. The room, it turned out, was already inhabited by a pack of cellar dogs: the descendants of pets, pests, and poor decisions, all ribbed flanks and damp noses and the patient expressions of creatures who had recently agreed among themselves that lunch ought to come to them more often. “Easy,” said Midas, because people always said that to dangerous animals and each other, and it had never once been binding. He backed toward a stack of crates. The dogs came forward in a crescent. Chewy, perhaps sensing stress through mechanisms not entirely dead, blurted, “Snack time fun for everyone!” The lead dog barked. “Well, that’s not helping!” Midas did the only thing he could. He snatched up a tin, hurled it at the far wall, and when the dogs leapt at the noise he scrambled up the crates like a man trying to become briefly theoretical. The stack swayed. The dogs barked and snapped. A crate split beneath his boot, showering him in old packets of powdered soup. He hauled himself onto a ventilation duct near the ceiling and crawled toward the hole he’d fallen through. Above, unfortunately, new developments were occurring. A face appeared at the edge of the opening. It was narrow, sunburned, and wearing the expression of someone who had just found a coin in another man’s pocket. “Well now,” said the face. “Looks like scavenging’s been done for us.” Two more faces joined it, attached to the sort of men who considered bathing a strategic weakness. Raiders. Naturally. Midas had often felt the world was less a place than a queue of people waiting to rob him in rotation. “That’s my satchel,” he called. One of the raiders lifted Chewy out by an ear. “Your little friend talks?” Chewy’s speaker fizzed. “Value beyond compare!” The raider blinked. “What’s it mean?” “Mostly,” said Midas, edging along the duct while dogs snapped below, “that if you pull his left arm he sings.” The man did. Chewy’s eyes flashed briefly with the last courage of a dying battery. “Buy one, love one, hug one free!” The raiders stared. There are moments in history when civilization hangs by a thread. This was one of them, and the thread was apparently attached to a promotional koala. In that pause, Midas reached the wall beneath the opening, jammed his pry-bar into a seam, and levered with all his weight. The bulging warehouse wall, which had already been considering retirement, finally committed. There came a thunderous crack. Light split the room. Dust exploded upward. The floor shuddered, the dogs fled, and every person involved was suddenly somewhere else. When Midas regained his senses, he was outside under a slope of rubble, half buried in powdered plaster and clutching three tins of beans with the fierce reflexes of the poor. Nearby, one raider staggered out of a bush and immediately ran away on finding himself unexpectedly in possession of a branch through his trousers. Another had vanished entirely, perhaps upward. The third lay dazed in a shopping cart. Chewy, by some malign miracle, sat atop the cart like a small furry king. “Unbeatable savings!” he announced to the afternoon. Midas collected him, the beans, and what remained of his dignity, which fit comfortably in one pocket. The journey home was accomplished in multiple ways, all of them unfortunate. First he limped. Then, trying to cross the old tram bridge, he discovered the planks had rotted through and completed the second part of the journey clinging to a floating door while Chewy bobbed beside him in the satchel, intermittently declaring, “Adventure approved!” in a voice made for suburban delight rather than canal water. Finally, after dragging himself ashore downstream and into sight of the signal tower at sunset, he accepted a ride from Pike in a mule cart. Pike did not mention the trap springs. He merely looked at Midas, looked at Chewy, looked at the three tins of beans, and decided, with the wisdom age occasionally bestows, that some stories were already punishment enough. Back at home base, Midas lit the lamp with the last dribble of oil, opened one precious tin, and found the beans had fused into a single reddish monument. He sat on the ammunition crate beside Chewy and ate what could be carved off with a knife. “Well,” he said at last, “we’re alive.” Chewy’s mechanism hummed softly. Then, with immense satisfaction, he said, “Home is where the happy is.” Midas looked around the cracked tower, the leaking roof, the damp blankets, the beans like masonry. “Yes,” he said. “And apparently happy is where all my bruises are.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations, and ability to find humor in dire circumstances. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both desolate and oddly whimsical, much like Pratchett's Discworld but grounded in a more recognizable reality. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose self-aware commentary and resigned acceptance of his perpetual bad luck feels authentic. His relationship with Chewy, the animatronic koala, is cleverly developed through the contrast between Chewy's cheerful corporate slogans and their increasingly dire circumstances. The koala's pre-programmed responses create genuine humor while serving as a poignant reminder of the lost world. The prose style successfully emulates Pratchett's voice with its mix of philosophical asides, unexpected metaphors, and dry observations. Lines like "Cities after the Collapse had two surviving instincts: to fall down at inconvenient moments, and to remember where all the sharp bits were" capture his distinctive blend of humor and insight. The narrative voice maintains consistency throughout, never breaking character or falling into pastiche. The plot structure follows the requested format perfectly: deliberation, mission planning, everything going wrong, and return home worse for wear. The escalating disasters feel organic rather than forced, each complication arising naturally from the previous one. The warehouse collapse that simultaneously solves and creates problems is particularly well-executed. The world-building is economical but effective, painting a vivid picture of post-technological collapse through carefully chosen details. The author avoids over-explaining, trusting readers to understand the world through context and implication. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being overly clever, with some metaphors feeling slightly forced. The ending, while appropriately bittersweet, could have been more emotionally resonant. Some of the humor, while well-crafted, doesn't quite achieve the effortless quality of Pratchett's best work. The dialogue, particularly Chewy's corporate slogans, works effectively as both humor and characterization. Midas's internal monologue and spoken words feel natural and consistent with his character. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 85.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell over the door gave a startled clang, and then the door itself slammed hard enough to rattle the postcards by the till. “Are you open?” a man demanded, breathless. The owner looked up from a cardboard box of second-hand paperbacks they were pricing on the floor behind the counter. “No, I just stand in here alphabetising for my own amusement.” The man blinked. He had sunglasses on despite the grey afternoon, rain shining on the shoulders of a dark coat, and a knitted scarf wound carelessly at his throat as if someone in a costume department had arranged it to look careless. He half turned, peered through the front window, then ducked lower. “Oh, brilliant,” he muttered. “They saw me come in.” “Who did?” He looked back at them as if they had asked who the king was. “Photographers.” “In Aberaeron?” “Yes, in Aberaeron.” They set a book aside. “Bit niche.” His mouth twitched. “You’d think.” Outside, a movement flashed beyond the rain-streaked glass: two people with cameras lingering under the striped awning of the bakery opposite. The man followed their gaze and let out a low groan. “I only wanted five minutes without someone trying to immortalise me buying toothpaste.” “Famous toothpaste, is it?” He took off the sunglasses at last. He was, irritatingly, exactly the face from the posters and trailers and magazine covers by the till in the newsagent next door: Luca Vale, all dark lashes, crooked smile, and that look of dangerous good humour that had launched a thousand regrettable crushes. In person he seemed taller, wetter, and less invincible. More hunted. Recognition must have shown on the owner’s face, because some of his tension eased and he smiled automatically, polished and bright. “Ah,” he said. “There we are.” “There we are what?” “The moment where you know who I am.” “I know who you are.” He waited. They folded their arms. “Congratulations.” His smile faltered for a fraction. “That’s all?” “What were you hoping for? A commemorative plaque?” A surprised laugh escaped him before he could stop it. “You’re not very welcoming, are you?” “You burst in like a shoplifter in a spy film.” “I’m not a shoplifter.” “Good. It’s mostly impossible to do discreetly in a one-room bookshop.” He glanced around. The shop was narrow and deep, shelves painted a faded blue-grey, little handwritten recommendation cards tucked beneath the spines. A coal fire breathed low in the grate at the back. It smelled of rain-damp wool, old paper, and coffee gone slightly bitter on the hotplate. “Right,” he said. “Then perhaps I should buy something, so my dramatic entrance feels less socially catastrophic.” “That would be the usual arrangement in a bookshop, yes.” He came towards the counter, peeling off wet gloves. “Do you always talk to customers as though they’re mildly disappointing?” “Only the handsome ones. Keeps them from getting complacent.” That startled him enough that he actually looked at them rather than at his own reflection in the window. “You think I’m handsome?” “I think mirrors have probably kept you informed.” His grin returned, slower this time. “And here I was, beginning to think you were immune.” “Immune to what?” “To me.” They reached for the pricing gun again. “You are not smallpox, Mr Vale.” “Luca.” “Mr Vale.” He leaned one hip against the counter. “That feels unnecessarily formal when I’m hiding in your bookshop from a minor siege.” “It’s not your bookshop.” “Not yet.” Their fingers slipped on a paperback cover. Annoying. “That line works on people, does it?” “More often than I deserve.” “Mm.” “Mm?” “It sounds rehearsed.” He put a hand to his chest. “You wound me.” “You’ll live.” He studied them for a second, and something in his expression changed. Less performance, more curiosity. “You really don’t care, do you?” “I care that you’re dripping on Welsh mythology and first editions of R. S. Thomas.” “I can be careful.” He stepped back obediently onto the mat. “There. House-trained.” “You say that with confidence for a man who arrived at speed.” A laugh flickered in his eyes again. “All right. Fair. Have you at least got a back room? If I lurk by the front window they’ll keep taking photos and I’ll look like a damp zoo animal.” The owner hesitated, then nodded toward the back. “There’s a kettle and a chair that only collapses occasionally.” “Luxury.” He followed them between the shelves, ducking instinctively under a hanging sign marked POETRY. “Do they know I’m in here for certain?” “They watched you run in.” “I did not run.” “You absolutely ran.” “I made a swift tactical withdrawal.” “Into a bookshop.” “I panicked.” They looked over a shoulder. “There’s a sentence your publicist would hate.” “You think I have the sort of publicist who hates honesty?” “I think you have the sort who bills by the hour.” “That too.” In the little space at the back, beside stacked boxes and a tiny sink, they switched on the kettle. He stood awkwardly, suddenly too large for the room, rain-dark hair curling at the temple. “You can sit,” they said. He lowered himself into the wonky wooden chair. “If I die, tell them I fell heroically.” “I’ll tell them you were defeated by furniture in Ceredigion.” “Savage.” “Tea?” “Yes, please.” He looked at the shelves nearest him. “You’ve got a good collection.” “You can read the spines upside down. Very impressive.” “I can read whole books, if encouraged.” “Can you?” “Occasionally. Usually on trains and in trailers while people ask me to become emotionally available on command.” They handed him a mug. Their fingers brushed his for the barest second—warm porcelain, cooler skin—and withdrew too quickly. He noticed. Of course he noticed. “Thank you,” he said, quieter. “You’re welcome.” He glanced at a display on the table by the till, visible through the doorway: books on birds, tides, and local walking routes, with a faded leaflet about coastal paths tucked beneath a stone. “You’re a walker.” They looked at him sharply. “What?” “The leaflet. The boots by the radiator. That sunburn line on your wrist where your watch usually is.” His mouth tipped. “I observe things for a living.” “You mean people pay to film your face while you say impossible lines with conviction.” “That too. But yes? You walk?” “On my days off.” “Where?” “Anywhere there are fewer people than books.” They paused. “Mostly the coastal path. Headland beyond New Quay. Sometimes as far as Mwnt if the weather behaves.” His whole expression lit up with something entirely unfeigned. “You’re joking.” “No.” “I walked Mwnt yesterday at six in the morning.” “In this weather?” “It was better at six. Windy, but clear. Saw three choughs and nearly lost a hat.” The owner stared despite themselves. “You know choughs?” “I know red beaks when I’m being judged by them.” “That’s… actually accurate.” “I go walking on location all the time,” he said. “It’s the only way I remember where I am. Otherwise it’s all hotels and cars and someone saying, ‘We need you in makeup in ten.’ If I can get onto a path, I feel human again.” The owner leaned back against the sink before they realised they were doing it. “Most people who come in talking about the coast just mean they’ve bought a second home and a striped jumper.” He winced. “I own neither.” “Good.” “I do own a regrettably expensive waterproof.” “That’s less of a moral failing.” He smiled into his tea. “You approve of me in increments. How exhilarating.” “I didn’t say that.” “No, but you thought I might know one bird from another. We’re making progress.” They should have let that go. Instead they said, “Choughs aren’t crows.” “See? We’re practically engaged.” A laugh escaped them before they could stop it. He looked up at once, triumph flashing over his face—not smug exactly, but relieved, as though he had been knocking on a locked door and finally heard movement inside. “There,” he said softly. “That’s better.” Their cheeks warmed. “Don’t look so pleased with yourself.” “Can’t help it. You laughed.” “You said something ridiculous.” “I say ridiculous things all the time. Usually people laugh because they think they’re meant to.” “And I laughed because?” He held their gaze. “Because you wanted to.” The room seemed suddenly smaller, the air close with steam from the kettle and the rain tapping the back window in uneven fingers. They busied themself with spoons. “Your ego is showing.” He exhaled through a smile, but there was a crack in it now, something uncertain beneath the polish. “It’s not all ego.” “No?” “No.” He looked down at the mug in his hands. “It’s a bit pathetic, actually. Spending enough years being watched does something odd to your head. You start needing people to like you before they’ve even met you. Makes life smoother.” He glanced up. “You, annoyingly, don’t seem interested in helping.” The honesty of it landed with more force than any rehearsed line could have. The owner’s reply came out gentler than intended. “Maybe I’d rather like people after I’ve met them.” He went still. Outside, one of the photographers knocked lightly on the window with a knuckle, trying their luck. Neither of them looked. Luca said, “And after meeting me, how dire is the verdict?” They folded a tea towel in half, then in quarters, simply to have something to do. “Jury’s out.” “Cruel.” “Cautious.” “Is there room for appeal?” “That depends.” “On?” “Whether you’re always like this.” “Charming?” “Needy.” He barked out a laugh, then rubbed a hand over his jaw. “God. Is it that obvious?” “To someone who sells novels all day? Yes.” “And yet you’ve not thrown me out.” “Yet.” His eyes warmed. “You like walking the cliffs. You know your birds. You insult me with unusual precision. I’m beginning to think this is fate.” “I’m beginning to think you say that to everyone.” “Not everyone.” His voice dropped just enough to make the words feel private. “Only the ones who don’t fall for it.” The owner met his gaze and then wished they hadn’t, because there it was again—that dangerous good humour, stripped now of some of its shine, and under it something more appealing: a man trying, against habit, to be sincere. From the front of the shop came the bell as the wind worried at the door. The owner cleared their throat. “If you wait ten more minutes, they’ll get bored.” “And if they don’t?” “You can go out the back, through the alley.” He smiled. “You have a smuggling route.” “I have bins and a gate.” “Still. Romantic.” “Everything sounds romantic in your voice, does it?” He considered that. “Not everything.” Then, with deliberate lightness: “Would it help if I bought half the nature shelf? To thank you for asylum.” “It would help if you bought the one on Welsh coastal birds, since apparently you need educating.” His grin came back, easy and bright. “Now who’s trying to keep me?” They set the book aside for him without answering. He noticed that too. “I’ll come back for it tomorrow.” “Assuming the siege lifts?” “Assuming you let me.” They looked at him, this absurd, rain-soaked man in their cramped back room, all charm and nerves and an ego held together with smiles, and found themself saying, “That depends whether you know the difference between a razorbill and a guillemot by then.” He stood, mug in hand, already smiling like he had been handed a challenge instead of a condition. “Oh, I’ll study.” “I’m sure you will, Mr Vale.” “Luca,” he said. This time, after the smallest pause, they answered, “Luca.” And from the way he looked at them then, you’d have thought he’d been given something far rarer than shelter.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue and action. The characterization is particularly effective. Luca Vale is portrayed as a charming actor with genuine vulnerability beneath his polished exterior - his need for validation feels authentic rather than contrived. The bookstore owner serves as an excellent foil, with their dry wit and apparent immunity to his charm creating compelling tension. The author skillfully shows the owner's internal conflict between their outward resistance and growing attraction. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Luca's lines feel naturally performative yet increasingly genuine, while the owner's responses are consistently sharp and grounded. The banter flows well and serves both character development and plot advancement. The Welsh setting is well-integrated through specific place names (Aberaeron, Mwnt, New Quay) and cultural details that feel authentic rather than tourist-brochure superficial. The bookstore environment is vividly rendered with sensory details that create atmosphere without overwhelming the dialogue. The romantic tension builds organically through their conversation about walking and birds, providing a believable foundation for mutual interest beyond surface attraction. The author avoids rushing the romantic development, instead focusing on the gradual shift from antagonism to genuine connection. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the famous person hiding from paparazzi is a familiar trope, and while executed well, doesn't offer significant innovation. The ending, while satisfying, follows expected romantic comedy beats. The prose occasionally veers toward being overly polished, though it generally maintains appropriate restraint. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through their verbal sparring before allowing moments of genuine connection. The author demonstrates skill in balancing humor with emotional depth, particularly in Luca's admission about his need for approval. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The lock gave beneath Katra’s pick with a soft, indecent little sigh. Aya had expected resistance. Wards that bit like frost through the skin. A second iron gate. A tripline to rouse every cut-throat in Morska from gin-sodden sleep. Instead there was only the hush beyond the threshold, thick and expectant, as though the underhold itself had drawn a breath and held it. She put one gloved hand to the door and pressed. The chamber beyond was long and low-vaulted, built of sweating black stone older than the district above it. Brackets of whalefat lamps burned along the walls, their flames guttering blue behind smoked glass. Brass pipes ran overhead like exposed veins, beading condensation that fell in slow, hollow drips to the flagstones. The air smelt of lamp oil, brine, and some older, more intimate corruption beneath—wet earth from a grave newly opened. At the far end of the room stood the artifact. It rested upon a plinth of green-veined stone within a cage of iron staves etched with warding script. Even in the uncertain light, the thing seemed to drink the eye. A scythan relic, old before the empire had first learned to rivet steel, it resembled a crooked spindle of blackened bone and tarnished silver, wound through with threads that moved when one was not directly looking at them. The sight of it touched that hidden chamber in Aya’s mind where her mother’s lessons still lingered, whispered through tobacco breath and candle smoke. Immateria. A seam in the world. And before it—quite at ease, one hand resting on the head of a cane-sword and the other gloved in lacquered black leather—stood Madame Veleris. The guild leader wore no prison irons. No sign of a raid clung to her save for a fresh scrape at one temple and a dark bloom of powder on the cuff of her coat. Her silver-shot hair was pinned high, severe as a judge’s wig; her burgundy velvet was neat as ever. On either side of her stood two bodyguards in boiled leather and mail, broad men with harpoon-guns slung at the hip and cudgels hanging from loops at the belt. Behind them, half-hidden in the penumbra, lurked three more shapes. Katra stopped so sharply Aya nearly walked into her. Simeon uttered a low oath. And from the quiet came the small, monstrously cheerful sound of a pocket watch ticking in Simeon’s waistcoat. Tick. Tick. Tick. “My dears,” said Madame Veleris, as if greeting tardy guests to supper. “How punctual of you.” Aya felt the cold move over her skin in a single sheet. It was not surprise exactly. It was the recognition of surprise as a thing one had been denied the luxury of. She had gambled on panic in the upper hall, on confusion, on greed. She had not gambled on theatre. Yet here was Veleris, alive and composed, with the stage dressed to her taste. Katra recovered first. “You were taken,” she said flatly. “So I allowed certain persons to conclude.” Madame Veleris inclined her head. “One must from time to time test the tensile strength of one’s organisation. Pull one pin, see what scurries from the floorboards.” Simeon’s watch went on with its infernal little counting. Tick. Tick. Aya knew at once that every route behind them was occupied. She did not turn to verify it. The underhold’s silence had not been empty at all; it had been padded with men. Her fingers curled reflexively inside her sleeves, feeling for the old gestures, the remembered angles with which one might pry at the membrane of the world and bid something dreadful peer through. But this room was crossed with wards. She could feel them the way one feels static before lightning. Brass plates had been riveted into the stones around the plinth and threshold alike, etched and oiled, making a cage not merely for the relic but for anything that trafficked with the immaterial. Men trusted brass and oil and steel. And because they feared magick, they had grown devilishly clever in caging it. Madame Veleris’s gaze settled on Aya’s mask: a neat little thing of black lacquer and silk, absurd now in the underhold’s bleak light. “I confess,” she said, “I had expected Foxglove to be ambitious. I had not expected her to be so very impatient.” Katra shifted her weight. Simeon’s hand twitched near his coat, and one of the bodyguards drew his harpoon-gun higher. “I should advise against heroics,” said Veleris mildly. “The last fellow to attempt them inconvenienced me by dying on my carpet.” Aya stepped forward before either of her companions could speak. “If this is a lesson,” she said, surprised at how level her own voice remained, “you have made your point.” “Have I?” Veleris asked. “With embellishments.” Veleris smiled. It did nothing kind to her face. “You disappoint me, child. Here I had thought to discover who among my underlings possessed a thief’s nerve and a statesman’s patience. Instead I find a trio of gutter Machiavels with a schoolgirl’s sense of timing.” “Schoolgirls,” muttered Katra, “do not generally break into warded vaults.” “No,” said Veleris. “They generally marry magistrates and become dull.” At a minute lift of her fingers, the hidden men behind the door stepped fully into view. Cold iron kissed the backs of Aya’s arms. Simeon drew breath through his teeth but did not resist as his wrists were seized. Katra’s expression hardened into the beautiful vacancy she wore when deciding whether to bite. Aya did not move. It would take so little, she thought, to call. There were things in the dark between moments that remembered her blood. Things with mouths like torn velvet. But the wards buzzed against her senses, needling, and she knew with a dreadful certainty that if she reached now she would not open a door; she would tear herself open against it. Tick. Tick. Tick. “Bind them,” said Veleris. The ropes were tar-stiff and smelled of the docks. Simeon gave a pained little laugh as his arms were jerked behind him. “Well,” he said, “that has gone sour.” “Do hold your tongue,” Katra hissed. Aya’s own captor was methodical. He searched her sleeves, boots, bodice seam. A slim blade, two lockpins, a vial of sleeping tincture. Each was dropped into a waiting tray with insulting tidiness. Then his hand came up to her mask. “No,” she said sharply. It was too sharp. Too quick. Every eye in the room sharpened with interest. Madame Veleris tilted her head. “Now, that is intriguing.” The man ripped the mask free. Cold air struck Aya’s face. For one suspended instant no one spoke. The lamps hissed. Condensation dripped from the pipes. Simeon’s watch clicked on, vulgar in its steadiness. Tick. Tick. Katra shut her eyes briefly, as though in pain. Simeon whispered, “Oh, hell.” Recognition moved over Veleris’s features not as shock but as satisfaction, a card neatly turned in a game already won. “Lady Aya Valcour.” Aya wished, absurdly, that she had had time to powder away the smudge of soot at her jaw. That she had not worn the pearl studs she had forgotten to remove. That her mother had lived long enough to warn her precisely how thin the silk was between two selves before the world delighted in shredding it. The name sounded monstrous here. It dragged with it chandeliers, polished silver, invitations written in a clerk’s precise hand, the family crest stamped in blue wax. Her father’s mouth, thinning to a line. Parliament men. Rectors. The newspapers. She saw with hideous clarity what would follow if this face became attached to Foxglove, to burglary, to conspiracy—and worse, to immateria. Her mother had swung for less than was true. “Well,” said Veleris softly. “You have been full of wonders.” Aya lifted her chin. “Then you know my worth.” Katra’s eyes snapped to her. Simeon made a strangled noise of protest. But Aya pressed on, because she could feel death arranging itself in the room with all the calm of a servant laying out tea things. “If you mean to kill us, kill them and keep me.” A murmur passed among the guards. Veleris watched her with renewed attention. “Is that so?” she asked. “I am not merely another hand in your guild,” Aya said. “You know what house I belong to. You know what circles my father sits in. A daughter returned quietly is one thing. A daughter vanished is another. A daughter exposed—” She let that hang. Her throat felt lined with ash. “That may be useful to you, depending on your appetite for leverage.” Katra stared at her as though she had been struck. “Aya—” “Hush,” Aya said, not looking at her. Simeon’s watch filled the pause. Tick. Tick. Tick. Veleris took one slow step forward, cane tapping stone. “And if I should prefer simpler remedies? A weighted sack. The canal. Your noble sire informed anonymously that his daughter had succumbed to female nerves and taken an unfortunate walk.” “Then you lose a bargaining chip fit to move ministries,” Aya said. “And you lose the one person in this room who has any notion at all what that thing”—she flicked a look toward the artifact—“actually is.” That, at last, touched something. Veleris’s gaze narrowed. “You know of it?” “Enough to know your brass wards are not for ornament.” Aya swallowed. The words tasted like betrayal and survival in equal measure. “Enough to know that if you mean to use it, you require someone the immateria has not already devoured.” One of the bodyguards crossed himself. Katra gave a low, furious laugh. “You should have led with that.” “Had I known we were to be received with such ceremony,” Aya said, “I might have prepared cue cards.” Madame Veleris stopped an arm’s length away. Up close, she smelled faintly of bergamot and gunpowder. “You bargain briskly for a woman with a noose at her throat.” “I was bred to house politics,” Aya said. “Crime merely improved the tutors.” For the first time, Veleris laughed outright. It was a dreadful sound in that sepulchral room. “Very good. Very good indeed.” She turned from Aya and paced once before the caged relic, the blue lampfire stroking the angles of her face into something almost cadaverous. “Here is what shall occur,” she said at length. “Your companions will remain alive so long as you remain cooperative. You, my lady, will explain to me exactly what the Scythans built, exactly what your mother taught you, and exactly how much of the devil is in your blood. Should you lie, I shall know. Should you attempt sorcery, those plates in the floor will cook your nerves like eels in a pan.” Simeon had gone pale beneath the grime. “Madame—” “Silence.” He fell silent. Only the watch continued, each second clipped and merciless. Tick. Tick. Aya breathed once, carefully. She had won nothing but time, and time in such places was often the most expensive thing in the world. Still, it was not death. Not yet. Yet beneath that thin triumph another horror opened its eyes: the certainty that by dawn, someone abovestairs would know whose daughter had been found in the underbelly of Morska with thieves for company and forbidden night in her marrow. Her mother’s face came to her then—not at the scaffold, never there, but by the nursery fire, smiling over a forbidden book and saying, very softly, that the world was full of locks made by frightened men. Aya looked at the brass warding, the guns, the ropes, Madame Veleris’s elegant hands. And thought: yes. But frightened men are diligent locksmiths. “Very well,” she said. The underhold seemed to lean in to hear her.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through detailed descriptions of the underhold with its "sweating black stone," whale-fat lamps, and brass pipes. The imagery is consistently evocative without becoming overwrought. The dialogue feels authentic to the established world and characters. Madame Veleris speaks with the calculated menace of an experienced criminal leader, while Aya maintains aristocratic composure even under duress. The exchanges feel natural and advance both plot and character development effectively. Character actions remain believable throughout. Aya's desperate gambit to leverage her aristocratic identity and magical knowledge as bargaining chips feels like a logical response from someone trained in "house politics." Veleris's elaborate trap demonstrates her cunning leadership style established in the prompt. The piece adheres well to the given instructions, incorporating all required story beats: the trio's capture, Aya's unmasking, and her negotiation attempts. The author avoids writing a fight scene as requested and maintains the gritty Victorian aesthetic throughout. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's internal conflict between survival and the inevitable exposure of her double life. Her realization that "by dawn, someone abovestairs would know" adds genuine stakes and psychological depth. However, the piece occasionally veers toward purple prose, particularly in descriptions like "things with mouths like torn velvet" and some of the more elaborate metaphors. While atmospheric, these moments can feel slightly overwrought. The pacing is generally strong, though there are moments where the descriptive passages slow momentum slightly. The recurring motif of Simeon's pocket watch effectively builds tension. The ending strikes an appropriate dark tone without being gratuitously bleak. Aya's final acceptance feels earned given her circumstances, and the closing image of "frightened men are diligent locksmiths" provides thematic resonance. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 85.5
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bar squats at the edge of the docks like something the tide tried to drown and gave up on. Its neon sign buzzes in sickly pink, one letter dead, and the windows are filmed with grease, salt, and bad decisions. Inside, the air is cheap whiskey, old smoke, and wet wood. A jukebox in the corner coughs out brass through a blown speaker. Nobody looks clean. Everybody looks armed. Spike Spiegel fits right in. He leans on the bar in a wrinkled blue suit that has seen too many fights and too few laundries, cigarette hanging from his mouth, eyes half-lidded like he’s bored by the whole arrangement. His hair does what it wants. His stomach has been empty long enough to be mean about it. He flips open his lighter with one hand. There’s a ring of rust on it that suggests it’s been dropped in water more than once. The flame catches on the second try. The bartender slides him another bourbon without asking. Spike didn’t order it. That means somebody wants something. “Your mark’s upstairs?” Spike asks. The bartender jerks his chin toward the back, toward the stairs with the cracked red runner. “Was. Then a lady showed up.” Spike exhales smoke. “That kind of lady, huh?” Then he hears her laugh. It cuts through the room like a bottle breaking. Revy comes down the stairs with a pistol in each hand and murder in her stride. Black crop top, shorts too short for decency and perfect for movement, boots loud on the wood. The dragon tattoo on her right arm twists when she raises one gun and points it right at Spike’s face. “There you are,” she says. “You the asshole tailing my bounty?” Spike glances at the barrel, then at her, then takes a drink. “Depends. You the asshole slowing me down?” For one second, neither moves. Then her grin shows teeth. “Cute.” “Been told.” She holsters one pistol but leaves the other hanging loose at her side, casual in the way only truly dangerous people can manage. She drops onto the stool beside him and knocks back the whiskey waiting there like she owns the glass, the bottle, and the building. Up close she smells like cordite, sweat, and cigarettes. Spike finds that distracting in a way he doesn’t care to examine. “Name’s Revy,” she says. “Spike.” She looks him over, dismissive and interested at once. “You don’t look like much.” “You look like trouble.” “I am.” “Yeah,” Spike says, mouth curving around the cigarette. “I noticed.” The bartender retreats to the far end. Smart man. “Target’s named Julio Vask,” Revy says. “Runs girls, guns, and anything else that fits in a crate. Lagoon Company got paid to collect him breathing if possible.” “Bebop got the same call. Breathing is optional.” Revy barks out another laugh. “Now that sounds more realistic.” “So what’s the play?” Spike asks. She turns on the stool, one knee brushing his. Deliberate. Testing. “Play is you walk away before I put a hole through that stupid hair.” Spike looks down at the contact, then up at her face. “You flirt with everybody like this?” “Only the ones I might shoot.” “That’s kind of my type.” Her eyes narrow, amused despite herself. “You always this relaxed with a gun in your face?” Spike shrugs. “Whatever happens, happens.” That lands differently than he means it to. Revy studies him, and for a moment there’s something behind the aggression, a recognition of another person too comfortable with dying. Then the back door slams open. Three men rush in, all cheap suits and cheaper trigger discipline. Julio’s backup. The first one shouts something pointless. The second reaches inside his jacket. Spike moves before the sentence finishes. His stool tips back as he kicks off the bar, bourbon glass spinning away. Revy is already firing. The room detonates. Her twin pistols roar in alternating flashes, brass raining over the floorboards. Spike draws in one smooth motion, one-handed and precise, his own shots economical where hers are savage. A bottle explodes behind the bar. The jukebox dies mid-note. One thug drops clutching his shoulder, another folds over a gut wound, and the third dives behind a table and sprays rounds wild enough to shred two lamps and the mirror. Spike vaults the bar, low and fluid, using the wood for cover. His prosthetic eye catches the glare wrong for a split second, depth skidding sideways, but muscle memory compensates. He comes up just enough to fire twice. The third man yelps and loses the table with him. Revy doesn’t bother with cover for long. She stalks forward through smoke with both guns up, furious and beautiful and completely insane. The last conscious thug scrambles for the stairs. She shoots the banister beside his head just to watch him panic. “Where’s Julio?” she snaps. “He bailed! Back alley!” Spike is already moving for the rear exit. “Race you.” “Eat shit.” They hit the alley shoulder to shoulder, neon reflecting in puddles of oil and rainwater. Julio Vask is halfway to a sedan, fat panic in a white suit, clutching a briefcase to his chest like it might save him. Two bodyguards turn and fire. Spike slides behind a dumpster, returns two shots. Revy goes the other direction, boots splashing through a puddle, firing both pistols while moving with impossible balance. One guard spins and drops. The other catches Spike advancing and swings the muzzle toward him. Revy’s bullet takes the man through the wrist. Spike closes the distance and drives a Jeet Kune Do side kick into the guard’s sternum hard enough to put him over the hood of the sedan. Julio bolts. They both sight in on him at once. “Leg,” Spike says. “Head,” Revy says. They fire together. Julio screams and crashes face-first into the pavement, one knee blown out and the briefcase skidding into the gutter. Silence settles in ragged and smoky. Revy lowers her guns first. “You clipped his ear.” “You shot his knee after I called leg.” “You’re welcome.” Spike retrieves the briefcase. Empty. “Decoy.” “Of course it is.” Revy holsters and steps close enough to jab a finger into his chest. “We split the bounty.” “Or we hand him over and let the broker decide.” She smirks. “You trust brokers?” He looks at her finger on his shirt. “You trust anybody?” That wipes the smirk away. By the time they dump Julio with a fence willing to ask fewer questions than usual, it’s past midnight and raining hard enough to turn the streetlights to watercolor. The broker stalls on payment until morning. Standard scam. Spike is too broke to get righteous about it. Revy is too drunk to wait quietly. So they end up in a motel across from the docks, the kind that rents by the hour and washes the sheets only when they remember. The room is all nicotine-stained walls, buzzing lamp, bedspread with cigarette burns. Rain taps the window unit. Revy sits on the dresser with a bottle between her knees. Spike leans against the door, smoking. His jacket is off. Her hair is damp around her face. Both of them look like they should leave. Neither does. “You cheat at cards?” Revy asks. “I don’t play cards with people who carry this many guns.” “You saying you’re scared?” “I’m saying if you lose, I’m the furniture.” She snorts, drinks, wipes her mouth with the back of her hand. “You got this whole dead man sleepwalking thing going on. That real, or just some line you use?” Spike watches the smoke curl toward the ceiling. “Real enough.” “And the eye?” He glances at her. “You ask everybody this many questions?” “Only when I can’t figure out if I wanna fuck ‘em or kill ‘em.” A beat passes. Spike’s smile is small, tired, dangerous. “And?” Revy slides off the dresser. For a second it seems like she might cross the room and kiss him, hit him, maybe both. Instead she draws one pistol and points it at his chest. Not trembling. Not joking. Spike’s own gun is in his hand a breath later, aimed at her ribs. The rain gets louder. They stand there in the motel’s yellow light, breathing smoke and whiskey fumes, close enough to touch, too close for clean shooting. Her dragon tattoo flexes with her grip. His expression is calm in the way of people who have run out of reasons to flinch. “You’re trouble,” Revy says softly. “You said that already.” “You keep looking at me like you know something.” Spike tilts his head. “I know you don’t trust rooms with one door.” Her mouth hardens. Because it’s true. “And you,” she says, “look like if I let you get close, you’d disappear the second it meant something.” That’s true too. Neither lowers the gun. Neither looks away. Outside, somewhere down by the harbor, a ship horn moans through the rain. The motel light buzzes. Whiskey sits open on the dresser. The bed remains untouched. Spike’s finger rests alongside the trigger guard. Revy’s smile returns, slow and sharp and not entirely sane. “Morning,” she says, “we settle the bounty.” Spike takes one last drag, lets the cigarette fall, and crushes it under his heel without breaking eye contact. “Sure,” he says. “Whatever happens, happens.” The room holds its breath with them.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author captures both Spike and Revy's voices authentically, maintaining their distinct personalities while creating believable chemistry. The noir atmosphere is well-established through vivid environmental details - the seedy bar, rain-soaked alley, and grimy motel all feel authentic to both source materials. The action sequences are particularly well-crafted, with clear choreography and appropriate pacing. The gunfight flows naturally and showcases each character's fighting style effectively. The dialogue crackles with tension and personality, avoiding exposition dumps while revealing character through subtext. Character portrayal is nuanced and faithful to source material. Spike's fatalistic attitude and casual competence shine through, while Revy's volatility and underlying vulnerability are well-balanced. Their mutual attraction develops organically through shared danger and recognition of similar damage. The prose style is confident and atmospheric without becoming overwrought. Descriptions are economical but evocative, particularly in establishing mood and setting. The writing maintains consistent noir tone throughout. The standoff ending is thematically appropriate and avoids easy resolution. Both characters remain true to their trust issues while acknowledging their attraction, creating genuine tension. The unresolved nature feels earned rather than frustrating. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive phrasing and a few moments where the prose edges toward purple territory, though these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. The pacing is generally strong, though the middle section could be slightly tighter. The piece successfully fulfills all prompt requirements while maintaining literary quality and character integrity. It reads like professional genre fiction rather than amateur fanfiction. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Astrid told me to wear wool and not tell anyone where we were going. She said it in the school corridor that afternoon, with the same flat certainty she used when she told me which teachers drank, which boys stole from the ICA, which girls read their mothers’ diaries and then cried in gym class as if their sorrow had arrived from nowhere. In the months since we had moved north from Stockholm, I had learned that Astrid never lowered her voice for things other people might call strange. She made the strange sound practical, almost boring. That was part of why I followed her. “Not synthetic,” she said, tugging at the sleeve of my jacket. “If you sit out, you should wear wool. And no metal if you can help it.” “Sit out where?” She looked at me as if I was being dense on purpose. “Útiseta. I told you about it. On a mound. At midnight.” I laughed then, because that was what I always did a second too late, after she had already moved on from deciding whether she was joking. “You’re serious.” “Yes,” she said. “Do you want to know things or not?” That was how she framed all of it. Not as games, exactly, though she called them midnight games when she wanted to make me less wary. Not even as magic. More like shortcuts. Old ways of listening where other people had forgotten there was anything to hear. Once she had shown me how to bury a slip of paper under the school steps with someone’s name on it and a knot tied in black thread. The next day that person had been sent home sick after vomiting in biology. Another time we had sat in the equipment shed behind the gym and melted candle wax into water to see who liked whom. That one had worked too well, or maybe not at all; afterward I could not look at a certain pair of initials on a desk without feeling implicated. By midnight the village was a scatter of yellow squares under a low sky that never became properly dark in summer, though it was late enough in the year now that the light had thinned to a bruised blue. I climbed out of my bedroom window because the front door always groaned and my mother slept lightly in the new house, as if she still had not forgiven its walls for not being the walls she knew. The grass was wet to my ankles. Astrid was waiting by the road with no reflector on, her bicycle laid in the ditch. “You came,” she said. “I said I would.” “You say lots of things.” She handed me a folded wool blanket and a little paper packet tied with string. Inside was coarse salt, a pinch of something dried and bitter-smelling, and three matchsticks. “What’s this?” “In case you get frightened and want to stop.” She began pushing her bicycle rather than riding it. “Or in case something notices you first.” I wanted to ask what counted as something, but that would have sounded childish. So I walked beside her along the gravel road, then onto a narrower path through the pines. The forest there was open and clean beneath the trees, the ground springy with old needles and pale reindeer moss. Every so often the wind moved through the branches and the pines rustled in long, dry sighs, almost like faint whispers from farther in among the trunks. I told myself that was exactly what it was: wind, trees, my city ears making language out of weather. Astrid knew where she was going without a torch. We came to a clearing I had never seen in daylight, with a rise in the ground at its center, round and too regular to be natural once you were close enough to notice. Stones ringed it, half-swallowed by lichen. The mound was not large. That made it worse somehow. It was intimate. Human-scaled. Meant. “This is it?” I whispered. Astrid shrugged. “There are older ones. This one is near enough to get back before morning.” I stared at her. “You’re talking about it like a bus route.” “That’s better than talking about it like Americans in a horror film.” She said horror film in English, making fun of me because my Stockholm accent still came out strongest when I was annoyed. Then she crouched and set down her bag. “Listen carefully,” she said. “My mormor said if you sit out for knowledge, you shouldn’t ask big questions. Nothing like will I die young or where do the dead go. That’s how idiots get tricked.” “Tricked by what?” She ignored that. “You ask something near to you. Specific. A person, maybe. Something hidden. And you sit on the mound alone, without crossing your arms or legs. You don’t pray. You don’t call on God. You don’t speak to whatever comes first. If you hear your name behind you, you don’t turn around.” A laugh tried to climb up my throat and failed. “And this is what your grandmother actually taught you?” “She taught me what not to do. The rest you can work out.” “That’s not comforting.” “It isn’t meant to be.” She marked a rough circle in the dirt with the heel of her boot. Not around the mound but at its base, as if giving me a boundary that was also not protection. She sprinkled a little salt on the ground and tucked the remainder back into the packet. “That part’s mine,” she said. “The salt isn’t traditional. Mormor said people add things when they don’t trust themselves.” “Do you trust yourself?” She considered. “More than I trust you.” I should have gone home then. I can say that now with the luxury of hindsight and age and the ordinary cowardice of survival. But at sixteen, humiliation was stronger than fear. I had spent months being the girl from Stockholm, the one whose boots were wrong, whose vowels were wrong, whose every attempt at belonging carried a shine of effort visible to everyone but me. Astrid, with her calm eyes and local roots and stories that made the village seem older and stranger than the maps admitted, had chosen me. Or that was what I thought. “What do I ask?” I said. “Someone at school. You wanted to know about Erik.” I felt heat rise in my face even in the cold. “No.” She smirked. “Then ask about Karin. You’re always pretending not to care what she says about you.” I hated how accurately she chose. Karin had lived in the village all her life and had perfected the art of smiling while excluding me from every conversation before I reached it. “Fine,” I said. “I ask about Karin.” Astrid nodded. “Good. Sit.” The top of the mound was flatter than it looked, the grass cropped short by deer or sheep. It felt wrong to put my full weight there. I spread the blanket and sat cross-legged until Astrid hissed, “No, not like that,” and made me uncross my legs and place my hands palm-down on my thighs. “Eyes open,” she said. “If you close them, you’ll only get yourself.” “And what do you do?” “Wait.” She stepped back beyond the ring of stones, becoming a darker shape among the trees. For a while nothing happened. My breathing was too loud. The air smelled of damp earth, resin, and something cold under both of them, like stone pulled from deep water. I fixed my eyes on a pale rock at the edge of the clearing and tried to form the question in my head clearly enough to count as asking. What did Karin say about me when I wasn’t there? It sounded petty as soon as I thought it. The whole thing did. Two girls at midnight on a grave, wanting gossip from the dead. The wind moved again. The pines whispered around us. Then I heard another sound: a soft click, as if two stones had touched. I glanced toward Astrid, but she stood still, arms at her sides. Somewhere in the forest a branch cracked. An animal, I thought immediately. Moose, maybe. Or a fox. There were always explanations here, practical ones, local ones, and my problem was that I did not know them well enough. Minutes passed. Or many minutes. Time on the mound thickened strangely. My feet began to go numb. I became aware of every small sensation—the drag of wool against my calves, a mosquito whining near my ear, my pulse in my fingertips. Then, very distinctly, I smelled cigarette smoke. My father smoked in secret out by the garage when my mother was in the bath. It was such a familiar smell, so misplaced in that clearing, that my eyes filled with tears before I understood why. I turned my head a fraction. “Don’t,” Astrid said sharply from the dark. “I smell—” “I know.” The smoke vanished. In its place came another odor, sweet and stale, like flowers left too long in warm water. Something touched the back of my shoulder. Not a hand. Not exactly. More like the sensation of pressure without weight, the way a limb can feel occupied just before it goes pins and needles. Every muscle in my body locked. The night seemed to narrow around me until there was only the mound, my skin, the violent beat of my heart. Then, close to my left ear, I heard my name. Not spoken dramatically. Not a ghost in a film. Just a voice pitched low, almost conversational, as if someone standing beside me had leaned down to get my attention. “Linnea.” I did not turn around. I could not have, even if I had wanted to. My jaw hurt from clenching. From somewhere beyond the ring of stones, Astrid said, “Ask now.” Her voice sounded thinner than before. Frightened, or excited. I couldn’t tell which. My mouth was dry. “What does Karin say about me?” Silence. Then, from behind me—or inside my own head, or from the wind worrying the needles loose in the pines—I heard, very softly: “That you wanted to be chosen.” I stood up so fast the blanket slid from the mound. The pressure at my shoulder disappeared. I stumbled down the side, nearly twisting my ankle, and Astrid caught my arm hard enough to bruise. “Did you hear it?” she whispered. I pulled away from her. “You said that. You had to have said that.” “I was over here.” “You could still have—” “Did it sound like me?” No. That was the worst part. It had not sounded like her. It had not sounded old or young, male or female. It had sounded familiar in the way certain dreams are familiar, as if I had always known that voice and only forgotten it while awake. Astrid’s face was pale and avid in the half-light. “What did it say?” I did not answer. I was suddenly certain that telling would make something final, would move the whole night from embarrassment into fact. We packed in silence. She did not tease me for wanting to leave. She did not ask again until we reached the road and the village lights showed through the trees. Even then I only said, “Probably nothing.” Astrid looked back toward the forest. The wind passed through the pines one more time, whispering in that dry, secretive way. “Probably,” she said. But neither of us sounded convinced.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in executing folk horror within the specified parameters. The author successfully captures the voice of an adult Linnea recalling teenage events, maintaining the requested first-person past tense perspective while keeping the framing device appropriately transparent. The 1990s Swedish setting feels authentic through specific details like the ICA store and cultural references. The characterization is particularly effective. Astrid emerges as a compelling figure who treats supernatural practices with matter-of-fact practicality, making her both believable as a local teenager and genuinely unsettling. Her line "That's better than talking about it like Americans in a horror film" perfectly captures her dismissive attitude toward dramatic interpretations of what she considers routine knowledge. Linnea's psychology as an outsider desperate to belong rings true, and her motivations for participating despite growing unease feel earned. The prose maintains an elegant restraint that serves the folk horror genre well. Descriptions like "the forest there was open and clean beneath the trees, the ground springy with old needles and pale reindeer moss" create vivid sense of place without overindulgence. The author skillfully builds tension through accumulating details and sensory experiences rather than melodramatic language. The supernatural ambiguity is expertly handled. The cigarette smoke that reminds Linnea of her father, the pressure without weight, and the voice that answers her question all maintain plausible deniability while creating genuine unease. The response "That you wanted to be chosen" is particularly effective—it's both psychologically penetrating and potentially supernatural, addressing Linnea's core insecurity about belonging. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development well. Astrid's instructions mix teenage casualness with eerily specific traditional knowledge, while Linnea's responses reveal her conflicted desire to belong versus her growing fear. The conversations avoid exposition dumps while conveying necessary information about the ritual. The pacing builds effectively toward the climactic moment on the mound, then handles the aftermath with appropriate restraint. The ending maintains the crucial ambiguity while suggesting lasting impact on both characters. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the prose occasionally edges toward being slightly overwrought, and a few instances where character motivations could be more deeply explored. However, these are minimal concerns in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances multiple complex elements. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
