+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 89.5
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
Before dawn the ludus smelled of damp straw, lamp smoke, and men. I woke to the scrape of a shovel in the yard and the wet cough of Dama in the bunk below mine. For a little while I did not move. The air still held the coolness of night, and in that narrow hour before the city began shouting, I could pretend I was nowhere at all—not in Rome, not in the stone belly of the training school, not a man counted by the lanista among his other costly beasts. Then the trumpet from some distant barracks sounded over the rooftops, thin and brazen in the dark, and the spell broke. “Up,” came old Galenus’s voice from the doorway, rough as a file on iron. “Unless you mean to sleep through your own fame.” A few men laughed. Most only groaned. Straw rustled; bare feet slapped the floor. Someone swore in Punic when he cracked his shin against a bench. I sat up and rubbed the stiffness from my shoulder. My body remembered every day that had come before: the old scar under my ribs from the first owner who taught me obedience with a spear butt, the ropey seam at my thigh from the arena at Capua, the deep ache in my wrists from endless drilling with shield and wooden sword. They were the tally marks of a life that no augur had read for me at birth. By the time we filed into the yard, the eastern sky above the wall had gone from black to ash. The city beyond was waking. I heard cart wheels on stone, the bray of mules, the sing-song cry of a baker advertising hot loaves. Somewhere farther off, from the direction of the Subura, rose the shrill argument of women at a fountain. Rome was never truly silent. Even half asleep, she muttered. We trained before breakfast. We always did. Galenus said hunger made a man attentive, though I thought he simply liked seeing us uncomfortable. He had once fought in the amphitheater at Pompeii and still carried himself as if the crowd had not stopped roaring for him. His nose had been broken too many times to sit straight on his face, and one ear was puffed and folded like boiled leather. He struck at our shields with a vine staff as we moved through the forms. “Again.” We stepped and turned in the pale light, wooden weapons thudding against posts wrapped in rope and rags. Dust rose around our ankles. Sweat came quickly, stinging my eyes. The younger men grunted with effort, eager to show themselves worthy of notice. The veterans saved their breath. Every now and then the smell of baking bread drifted over the wall and made the whole yard ache with wanting. I was paired that morning with a Thracian called Cotys, narrow-faced and quick. He had been sold after some tax revolt in the north, though whether he had truly raised a hand against Rome or merely lived where Rome needed an example, I never knew. He smiled when he fought in practice, a habit I distrusted. “You are slow today,” he said, circling me with his blunted sica. “I am old,” I told him. “You are not old. You are Roman old. That begins at thirty.” I barked a laugh despite myself and turned his wrist with my shield. We touched weapons, stepped apart, and went on. Around us men cursed in Latin, Greek, Gaulish, and tongues I did not know. That was the empire in one yard: conquered men taught to entertain their conquerors, each carrying his own gods in secret and learning the same rules of survival. When at last Galenus released us, the kitchen slaves brought out our meal in steaming bowls: barley porridge thick as plaster, beans, a little cheese sweating in the warmth. We sat along the wall and ate with wooden spoons. My hands shook slightly from exertion. A fly landed on the rim of my bowl and I flicked it away. Above us, beyond the bars of the high gate, a strip of morning sky had turned blue. Through it I could see the upper stories of insulae leaning crookedly over the lane, laundry already strung between windows like signal flags. Rome climbed on top of herself. The rich spread wide over hills and gardens; the poor piled upward in timber and brick, always one spark away from disaster. Yet all roads, all taxes, all grain, all fear came here in the end. Dama lowered his voice and jerked his chin toward the office. “The editor comes today.” That sharpened every man at once. The editor was the magistrate who paid for the games, though in Rome payment was never simple generosity. A man staged spectacles the way generals raised triumphs: to purchase love, to drown memory, to make the crowd feel itself powerful while it remained obedient. “Which one?” asked Cotys. “The aedile with the dyed beard,” Dama said. “The one who smiles too much.” “Then he wants votes,” I said. Dama snorted. “What else does any Roman want?” That was not fair, but it was not false. The city fed on favors and promises. Grain dole, public baths, races in the Circus, beasts from Africa, executions at noon, gladiators in the afternoon. Bread and games, some muttered with contempt, but those same men still came to watch. The emperors understood what calmed hunger and what directed anger. Better the crowd shout for blood in the amphitheater than at the doors of the Senate or the palace on the Palatine. By midmorning the editor arrived with attendants, perfumed enough that we smelled them before we saw them. The lanista hurried out to meet him, all oily deference and practiced concern, wringing his hands over the quality of his stock. We were lined in the yard, oiled and clean, scars visible, muscles shown to advantage. We stood as horses might stand at market. The editor was indeed the one with the dyed beard, black as a raven’s wing though his brows showed silver. He wore a white toga with a border so bright it seemed to gather the sun. Beside him trailed a clerk carrying wax tablets, and behind them two boys fanned at flies with feathers. The editor walked the line appraising us with a politician’s eye—calculating not our souls, nor even quite our skill, but our effect. “This one,” he said, tapping Cotys on the shoulder. “The crowd likes the curved blade.” “Excellent taste,” murmured the lanista. “This African as well. And him.” A finger pointed at me. “The murmillo. He carries himself well.” Carries himself well. As if I were a serving bowl or a statue to be set in a niche. I kept my face still. Men had died for less than an expression interpreted as insolence. But inside me something tightened, not fear exactly, not yet. It was that old feeling I got when chosen: the sense of narrowing, as if the broad untidy world had suddenly become a single gate. After they went away, the yard loosened again. Some men were elated, some darkly quiet. To be selected for a high official’s games could mean prize money, gifts, even the eye of someone influential. It could also mean a larger crowd, louder demands, less mercy if the people wanted death. Fame in the arena was a torch: bright, hot, and hungry. Near noon we were allowed to wash. I poured cold water from a bucket over my head and stood shivering while dust streaked my feet and the channel at the edge of the yard ran brown. Then I sat in the patch of sun by the wall to mend the leather tie on my arm guard. The city smell had thickened in the heat—olive oil, dung, hot stone, fish sauce from some cookshop nearby, and, beneath it all, the Tiber’s sluggish breath. It was an ugly smell and a rich one. Empire had an odor. It was spice unloaded from the East beside sewage in the gutter; incense in a shrine and blood in the sand. I took from under my tunic the little object I never let the others touch: a small disk of bone drilled through with a cord, polished smooth by my thumb. It had once been part of a sheep’s knucklebone charm. My mother had tied it around my neck when I was a boy and told me that Fortune, being blind, might be persuaded by touch. Before every fight I rubbed it three times and whispered the names of the dead I wished to remember. My brother first. Then my mother. Then, in recent years, the names of men from the ludus who had gone under the sand and not risen from it. It was a foolish ritual, perhaps. We all had our foolishness. One man spat over his left shoulder, another refused to lace his right greave before the left. Galenus, who mocked us all, touched the threshold of the armory each time he entered. No one lived by steel without bargaining with unseen things. That day, though the games were not until tomorrow, I rubbed the bone charm in my palm and felt shame at the tremor in my hand. I was not afraid only of dying. Death was a coin always on the table. What I feared was being spent for nothing—cut down to furnish a magistrate’s applause, my blood helping some office-seeker climb one rung higher while the crowd forgot my name before sunset. Yet there were moments, too, when I wanted the crowd. I hated them, and I wanted them. Their roar could lift a man out of himself. In the arena I had sometimes felt more visible than any senator wrapped in dignity, more real than the marble men in the Forum. Perhaps that was the cruelest trick of it: they taught us to crave the gaze that consumed us. In the afternoon a shadow crossed the yard wall, and with it came the long rolling cheer from far away—the Circus, perhaps, or some public announcement in the Forum. Rome exhaled and shouted and demanded, as she always did. I sat with the leather tie in my lap and listened. Beyond the wall were emperors, tax collectors, widows, bankers, priests, thieves, slaves carrying chamber pots, philosophers disputing under colonnades, children chasing dogs through alleys. Beyond the wall was the whole machinery that turned grain from Egypt into loyalty, conquest into law, suffering into spectacle. Inside the wall, I tested the knot, pulled it tight, and waited for the next day to come.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and deep understanding of both the Roman period and literary technique. The writing successfully creates an immersive experience through the first-person perspective of a gladiator, avoiding the obvious choice of combat to instead explore the quieter, more psychologically complex moments of preparation and reflection. The prose is consistently elegant without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "the stone belly of the training school" and "Empire had an odor" are evocative without being purple. The author demonstrates excellent control of pacing, moving smoothly between action (training), dialogue, and introspection. The sensory details are particularly strong - the smells of "damp straw, lamp smoke, and men," the sounds of the waking city, the physical sensations of training and eating. Character development is nuanced and believable. The protagonist emerges as a complex figure - neither noble savage nor broken victim, but a man caught between hatred and desire for the crowd's approval. His interactions with fellow gladiators like Cotys and his observations about Roman society feel authentic. The supporting characters, though briefly sketched, have distinct voices and purposes. The historical context is woven seamlessly into the narrative without feeling like a history lesson. The author captures the political machinery of Rome - the editor seeking votes through games, the bread and circuses dynamic, the empire's diversity represented in the ludus - while maintaining focus on the personal story. The emotional complexity is perhaps the piece's greatest strength. The protagonist's conflicted feelings about his situation, his shame at his trembling hand, his simultaneous hatred and craving for the crowd's attention, create genuine psychological depth. The ending strikes an appropriate note of uncertainty and resignation without false hope or melodrama. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and period-appropriate. Each character has a distinct voice, from Galenus's gruff authority to Cotys's sardonic observations. The conversations advance both plot and character development efficiently. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the philosophical observations feel slightly too modern in their articulation, though this is a common challenge in historical fiction. The piece also might have benefited from slightly more specific historical anchoring - while clearly set during the height of the empire, it could be any emperor's reign. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 19.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**North Muir Light Station** **Isle of Creag Bàn** **3 March 1958** Dear Mam, I’m here safe. The boat left me and the stores at half ten. Mr Rattray showed me the valves and the foghorn and all, though fast, as if he was already ashore in his head. The tower is sound enough. Whitewashed walls. Iron stair. Three rooms in the keeper’s house and all of them colder than a chapel. You asked if I’d got sea legs from Leith and the answer is no. I was sick twice coming in and once after, though that may have been the paraffin smell in the lamp room. It’s queer once the boat is gone. There is no one to wave to after a point because they’re too far and you only stand there like a fool with your hand up and the gulls seeing it. My hours are set by the light and weather. Trim and clean in the morning, polish the lens, check the clockwork, wind the mechanism, note visibility, keep records, maintain stores, and if the haar comes down, sound the horn every thirty seconds. There’s a weight-drive on the rotation, same as a big clock. I’ve to crank it up regular. If I let it run down, some poor devil could put his ship on the rocks below. So don’t think me grand because it’s a lighthouse. It’s mostly wiping soot and carrying oil and listening to bolts complain. Still, wages are wages. Better than the yard after Christmas. Tell Jean I got her scarf and I’m wearing it despite the colour. Your loving son, Alec --- **19 March 1958** **9 Giles Street, Leith** Dear Alec, I am glad to hear from you and gladder still to know you arrived in one piece, though I wish you had not mentioned being sick as it put me queer all over again just reading it. Your room here is as you left it except cleaner. Jean says to tell you the scarf is not “a colour,” it is maroon, and if you had any sense you would know the difference. I do not think you grand. I think you far away and in the wet, which is not the same thing. I have told Mrs Kelly downstairs that my son keeps a lighthouse and now she says that sounds romantic. I told her romance is for people that have never had chilblains. You may use that if anyone says the same to you. Eat proper meals. Men on their own go strange with tins. And write more than “I’m here safe” next time, because I had half a mind to come and fetch you back after such a poor letter. All my love, Mam P.S. Your brother Davie still owes me 17 shillings and now avoids my eye at tea. --- **North Muir Light Station** **7 April 1958** Mam, You wanted more, so here is more. The worst job is the horn. In clear weather it sits dumb and ugly on the cliff edge like a rusted bull. In fog it owns your whole head. You set the compressor going and the air stinks hot and metallic, then every half minute it bellows through the mist till your ribs seem to answer it. There was a haar three days running this week. I slept in snatches between blasts and came to hate my own watch. The steps from house to tower are green with slime where the sea throws itself up. I went down Tuesday with a sack of coal and near broke my neck. Coal all over, me on my backside, cursing enough for the minister to hear if he had been a seal. I’ve mended the hen run, though one hen has stopped laying from spite or nerves. There are six left from the last keeper’s lot. I talk to them more than I’d admit ashore. You’ll laugh, but I’ve started reading aloud in the evening just to hear a voice in the room. Stevenson mostly, because he sounds right here. If I read the paper, every bit of Parliament seems daft by lantern light. There’s a ship that passes west on Thursdays, near enough I can see washing on the line if the weather’s clean. I find myself waiting for it as if expected. No need to worry. I’m managing. Alec --- **15 April 1958** Dear son, I did laugh about the hens, though not unkindly. You are your father all over with that “No need to worry” after telling me three things to worry about. If you have fallen, say if you are hurt. If not, say so plain. Mrs Kelly has now told two more people and you have become, in the street, “that lighthouse boy,” though you are twenty-eight and no boy. There are worse reputations. I sent a parcel yesterday with oatcakes, a jar of jam, wool socks, and your father’s old harmonica if the damp has not ruined it. You can make an awful racket and call it company. Jean is engaged to a young man called Robert Menzies who works with buses and has nice hair. I do not fully trust nice hair. Davie has paid 10 shillings and claims the rest was a misunderstanding. I told him arithmetic is not a matter of opinion. Do not let yourself get into a state. Isolation is a thing. Dr Ferguson said so when Mr Laird’s nephew was up in the Borders by himself and began seeing lights. Which was unfortunate as his business was lights. Your loving Mam --- **North Muir Light Station** **2 June 1958** Mam, I’ve had your parcel this good while and should have written sooner. The jam went in a week. The socks are a blessing. The harmonica is less of one to any creature with ears, but I thank you. I’m not hurt from the fall. Bruised only. It’s lighter now, with the summer. Strange to need the lamp less and yet be busier. Salt gets into everything. The gallery rail wants scraping. The paint on the south side blisters. The cistern pump sticks. There’s no such thing as “done.” Last Thursday I watched that westbound ship again. This time she answered the signal with two blasts, not for need, just acknowledgement. Silly as it sounds, I was cheered all day by it. There was another thing. A small boat was found stove in below the east rocks after the gale. No one in her. Fishing creel, one boot, no name. I searched with the glasses when the tide eased but saw nothing but weed lifting. It put me low after. A man can vanish here and leave less behind than a broken crate. That’s the real bother of this place, Mam. Not loneliness exactly. More that everything feels one slip away from being erased. You trim the wick, mark the log, scrub the brass, boil the tea, and all of it is to hold back a dark that does not care. It gets in your thinking if you let it. Ignore me. I’m tired, that’s all. Alec --- **10 June 1958** Alec, I will not ignore you when you are saying true things badly. You are lonely. There is no shame in using the right word. Men make themselves ill trying to step around it. What you do matters exactly because the dark does not care. There are mothers who sleep because a light turns where it should. There are men coming home who never know your name and live by your work all the same. That is not nothing. Your father knew that sort of labour. The tramlines had to be right though nobody thanked the man with frozen fingers at dawn. I am saying this because I know your habit of souring on your own worth. If it grows too much, ask for relief. Pride is dearer than passage and less useful. With love, Mam P.S. Jean’s Robert has parted his hair differently and I trust him less. --- **Telegram** **North Muir to Mrs McBride, Leith** **28 October 1958** STORM DAMAGED LANDING STEPS NO BOAT THREE WEEKS STOP I AM SAFE STOP SUPPLIES LOW BUT MANAGEABLE STOP DO NOT FRET STOP ALEC --- **1 November 1958** Dear Alec, I have already fretted, as you well knew I would. I took your telegram to the Board office and made a nuisance of myself till a clerk looked up the station reports. He said if weather permits a relief boat will go out by the end of the week. I told him weather never permits anything; men decide. I am enclosing this though I do not know when you will have it. Please be careful. Low supplies is not “manageable” if you start saying that with your lips blue. Eat the hens before you starve out of politeness. I dreamed last night of that horn you described, only in the dream it was calling from the stairwell here and no one else in the house heard it. Come home at Christmas if there is any sense left in administration. Love, Mam --- **North Muir Light Station** **14 December 1958** Mam, The letters came in a bundle with stores after the sea finally relented. I’ve put off writing because I did not know how. There was a bad night on the 23rd of November. Wind east by north, thick snow squalls, visibility gone in flashes then back enough to show the rocks white with breaking water. The landing steps were already half smashed. Near midnight the clockwork on the lamp began sticking. Salt had got in somewhere after all my cleaning. The rotation slowed. I could see it, a hesitation in the beam, enough to make your skin crawl. I stripped the housing with my hands shaking and the whole while the horn was sounding and the tower moving under me like a thing breathing. Then the compressor failed. No horn. Snow. Black sea. I worked till I could not feel my fingers. Took the valve apart on the floor with a torch in my teeth. Got it going again by luck and force. But in the middle of it, while the light was near stopping, I thought: if this goes out and something strikes because of me, I will hear it and never be rid of it. That thought has not left. A Danish coaster came by next day farther out than usual and gave three blasts. Whether for thanks or greeting or chance, I do not know. I sat down after and cried like a bairn, which I haven’t done since Dad died. I think I cannot do another winter here. Your son, Alec --- **20 December 1958** My dear Alec, Then you will not. I have written the Board already and enclosed your last letter copied in my own hand since men in offices believe mothers less than official paper. Dr Ferguson has also written, using words like strain and exhaustion and unfit for continued solitary posting. Let them argue with him. Come home as soon as relief reaches you. There is no bravery in grinding yourself to powder for wages. I wish I could have been there to put my hand on your head as when you were wee and feverish. Since I cannot, this must do: you did enough. More than enough. The light did not go out. No ship struck. You are not answerable for every wave God made. Jean is married now and Robert’s hair has settled into something tolerable. Davie finally paid the 17 shillings entire, proving miracles happen in port cities too. Your bed is ready. I aired the blankets yesterday. All my love, Mam --- **Leith** **6 February 1959** Dear Mam, I’m writing this from the kitchen though I could just say it over tea. Maybe letters suit some things better now. I’ve got work at Granton, not the yard proper but maintenance on the harbour lights and buoys. Mr Sinclair from the Board helped place me, perhaps to keep you from storming their office again. It’s outdoors, honest, and I come home at night. That last is the chief wonder. I slept the first week back as if hauled from deep water. Since then I’ve been easier. Not entirely. Some nights a lorry brake in the street sounds like the horn and I’m up before I know where I am. But less often. I went to the shore yesterday at Newhaven and watched the lamps come on one by one along the Forth. I felt, not sorrow exactly, but respect for the man still out on Creag Bàn. I hope they send two next time in winter, though hoping and Boards are poor companions. You were right, which I mention sparingly to preserve balance in the universe. It was loneliness. And fear, if I’m plain. Not fear of ghosts or weather. Fear of failing alone where no one could help and no one would know till after. Writing to you was the bit that kept me tied to the land. So thank you. For the jam, the bad advice about hens, the better advice about pride, and for not making a song and dance when I came in thin and shaky and cross. I’ve mended the back gate this morning. A thing with hinges and no sea under it is satisfying beyond words. Your loving son, Alec
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity. The author successfully creates two distinct voices - Alec's terse, working-class Scottish dialect contrasts effectively with his mother's more direct, maternal tone. The correspondence spans nearly a year, showing a gradual psychological deterioration followed by recovery. The characterization is particularly strong. Alec's voice feels authentic - his reluctance to admit vulnerability, his dry observations about mundane tasks, and his gradual opening up about his fears all ring true. The mother's voice is equally well-crafted, showing maternal concern without sentimentality, practical wisdom, and gentle humor. Her progression from light worry to serious intervention feels natural. The setting comes alive through specific, unglamorous details - the paraffin smell, slime-covered steps, salt corrosion, and the mechanical demands of lighthouse keeping. The author avoids romanticizing the lighthouse keeper's life, instead showing it as repetitive, physically demanding work with genuine psychological hazards. The emotional arc builds convincingly from initial adjustment through growing isolation to crisis and resolution. The November storm sequence is particularly effective, showing both external danger and internal breakdown without melodrama. Alec's admission of crying "like a bairn" feels earned rather than manipulative. The prose style is restrained and period-appropriate. Dialogue within letters feels natural, and the author shows rather than tells emotional states through concrete details and actions. The Scottish dialect is handled subtly without becoming caricature. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the isolated worker breaking down, the wise mother providing support, the return to safer work. The resolution, while not unearned, follows expected patterns. The writing occasionally borders on being too neat in its emotional beats. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - acknowledging lasting effects of trauma while showing genuine recovery. The final letter's reflection on loneliness and fear feels honest rather than overly optimistic. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I promised myself I wasn’t going to do recon. That was the deal I made in the bathroom mirror before I left my apartment: you are going to a restaurant, not a penetration test. You are meeting a person, not an attack surface. You are allowed, for one evening, to stop seeing the world as a layered stack of bad assumptions wrapped in human optimism. Then I got to the restaurant ten minutes early and immediately noticed the host stand iPad was running a version of iOS old enough to have voting rights, the QR code menu redirected through a link shortener I wouldn’t trust with a grocery list, and the point-of-sale terminal had a default admin sticker still on the underside if you knew where to look. So. Not a perfect start. I made myself sit with my back to the room, which is the emotional equivalent of disabling root access. Hard. Unnatural. Sweaty. The restaurant was one of those dimly lit places trying to look effortless while charging twelve dollars for olives. Exposed brick. Edison bulbs. The kind of place where everyone pretends they don’t hear the neighboring breakup because hearing would make them complicit. When Maya walked in, I forgot the host stand, the POS, the unsecured guest Wi-Fi called *BistroGuest* with no captive portal, all of it. Okay, not forgot. Backgrounded. She smiled when she saw me, a quick nervous smile like she was also trying very hard not to run diagnostics on the evening. We did the awkward first-date half-hug, half-handshake that always feels like two operating systems trying to mount each other’s file structures and failing gracefully. She sat down. We did the opening exchange. Work, commute, “sorry parking was impossible,” the usual handshake protocol. I tried to keep my eyes at face level and not dart to her watch, her bag, the phone she set screen-down on the table for exactly three seconds before flipping it face-up again. People always think their tells are in what they say. Mostly they are in what they protect. She was easier to talk to than her texts had suggested. In messages she had been funny, but in a rate-limited way, like she worried about packet loss. In person she was quick and dry and observant. Good. Better than good. Dangerous. We made it through appetizers without me mentioning that the menu’s online payment portal embedded a third-party script from a domain registered six weeks ago. Growth. Real personal growth. Then she said, “Okay, so this might be a dealbreaker, but I spent way too much of college money on cardboard.” My brain, traitorous and immediate: cardboard? Which cardboard? I said, “That depends. Beanie Babies or Magic?” She laughed. “Magic.” And just like that the night opened. There are moments on first dates where the conversation stops being a sequence of probes and starts feeling like play. This was one. We went from careful small talk to stack interaction speed. Favorite formats, first decks, worst financial decision disguised as a mana base, the ethics of infinite combos in casual pods, the social contract, the absolute moral bankruptcy of saying “my deck is a seven.” She was a Commander player, of course. Everyone over thirty with enough disposable income to ruin themselves tastefully is a Commander player. As she talked, part of my mind—the part that never clocks out—started cataloging every card name she dropped, not because I wanted to, but because I always map systems when I’m inside them. She mentioned **Meren of Clan Nel Toth** first. Graveyard recursion, attrition, value from death. She likes inevitability. Likes turning loss into infrastructure. Then **Skullclamp**. Efficient, slightly evil, says she is willing to look you in the eye while extracting maximum utility from a doomed board. Then **Dictate of Erebos**. Ah. So not merely inevitability—shared suffering. She prefers games where everyone learns together. She said she used to run **Birthing Pod** until her playgroup “politely staged an intervention.” Toolbox thinker. Ladder climber. Sacrifice as optimization. Later she referenced **Kokusho, the Evening Star**, with a wistful little smile. Nostalgic for dramatic life swings. Likes a table reaction. She confessed a brief fling with **Yuriko, the Tiger’s Shadow** because “I wanted to see what being the villain felt like.” Translation: she enjoys precision, asymmetry, and the occasional weaponized surprise, but wants plausible deniability. Then **Cyclonic Rift**, and she physically winced before saying, “I know, I know.” So she has a conscience, but not one strong enough to leave power on the table. **Teferi’s Protection**. Defensive foresight. An instinct to preserve optionality. **Dockside Extortionist**. Fine, yes, everyone runs Dockside if their conscience can absorb market price as a moral category. Still: opportunist, resource spike, capability escalation. **Austere Command** over **Wrath of God** because “modularity is hot.” I nearly said marry me. Instead I took a sip of water and tried to look like a person who had ever been normal in public. I offered my own disclosures in return. **Niv-Mizzet, Parun**. **Ashiok, Dream Render**. **Stony Silence** if I want to lose friends and influence no one. She grinned in exactly the right places. We were establishing compatibility through cardboard priors, inferring ethics from mana curves and preferred removal suites. Better than astrology. More honest than résumés. By the time the mains arrived, I was thinking this might actually work. Which was when she picked up her phone. It happened fast. A notification buzzed, she glanced at the screen, and with the muscle memory of all unthinking habits she traced her unlock pattern. I saw it. Not because I was trying to see it. Because once you’ve spent enough years breaking things—legally now, contractually, invoiced with proper line items, but still—you don’t stop noticing. The pattern was a simple L-shape with a retrace tendency and a visible grease trace under the pendant lights. Top left to center to bottom right would have been better. This one was practically a confession. Four nodes. Corner anchored. Common shape class. Observable from two meters at a lousy angle. Given the smudge profile, attack complexity approximately: embarrassing. And before my social filter could patch the outbound traffic, I heard myself say, “You should really change that.” Silence. Not total silence. Restaurant silence. Silverware, low music, someone behind me saying “no, but like emotionally I’m in my startup era.” But at our table, an application crash. Maya’s eyes lifted slowly from the phone to my face. “Excuse me?” Abort. Roll back. Restore from backup. I felt heat rise up my neck. “I—sorry. That came out wrong.” “How,” she said, very evenly, “could that possibly come out right?” Correct question. Excellent question. If this were an incident report, root cause would be: engineer forgot humans don’t enjoy unsolicited vulnerability assessments over dinner. I could lie. I could say I guessed. I could minimize. People do that all the time after they trip over someone’s boundary: they exploit ambiguity and hope not to get caught in the logs. But if there is one thing reform has made me cherish, it’s clean accounting. “I work in security,” I said. “I notice stuff. Too much stuff. It’s a bad habit and that was rude. I’m sorry.” She leaned back. Not dramatically. Just enough to increase distance. “You looked at my phone.” “I looked up when you picked it up.” “That’s not better.” “No,” I admitted. “It isn’t.” The waiter materialized with the expression of a man who knew instinctively not to ask whether we wanted pepper. Inside my head, everything forked. One branch said: You idiot. You had a live connection and you injected panic into it. Wonderful. Truly elegant self-sabotage. Another branch, colder, said: This is useful information. Her reaction indicates strong boundary awareness, low tolerance for surveillance framing, probably prior experience with someone invasive. Which, congratulations, you have now imitated in appetizer-to-entrée time. Also, because my brain is a goblin rifling through side channels even now: she had mentioned **Swiftfoot Boots** earlier. Protective equipment, hexproof preference over shroud because she still wants agency. Of course this hit a nerve. She likes defenses she can control. “Do you tell everyone their passwords suck?” she asked. “Only if I want them to hate me.” A beat. Not a good joke. Too self-aware to charm, not self-aware enough to absolve. She exhaled through her nose anyway, not quite a laugh. CRC check uncertain. “I’m serious,” I said. “I shouldn’t have said anything like that. You don’t know me well enough for that to be anything but creepy.” “That is refreshingly correct.” “I’m trying this new thing where I act like a human man on dates.” “How’s it going?” I looked at my plate. “Mixed results.” That got a real laugh. Small, but real. Connection re-established? No. But maybe the handshake hadn’t fully failed. She turned her phone over, face-down again. “For what it’s worth, I changed from a PIN because I can never remember numbers.” “Biometrics,” I said automatically, then wanted to throw myself into the decorative candle. Her eyebrow lifted. “Sorry. Sorry. I know. I hear it.” “You really can’t help yourself, can you?” And there it was—not anger, exactly. Curiosity. Which is more dangerous. Curiosity can become interest or caution depending on what you feed it. “I can,” I said. “I just didn’t. There’s a difference.” That seemed to land. Honesty is not a fix, but it’s a decent first patch. She studied me for a second, doing her own threat modeling. I wondered what she saw. A man who made eye contact but also scanned exits. Someone who knew when software updates were overdue by the shape of a settings menu. Someone who was trying, visibly, not to reduce her to a set of exploit vectors and leaked metadata. Someone failing imperfectly. I thought of the cards again, involuntary and fond. **Meren**. **Skullclamp**. **Austere Command**. A player who expects some mess, plans for recursion, keeps the pieces worth saving. “So,” she said, cutting into her food, “if my phone security is terrible, what does my deck say about me?” Ah. A test. Or an olive branch disguised as one. Best kind. I considered making a joke and decided to risk sincerity instead. “That you like systems that keep working after disruption. That you enjoy having options. That you don’t mind being underestimated if it gives you time to build. And that if someone wipes your board, they probably made your position better.” She chewed, thinking. “That’s… annoyingly perceptive.” “I’m trying to use my powers for good now.” “Now?” There it was: the little opening under the firewall. Not trust. Not yet. But willingness to inspect the certificate. I smiled. “First-date answer or real answer?” She smiled back, cautious but genuine. “Depends. Which one is less incriminating?” I glanced at her face-down phone, then deliberately kept my eyes on hers. “Probably the real one.” And the night, improbably, did not crash. Not fully. We kept talking. About old decks and bad decisions and why **Sol Ring** is both sacred and stupid. About the ethics of optimization, in games and outside them. I did not mention her phone again. She mentioned **Propaganda**, **Smothering Tithe**, and **Reanimate**, and I stored each one away, not as leverage this time, but as clues in the gentler sense: evidence of a person assembling herself through preferences, jokes, and chosen engines. I still noticed the unsecured Wi-Fi. I still noticed the waiter reusing a four-digit code on the payment device. I still noticed the camera over the bar pointed low enough to catch unlock gestures if someone had motive and patience. But for most of the rest of dinner, I noticed her more. Which, for me, is as close to grace as it gets.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging narrative premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout - a reformed hacker whose analytical mindset creates both humor and genuine interpersonal tension. The internal monologue feels authentic and consistent, blending technical terminology with emotional vulnerability in a way that never feels forced or performative. The Magic: The Gathering conceit works brilliantly as both a bonding mechanism and a character development tool. The protagonist's analysis of Maya's card choices reveals his compulsive need to systematize everything, including human behavior, while simultaneously showing genuine affection and compatibility. This dual function elevates what could have been mere nerdy flavor text into meaningful characterization. The central conflict - the phone unlock pattern observation - feels entirely believable and well-executed. The protagonist's inability to filter his professional observations creates genuine cringe, and Maya's reaction feels proportionate and realistic. The aftermath avoids both easy forgiveness and dramatic overreaction, instead finding a middle ground that suggests two adults navigating a genuine social misstep. The prose demonstrates sophisticated control, particularly in its use of technical metaphors that illuminate emotional states without becoming precious. Phrases like "emotional equivalent of disabling root access" and "two operating systems trying to mount each other's file structures" are clever without being show-offy, and they serve the character's perspective authentically. The piece maintains excellent pacing, building tension through the protagonist's mounting observations before the inevitable social collision. The resolution feels earned - not a complete redemption, but a realistic salvage operation that suggests potential without guaranteeing success. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors pile up slightly too densely, and some descriptions of the restaurant setting feel somewhat generic despite the specific details. The ending, while appropriate, could have pushed slightly further into either direction - complete disaster or tentative hope. The dialogue, though limited, rings true, particularly Maya's responses which feel like genuine human reactions rather than plot devices. The piece successfully balances humor with genuine emotional stakes, creating a protagonist who is simultaneously sympathetic and genuinely problematic. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time Ms. Ortega says, “I’ll assign partners,” my pulse is already acting like I’m being chased through the woods instead of sitting in third-period science with a mechanical pencil and a half-finished diagram of a plant cell. It always happens when teachers say partners. There’s this exact half second where everyone in the room becomes an animal and the air changes. Chairs squeak. People start looking around too casually. Nobody wants to be the last person left, the human remainder. I’m preparing myself to get paired with whichever person forgot their folder and says “wait, what are we doing?” halfway through every lab, when Ms. Ortega looks at her clipboard and says, “Maya, you’ll work with Evan.” My first thought is not a thought so much as a bright electrical event. My second thought is: don’t react. So obviously I react internally with the force of a collapsing star while trying to make my face look like this is neutral information, like she just said, “The mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell,” and not, “You will now spend forty minutes sharing oxygen with the boy you have been stupidly, inconveniently crushing on since October.” Okay. Stand up normally. Don’t trip on backpack. Don’t smile too much. Don’t smile too little, because then you’ll look offended to be paired with him, which is insane, because actually this is either the best thing that has happened all week or the beginning of my public humiliation, and honestly those are not mutually exclusive. I carry my notebook over to lab table four and sit down across from him. “Hey,” he says. This should be easy. There are only so many appropriate ways to reply to “hey.” It is probably the least dangerous word in the English language. One syllable. No hidden traps. “Hi,” I say. Good. Fine. Human. We’re in. Except now I am aware of everything. The way I put my notebook down too carefully, which probably looks weirdly formal. The fact that my left hand doesn’t know what to do, so it rests on the table like a prop hand in a school play. The exact angle of my shoulders. The possibility that my face is doing something incorrect. Am I making eye contact the normal amount? There should be a chart for this. Eye contact every three seconds for one second? Less if speaking? More if listening? Why is there no official government pamphlet on how to act like a person? Ms. Ortega starts explaining the assignment—design an experiment to test which conditions affect mold growth on bread the fastest—and I write down everything because writing is easier than existing. Next to “variables,” I doodle a tiny loaf of bread with a crown, because if I don’t draw in the margins my brain starts running laps. At the top of the page, where nobody can see unless they’re aggressively invading my space, I make tiny sketches for everyone at our table group. Jada gets a little lightning bolt because she talks fast and bright, like every sentence already has exclamation marks built into it. Luis gets a gear because he always understands the equipment before the rest of us. And Evan— No. Don’t make his too obvious. If someone saw a heart I would have to move to another state. I draw him as a small telescope. Which is somehow worse, because now I’m the kind of person who assigns symbolic doodles to her crush in her science notes like a Victorian heroine with access to graphite. “Do you want to test temperature?” Evan asks. This is good. Normal project talk. Safe territory. Just answer. “Temperature would be a good variable because—” “Yeah,” I say. Just “yeah.” Great. Incredible contribution, Maya. Future Nobel Prize winner. Why use your full vocabulary when you can respond like a malfunctioning NPC? He waits, probably expecting the rest of the sentence to arrive. I can feel it hovering in my throat like a bird hitting a window. Because temperature is measurable. Because we could keep the moisture level constant. Because there are clear control conditions and less room for random contamination. Because I know things. Because in my head I am capable of speaking in complete paragraphs. “Because it’s easy to control,” I add, finally, and even that comes out too quiet. He nods. “That makes sense.” He is so normal about everything. That’s almost the worst part. He just says things and doesn’t seem to spend the next twelve minutes auditing them for possible stupidity. Imagine that. Imagine talking and then moving on with your life. Okay. New goal: be useful. Useful is safer than interesting. Interesting can become weird in half a second. Useful is just competence with less risk. “We could do three slices,” I say, before my courage expires. “One in the fridge, one at room temperature, one near the radiator. And take pictures every day.” There. Full sentence. Maybe even two and a half. “That’s smart,” he says. My brain immediately splits into factions. Faction One: He said that’s smart because he thinks you’re smart. Faction Two: He says “that’s smart” to everyone, calm down. Faction Three: Did he say it in a polite voice? Was it pity-smart? Charity-smart? There are probably seventeen subtypes of “smart,” and now I have to analyze all of them until my spine turns to dust. I pretend to be writing while actually replaying the exact intonation of his voice. This is a disease. Say something else. Something casual. Maybe a joke. Not too funny. If it’s too funny, it looks try-hard. If it’s not funny enough, it’s just a failed joke, which is worse than saying nothing because then there’s evidence. Possible line: Guess we get to grow something in science besides stress. No. Too rehearsed. Possible line: Bread is kind of gross even before the mold. No, that’s not a joke, that’s just anti-bread propaganda. Possible line: At least this lab smells better than the owl pellet one. Actually decent. Relevant. Shared classroom history. Human. I open my mouth. Jada says, “If you guys need extra petri dishes, we have four,” and the moment evaporates, which is almost a relief because now I can stop deciding whether my face should look lightly amused before, during, and after speaking. “Thanks,” I say to her, and that comes out perfectly normal because of course it does. Apparently my personality only malfunctions in one very specific direction. Evan is sketching a chart on scrap paper. His handwriting is messy in a confident way, like he doesn’t doubt that words deserve space. I wonder what it feels like to move through the world without pre-editing every gesture. To scratch your eyebrow without worrying somebody will think you’re making a face. To laugh without instantly checking whether it was too loud, too fake, too much. Maybe I should ask him something not about science. That’s what normal people do when they’re sitting together. They create conversation. They don’t sit in highly monitored silence like they’re trying not to set off lasers in a museum. What could I ask? Do you still play soccer? No, because then if he says yes, I have to prove this is a normal thing to know and not the result of remembering everything he has ever mentioned in class. Did you understand the math homework? Too utilitarian. Also sounds like I’m using him for answers. Are you doing anything this weekend? Absolutely not. That is how desperate people in teen shows talk right before they are publicly rejected near lockers. I doodle a tiny thermometer beside the bread king. Then, because I need somewhere to put the extra static in my body, I shade the crown darker than necessary until the paper starts to go soft. He looks over at my notes. My entire central nervous system leaves my body. “Those are cool,” he says. What. What are cool. No. Not the doodles. I glance down like I don’t know what belongs to me. “Oh.” Excellent start. “The little drawings,” he says. “The bread guy.” The bread guy. I have never been so grateful for an object’s existence. “Oh. Thanks.” Don’t overexplain. Don’t say I always draw when I’m nervous. Don’t say I make icons for people because then he’ll ask which one is his and I’ll have to pass away on the spot. “It helps me remember stuff.” That is true enough to count. He smiles. “You should draw the mold evolution like a video game boss.” And because this is not a question about my soul or my weekend or whether I have been secretly cataloging his existence for months, I can answer it. “It starts as Level One Spores,” I say, and my voice comes out almost steady, “and then by Day Five it’s, like, Fungal Overlord.” He laughs. Not a polite exhale. An actual laugh. For one whole second, I forget to monitor myself. It’s like missing a stair and somehow not falling. Warm rush, then immediate panic about the warm rush. Did I look too pleased? I definitely looked too pleased. My smile is probably enormous and weird. Reduce smile by twenty percent. No, now it’s probably vanished too fast and looks unnatural, like a robot completing a smile protocol. Why can’t my face just be on autopilot like everyone else’s seems to be? But he’s still smiling, drawing a ridiculous monster cloud on the edge of the chart, and Ms. Ortega is talking to another table, and nobody is staring, and nothing catastrophic has happened. I am still here. The room has not opened beneath me. Maybe this is the most exhausting part: not that I think everything will go wrong, exactly, but that I’m always braced for it, always holding every part of myself in midair like if I loosen my grip for one second, something embarrassing and permanent will crash to the floor. We go back to planning. I suggest labeling the bags. He says we should make a prediction chart. I write neatly; he tapes the bread bags crooked; somehow this feels companionable enough to be dangerous. When the bell rings, everyone starts packing up in that loud chaotic stampede way that makes the end of class feel like a minor evacuation. I close my notebook before anyone can see the telescope doodle. “See you tomorrow, lab partner,” Evan says. Lab partner. Such harmless words. Such devastatingly harmless words. “Yeah,” I say, then force myself to add, “See you.” And on the walk out, while my brain immediately begins a full forensic review of every syllable I produced in the last forty minutes, another thought slips through the noise, small but bright: He laughed. At my joke. I was there when it happened. And somehow, despite all my overthinking, I didn’t disappear.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's specific requirements. The narrative successfully captures the voice of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety, maintaining consistent first-person internal monologue throughout. The author skillfully portrays the exhausting mental gymnastics of overthinking every social interaction, from the protagonist's pre-rehearsed conversations to her constant self-monitoring. The characterization is particularly strong. Maya feels authentically adolescent - her intelligence shines through her internal voice while her anxiety creates a believable gap between thought and speech. Her tendency to assign symbolic doodles to classmates and her detailed analysis of social interactions ring true for a bright, anxious teenager. The supporting character of Evan is rendered naturally, neither too perfect nor dismissive, which makes Maya's crush feel realistic rather than contrived. The prose demonstrates considerable sophistication without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "my pulse is already acting like I'm being chased through the woods" and "my first thought is not a thought so much as a bright electrical event" capture the physical reality of anxiety with vivid precision. The writing maintains an age-appropriate voice while showcasing literary merit. The emotional complexity is well-executed. The piece captures the simultaneous hope and terror of teenage attraction, the exhaustion of constant self-surveillance, and the small triumph of a successful interaction. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Maya experiences a moment of genuine connection without any unrealistic transformation of her anxiety. The narrative structure flows naturally from the initial partner assignment through the gradual warming of their interaction. Each beat feels earned, from Maya's initial panic to her tentative joke-making to the final moment of cautious optimism. The pacing allows readers to experience the real-time nature of overthinking without becoming tedious. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal voice becomes slightly too articulate for an 8th grader, though this is largely offset by the authentic emotional content. The piece also occasionally borders on being too dense with internal observation, though this serves the thematic purpose of illustrating the overwhelming nature of social anxiety. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 88.8
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I start getting ready an hour too early because if I leave any margin at all my mind fills it. Shirt on, shirt off. The black one says trying too hard. The gray one says depressed on purpose. The white one says please approve of me. I hold each up to the mirror and hear Sam’s voice doing what it has not done, what it has never actually done, but what my body has decided is imminent. You look tired. You always make everything so intense. I didn’t know how to tell you this, but this has become a lot. You’re a lot. Then my own voice, quicker, sharper, already armed: Then why did you sleep with me? Why did you say you missed me? Why did you kiss me like I was something breakable and worth protecting? Don’t act like I invented this alone. Don’t make me the sole author of whatever this is just because you’ve gone quiet for three days. Through the wall the neighbor’s television leaks canned laughter, then a man’s voice too muffled to make out, then music swelling under some dramatic reveal. It sounds like a life happening at a safe distance, all conflict flattened by drywall. I wish my mind came with laugh tracks, some cue to tell me when I am making a scene out of nothing. He’s been distant lately. That is the fact I keep polishing until it blinds me. Distant can mean busy, tired, distracted, overwhelmed, having a life not arranged around my pulse. Distant can also mean regretting me, and my brain takes that path like water finding a crack. He took four hours to answer on Tuesday. He sent a heart, but only one, and not the red one he usually sends. Yesterday he said, Looking forward to seeing you, and I read it twelve times until “looking forward” became formal, almost managerial. The kind of thing you say before a performance review. I sit on the edge of the bed and rehearse. If he says, I think we moved too fast, I’ll say, That’s convenient. If he says, I need some space, I’ll say, You already took it. If he says, You’ve been kind of spiraling, I’ll say, Wow, thanks for using my fear against me. If he says, I care about you, but— I’ll say, Don’t. Don’t make yourself the nice one. The trick is to get angry before the hurt arrives. Lay down the resentment like salt around a doorway. If I can decide he’s selfish, cowardly, patronizing, then when he leaves I won’t have to feel left; I’ll have dismissed him first, in my heart if not out loud. This is self-protection, I tell myself. This is experience. This is pattern recognition. This is not, I tell myself less convincingly, me building a little courtroom in my skull and convicting him in absentia. The TV next door goes quiet for a second, then applause. My phone lights up. Parking might be a nightmare btw. Take your time, no rush ❤️ I stare at the heart. Red. The red one. Immediate relief rushes through me so hard it almost hurts, and then, because apparently no feeling is allowed to live here without interrogation, suspicion follows right after. No rush can mean I’m not eager. It can mean I’m preparing you for my own lateness. It can mean emotional softening before bad news. Why add “btw”? Casual. Overcasual. And the heart— compensation? Guilt heart? Courtesy heart? No. No, that’s insane. Maybe. But also people do that. They do. They soften the edges before they cut you. They say sweet things because they don’t want to feel like villains. I know this is the pattern. I actually know. Sometimes I can catch it with labels, like naming an animal at the edge of the woods. Splitting. Mind reading. Catastrophizing. Hypervigilance. I can line the terms up neatly, and for a few seconds it almost looks manageable, clinical, a thing one could place in a tray and examine. Then my chest tightens and the labels turn to confetti. Knowledge is thin. Feeling is law. I type, Sounds good! and delete it because the exclamation point sounds overeager. Then, Sounds good, see you soon ❤️ and delete that because matching the heart feels needy. Then just, okay, and that looks injured. I settle on, Thanks, heading out in a bit. Balanced. Unassailable. Cool in the way I have never naturally been. As soon as I send it I hate it. Too stiff. Now he’ll know I’m upset. Now he’ll ask if I’m okay in that careful voice, and I’ll have to decide whether to lie or become unbearable. I put the gray shirt back on. The television next door gives me a burst of dialogue—“I didn’t mean it like that”—clear enough to make me freeze. Of course. Even the wall is rehearsing with me. On the walk to his place I practice facial expressions. Open but not desperate. Warm but not too warm. If he hugs me loosely, I will know. If he keeps one hand on my shoulder instead of my waist, I will know. If he makes tea instead of kissing me right away, I will know. I am a forensic expert in tiny gestures, except my findings are inadmissible everywhere but inside my own head. When he opens the door, he smiles immediately, a real smile that reaches his eyes, and says, Hey, there you are, like he’s relieved. He leans in and kisses me, soft and lingering, hand at the back of my neck. His apartment smells like garlic and soap. Over his shoulder I see the table set for two, not casually, actually set. Candles not lit yet. A bottle of wine breathing on the counter. My heart gives one huge stupid hopeful thud and then starts scrambling to protect itself from the hope it just felt. Maybe this was planned before he started pulling away. Maybe he’s compensating. Maybe the carefulness is because he’s about to say something difficult and wants me regulated first. You made dinner, I say. Yeah, he says. I remembered you liked this pasta. I wasn’t sure if you’d eaten. The care in that lands directly in the center of me, where all the need lives. It is almost unbearable, how badly I want to collapse into it. To let it mean what it means. To be simple for one second. He remembered. He thought of me at the store, in the kitchen, with a pot boiling. He wanted me fed. This should be enough evidence to acquit him of all charges. But another part of me is already cross-examining. People can remember what you like and still leave. People can be tender while withdrawing. Sometimes tenderness is withdrawal in a nicer shirt. He touches my arm. You okay? You seem a little far away. There. Here it comes, I think, because of course I do. Concern turns instantly into scrutiny in my translation. He notices. I’m off. I’m too much already. We haven’t even sat down. I could say, I’ve been anxious because you seemed distant. That would be the honest thing. The adult thing. The thing my therapist would call vulnerable communication, and I hate how that phrase makes me feel skinned. Instead I hear myself say, I’m fine, just tired. He nods, not offended, just accepting it for now, and hands me a glass of water before wine because “you always forget to drink water.” Again that terrible sweetness, practical and observant. I want to cry from gratitude. I want to accuse him of gathering data on me for future abandonment. Both feel equally available. During dinner he tells me work has been awful this week; his boss dumped a project on him, his sister called in tears twice, he’s been sleeping badly. These are normal reasons for distance. Ordinary, boring, sufficient reasons. They arrive one by one and should dismantle the whole edifice I’ve spent all day constructing. Instead my mind starts renovating around them. If work was bad, why not say so more clearly? He did say he was slammed. Not enough. What amount would have felt like enough? I don’t know. Something impossible. Something that proves I matter continuously even when I’m not in the room. He reaches across the table and rubs his thumb over my knuckles while he talks. The gesture is unconscious. That’s what undoes me. If it were performative I could dismiss it. But unconscious affection is harder to argue with. I feel wanted and immediately frightened by how much I feel it. If he is this important, he can destroy me. Better to pull away first. Better to remember every delayed text, every tonal shift, every maybe. You’re very quiet tonight, he says later on the couch, and his voice is gentle, almost worried. Did I do something? No, I think, and also yes, and also not in reality, only in the parallel trial still underway in my head. I look at him. His face is open. No contempt, no impatience. Just concern and that awful willingness to understand me if I would let him. The television through the wall mutters a chase scene, dull percussion, someone shouting a name. My mind is still sprinting, but something in me stumbles. This is the part where I usually decide for both of us, I think. This is the part where I hear rejection in static and then punish him for the crime of not preventing my fear. I can see it, briefly, like lightning showing the whole landscape at once. I say, before I can make it strategic, I got in my head. You felt far away and I started telling myself stories. He squeezes my hand. I’m sorry I felt far away. I wasn’t pulling back. I was just drowning a little this week. The relief is immediate, liquid, holy. It floods every locked room. I want to believe him with the absolute faith of the starving. I want to climb inside the sentence and live there: I wasn’t pulling back. And even as I do, some stubborn splinter in me whispers, For now. People mean things when they say them and then stop meaning them. Don’t get comfortable. Don’t hand over your throat. So I sit there, held and unconvinced, loved and suspicious, ashamed of the suspicion, suspicious of the love, feeling both the wound and the bandage as if they are somehow the same thing. He kisses my temple. The neighbor’s television erupts in applause again, muffled and absurd. I lean into him anyway.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional psychological authenticity in portraying borderline personality disorder through internal monologue. The writer masterfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone caught between desperate need for connection and terror of abandonment. The narrative structure effectively contrasts the protagonist's catastrophic internal predictions with the gentle reality of their partner's behavior. The prose is sophisticated and precise, with particularly strong metaphorical language ("I am a forensic expert in tiny gestures, except my findings are inadmissible everywhere but inside my own head"). The writer avoids clinical terminology while still accurately depicting BPD symptoms like splitting, hypervigilance, and emotional dysregulation. The internal voice feels genuine rather than performative. The piece excels at showing cognitive dissonance - how validation is simultaneously craved and questioned. The protagonist's awareness of their patterns ("I know this is the pattern. I actually know") followed by their inability to stop the spiral feels psychologically accurate. The ending strikes an appropriate balance, offering momentary relief without suggesting the underlying patterns are resolved. The imagery is vivid and purposeful, from the shirt selection ritual to the neighbor's TV providing inadvertent commentary. The writing maintains consistent tension throughout, with the protagonist's hyperanalysis of every text and gesture creating genuine suspense despite the mundane setting. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose borders on being slightly overwrought, and a few instances where the internal monologue becomes slightly repetitive, though this may be intentional to reflect the obsessive nature of the thought patterns. The piece successfully fulfills the complex prompt requirements, delivering nearly 1000 words of primarily internal monologue that captures both the exhausting nature of BPD and the tragic irony of pushing away what one most desperately needs. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
