+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The contract came rolled in a waterproof syringe case, tucked inside a sack of millet husks and dead batteries. Nara found it at dawn, while the settlement was still coughing awake around the old agri-clinic. The clinic had once belonged to HelixDyne Nutritional, if the peeled logo on the ferrocrete wall could be believed. Now its hydroponic bays were dry, its gene printers stripped, and glow-in-the-dark mushrooms pushed up through the cracked lab tiles in pale green clusters, painting the floor with corpse-light. They were remnants of old splicing tests, the elders said. Nobody ate them unless they were desperate or curious or both. Nara was all three, most days. She cracked the syringe case open with her thumbnail. Inside was a strip of synth-paper and a curl of black thread, which meant the message had come through the Undertow market and not one of the river caravans. Black thread meant no names, no haggling, no second chances. The note was brief. **Tallow Fen settlement. Blight in all grain lines. Child cohort starving. Need viable seed, not one-cycle corporate rot. Payment: insulin cultures, copper mesh, one sealed nitrogen bottle. Deadline: six days. Courier waits two nights at Mile Marker 14.** At the bottom was a thumbprint in blue fungal dye. Nara read it twice, then folded it into the breast pocket of her patched lab coat. Insulin cultures alone were worth the risk. Copper mesh meant cleaner filters for the still. The nitrogen bottle—if the seal held—meant one more season of preserving cell lines without relying on winter. “Bad news?” asked Ivo from the doorway. He was carrying a crate of scavenged glassware and looked like he’d slept in the dust under the solar inverter again. His left eye had a permanent gold fleck in the iris from a corneal graft Nara had improvised last year. He claimed it sharpened his night vision. He also had migraines now whenever storm fronts rolled in. “Work,” Nara said. “Same thing.” She handed him the note. He whistled low. “Tallow Fen’s a three-day walk.” “Two, if Metch takes the skiff canal.” “You trust Metch with a black thread courier job?” “No,” Nara said. “That’s why I’m going.” Ivo grimaced. “Mara will say no.” “Mara says no to everything until she says yes.” He set the crate down on the least contaminated bench. The bench sagged on one side where they’d replaced a leg with stacked enzyme canisters. Above it, old tubing ran from a pressure cooker autoclave to a condenser coil made from refrigerator guts. Their thermal cycler was a miracle of desperation: a lunchbox-sized shell containing a hacked microcontroller, motorcycle battery, and three mismatched heating blocks insulated with wool and ceramic paste. It only held temperature if someone thumped the relay every ten minutes. Their centrifuge was a ceiling fan motor with 3D-printed tube holders and a lid bolted from a paint tin. HelixDyne had once sold rural franchises glossy machines that could rewrite seed lines in an afternoon. Then they designed the genes to fail after one generation. Sterility triggers. Delayed apoptosis cassettes. Dependency sequences activated by proprietary nutrient sprays no one could make after the supply chains snapped and the riots burned the ports. Profit logic outlived profit itself. The world was still starving from code inserted by people who had expected to retire before consequences matured. Nara moved to the cold cabinet—an old morgue drawer run off scavenged solar—and checked her inventory. Heritage barley embryo. Marsh tuber stem cells. Wild amaranth pollen. A fungal blight resistance sequence she’d excised from a volunteer reed in the southern flats. And the hard part: stabilizers. Everything decayed faster now. Not because DNA had changed, but because what survived in the ruins was already compromised—old stock designed to collapse, templates nicked by radiation, storage interrupted by years of blackout and heat. She spent half her life repairing rot introduced on purpose by people who had called it innovation. Behind her, Ivo said, “If you’re going to make a lasting line in six days, you’ll need live vectors. The reed cassette alone won’t hold.” “I know.” That meant medicine. Untested, always. Herself first, because there was no one else she’d ask. Nara pulled up her sleeve. Along her forearm ran a seam of faintly green capillaries, visible under the skin when she was tired. Two winters ago she’d used an adaptive viral scaffold to survive Marsh Fever after the clinic antibiotics ran out. It worked—rewrote receptor sites fast enough to blunt the infection—but it left side effects. Better oxygen uptake. Faster clotting. A constant ache in her long bones whenever the moon pulled the tides high. Once, in darkness, she had looked down and seen the veins in her wrist lit dimly as foxfire. Unexpected upsides, Ivo called them. She called them rent. From the medicine locker she took a vial labeled only with a red slash. The suspension inside shimmered faintly, full of engineered phages designed to enter plant nuclei and ignore the self-destruct markers in legacy corp genes. She’d tested them in petri dishes, then in two tomato vines. One vine had lived. The other had grown translucent leaves and burst in sunlight. Ivo watched her prepare the injector. “You don’t know what it’ll do in a mammal host.” “I know what not doing it will do.” “That’s not the same.” “No.” She pressed the needle into the green-lit seam of her arm and depressed the plunger. The burn hit immediately, racing to the shoulder and down into her ribs. She gripped the edge of the bench until the mushrooms on the floor blurred. Her teeth ached. Somewhere deep under the pain was the familiar sensation of systems renegotiating terms—immune markers, protein expression, fever chemistry. Her heart sprinted, then steadied. After a minute she let out a ragged breath. “Well?” Ivo said. Nara flexed her hand. “Metal tastes sweet.” “That’s new.” “Vision’s sharper.” “You say that every time.” “No.” She squinted across the room at a faded warning placard. “‘AUTHORIZED GENETIC PERSONNEL ONLY.’” She smiled despite herself. “That’s new.” By noon she had Mara’s permission, mostly because the insulin cultures turned a refusal into murder. By sunset she had the mobile kit packed into two waterproof trunks: pipettes rebuilt from veterinary syringes, agar plates poured in baby-food jars, hand-cast electrophoresis gel trays, copper wire electrodes, seed stock in cryotubes nested in insulation foam. The nitrogen bottle would come after delivery. First she had to earn it. Metch poled the skiff through irrigation canals furred with duckweed and chemical rainbow. They traveled under broken wind turbines and apartment towers wrapped in bean vines. At night they moored inside a collapsed pump station and listened to distant gunfire scatter over the marsh. On the second night, at Mile Marker 14, the courier appeared exactly as promised. Not one courier. Three. All armed. The woman in front wore a respirator mask lacquered with river mud and carried a compact flechette gun. “You Nara?” “Depends who’s paying.” The woman tossed a sealed glucose ampule onto the deck. Real factory glass. “Advance.” Nara didn’t pick it up. “Black thread said Tallow Fen.” “It is for Tallow Fen,” the woman said. “But we’re not from there.” Metch muttered a curse. The woman lowered her mask. Her teeth were capped with old dental ceramic, corporate white in the moonlight. “Fen’s under levy by Rhos-Kline Security. They’ve got the last working seed vault in the district and they’re enforcing patents on dead brands. Fen buys from them, they eat. Fen refuses, they starve. We’re helping them refuse.” “By hiring me to make counterfeit seed.” “By hiring you to make free seed,” she said. “Call it what you want.” That was the black market contract, then. Not just a rescue job. An act of economic sabotage against a company-state still collecting on genomes designed before the end. “If I do this,” Nara said, “Rhos-Kline will test the crop. They’ll look for stolen sequences.” “Then don’t steal,” said the woman. “Make something new.” Nara laughed once, short and tired. “In six days? With swamp rot templates and a lunchbox cycler?” The woman shrugged. “You’re the one people cross three floodplains to find.” Flattery was cheaper than bullets, but not by much. They reached Tallow Fen at dawn: a ring of stilt houses around black water, children with bark-thin wrists, adults grinding moldy grain that smelled like old paper. The blight had stripped every corporate cereal line to mush. In the communal shed, Nara set up her lab on fish crates and a door laid across barrels. The shed filled with the sounds of improvised science: the whine of the fan-motor centrifuge, the pop of relays in the thermal cycler, the hiss of alcohol burners under a cracked water bath. She isolated whatever still lived in the wild amaranth and marsh tuber, amplified the reed resistance cassette, then used her own altered blood serum as an emergency vector medium when the commercial buffers denatured in the heat. That part she did not mention aloud. By the second night her hands shook from fever. Side effect. Or feature. The phages in her system were still busy. Her pupils had widened until her irises looked like narrow rings of brown. But patterns came easier now. Sequence overlaps stood out bright and clean. She could almost feel the failures before they appeared, the old corporate kill-switches like burrs in a thread. On the fourth day she had a seed line. Not pure grain. Better. A marsh-hardy pseudocereal with tuber nodules and edible leaves, fast-cycling but not sterile, carrying resistance from the reed and carbohydrate density from the barley salvage. Ugly as sin. Viable. When the first germination tray broke green, the whole shed went silent. A child reached toward the sprouts as if they might vanish. “Will they breed true?” the Tallow Fen headwoman asked. Nara looked at the tray. At the blunt leaves. At life making no promises beyond itself. “They’ll try,” she said. “That’s more than the old world gave us.” Outside, somewhere beyond the marsh, corporate men with scanners and contracts still believed ownership could outlast civilization. Inside the shed, under jury-rigged lamps and the stink of algae fuel, something new unfolded cell by cell—messy, unstable, alive. Nara swayed where she stood. The headwoman caught her elbow. “You’re burning up.” Nara smiled with split lips. In the dim, the veins under her skin gave off a soft green light, enough to read by. “Unexpected upside,” she murmured, and fainted beside the seedlings.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk story demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse. The author effectively incorporates all requested elements: a biohacker protagonist working with salvaged equipment, jury-rigged lab descriptions, physical tolls and benefits of genetic modifications, and a black market contract as the central plot driver. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, with vivid details like the "glow-in-the-dark mushrooms" in abandoned labs and the improvised equipment descriptions (thermal cycler made from a lunchbox, centrifuge from a ceiling fan motor). The author creates a believable post-apocalyptic scientific landscape where desperate innovation meets corporate legacy systems designed to fail. Character development shows nuance, particularly in Nara's willingness to experiment on herself and her complex relationship with the modifications she's undergone. The supporting characters like Ivo feel authentic in their interactions and concerns. The dialogue is natural and serves both character development and exposition effectively. The prose maintains a consistent, gritty tone throughout that matches the biopunk genre. Descriptive passages are evocative without becoming overwrought - phrases like "corpse-light" and "chemical rainbow" create atmosphere efficiently. The technical details feel researched and authentic while remaining accessible. The story structure follows a clear arc from contract acceptance through execution to completion, with appropriate pacing and tension. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - cautiously hopeful but not unrealistically positive given the harsh world established. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and a few moments where the technical descriptions slightly slow narrative momentum. The transformation elements feel earned through the established worldbuilding and character motivations. + Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 84.8
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Arthur kept adjusting his collar. It was an absurdly small thing to be conscious of, under the circumstances, yet it had become the fixed point upon which his uneasiness revolved. The tunic was dark, close-cut, and of some fabric that retained a crease with military obstinacy. It sat badly on him. Arthur Neegan had been born beneath a Martian dome, had grown up among ore hoppers and pressure seals, and had spent most of his adult life with fine red dust in the seams of his knuckles. Formal clothes transformed him neither into a gentleman nor a diplomat. They only made him feel as though he had been wrapped for shipment. The transfer vessel hummed around him with a subdued and expensive power. There was no clatter of freight, no smell of hot lubricant, no shouted profanity over intercom. The air was cool, dry, and almost offensively clean. A thin strip of luminescence ran in an oval around the cabin wall, and beneath it the metal had been finished in muted gray enamel, unmarked by use. Through the port at his right shoulder Mars receded—rust-colored, immense, and deceptively tranquil beneath its thin halo of atmosphere. He stared at it and thought: I had a claim, a house, a patch of regolith and a survey map. A man ought to be able to stand on what he has worked. Instead, six armed men with polished boots and polite voices had stepped through his airlock at dawn. They had worn the insignia plainly enough: the many-rayed star of the Antares Diplomatic Corps, stitched in silver thread over black. On Mars, everyone knew the emblem. “Diplomatic” had once meant envoys, treaties, protocols. In the last twenty years, with the rise of the interstellar combines and the gradual enfeeblement of the old planetary authorities, it had come to mean something rather different: accredited coercion at respectable volume. The tallest of them had removed one glove and said, with grave courtesy, “Engineer Arthur Neegan, you are requested at an off-world conference. We are instructed to ensure your safe attendance.” Requested. Ensured. Such were the velvet words beneath which steel traveled. Arthur adjusted his collar again. “You’ll wear through it.” The voice came from the opposite seat. Arthur looked up. The man sitting there was one of the retinue, though he had removed his sidearm and laid it holstered beside him on the bench, as if to imply relaxation. He was broad-faced, middle-aged, and carried himself with the loose steadiness of a veteran spacer. His insignia showed he was no common escort officer. Arthur said, “If I ruin it, maybe they’ll let me put my work jacket back on.” “They won’t.” “That certain?” “Yes.” Arthur looked at him for a moment. “You know where we’re going?” “I know enough to tell you that if you did know, it wouldn’t improve your temper.” “That presumes I have one worth improving.” The officer smiled faintly. “Everyone from the colonies has a temper. It’s one of the few luxuries you can afford.” Arthur could not decide whether that was wit or condescension. “You know my background, then.” “I know everyone does, now.” There it was. Not said heavily, not emphasized, but present. Everyone. Arthur said, “Because of the vein.” “Because of what may lie under your land,” said the officer. “And because of what you may already know.” “I know rock densities, drill tolerances, and that my lease assessor was either blind or bribed.” “Which?” Arthur shrugged. “On Mars? Why choose?” The officer gave a short breath that might have been amusement. “You are not a fool, Mr. Neegan. Try not to become one by pretending ignorance is armor.” Arthur leaned back. The restraint webbing crossed his chest in neat bands. “Then tell me something useful. Why the Antares Corps? My plot’s under Colonial registry. If some company wants to squeeze me, they usually send lawyers before gunmen.” “Lawyers are slower.” “And diplomats are faster?” “When they are told to be.” Arthur glanced once more through the port. Mars had shrunk further, now a coin of ochre fire against blackness. Beyond it, stars burned with the hard steadiness one never saw from the ground. He had known all his life, in the abstract, that the affairs of worlds were determined out here, in the vacuum lanes between planets and in the sealed rooms of vessels too costly for ordinary men to board. Yet abstract knowledge was one thing; feeling oneself physically removed from one’s own horizon was another. He said quietly, “There’s too much noise over one discovery.” The officer’s eyes narrowed, not suspiciously but appraisingly. “Yes.” “So it’s not merely the size of the deposit.” “No.” Arthur turned to him fully. “Then what?” The man hesitated, and for the first time his confidence seemed practiced rather than natural. “That is for someone else to discuss.” “Convenient.” The cabin light shifted almost imperceptibly as the vessel entered docking alignment. Somewhere forward, relays clicked. The officer straightened. “We’ve arrived,” he said. --- The station was not one Arthur recognized. He had expected one of the familiar Martian transfer rings: functional, crowded, its corridors lined with cargo manifests and labor contracts. Instead he was led into a spindle-shaped orbital with carpets on the deck, subdued wall sconces, and murals beneath transparent sealant depicting heroic ships arcing among constellations. It had the aesthetic confidence of old money and the strategic anonymity of classified ownership. Their footsteps made almost no sound. At each hatch, silent personnel stepped aside. None wore insignia. None met his eyes. Arthur was escorted into a chamber that managed to be both spacious and airless in feeling. The floor was dark wood veneer laid over alloy, the table elliptical and glossy as still water. One wall was entirely viewport, and through it the stars looked near enough to touch. Opposite stood a sideboard bearing a silver coffee service that was so archaic it bordered on theatrical. A woman stood by the viewport, hands clasped behind her back. She turned as Arthur entered. She was elderly, though not frail, with iron-gray hair swept back in severe lines and a face composed of planes that time had sharpened rather than softened. Her clothing was simple: a high-collared black gown with no ornament except a small pin at the breast—a broken circle crossed by three vertical lines. It was not a symbol Arthur knew. The escort officer said, “Madam, Mr. Neegan.” “Thank you, Commander Vale,” she said. Her voice was low and exact. “Leave us.” Vale inclined his head. “Madam.” He withdrew with the others. The hatch sealed. Arthur listened to the locking dogs engage and felt, absurdly, that the room had become smaller. The woman regarded him. “Mr. Neegan, please sit.” Arthur remained standing a heartbeat longer than was wise, then took the chair opposite her. “I’d ask your name.” “You may call me Madam Seldene.” “May?” “It is the name by which I am most often addressed in situations requiring discretion.” “That’s not an answer.” “No,” she said. “But it is true.” Arthur looked at the coffee service. “Should I assume this is where I’m reassured?” “You may assume,” said Seldene, seating herself, “that reassurance is among the cheaper commodities in the inhabited system. I prefer to trade in facts.” “Then start with one.” She folded her hands. “Your land sits above one of the richest Azra concentrations yet detected on Mars.” “That much I know.” “Do you know why the initial survey missed it?” “Bad equipment.” “No.” “Corruption.” “Partly. More importantly, the field signature was masked.” Arthur frowned. “Masked by what?” Seldene tilted her head. “That is the first interesting question you have asked.” Arthur felt irritation stir. “I’ve had men with carbines in my kitchen for six hours. You’ll forgive me if my priorities have been domestic.” A flicker of approval crossed her face. “Very well. The answer is: by another source of radiation acceleration in the substrata. Smaller than the vein itself, but unlike any Azra matrix yet catalogued.” Arthur stared at her. “You’re saying there’s something else down there.” “Yes.” “What?” “We do not know.” Arthur let that settle. “And because you don’t know, you drag me off-world?” “Because others suspect enough to become dangerous.” “Others meaning corporations?” “Among others. Governments, if one still uses that old-fashioned word. Treaty blocs. Military procurement offices. Scientific bureaus that have forgotten the meaning of science. The discovery of Azra altered the balance of interstellar travel in a decade. Ships that once required cumbersome reactor shielding can now drive harder and farther. Warheads may be made compact enough to fit where no doctrine anticipated them. Remote colonies become central overnight. Marginal planets become arsenals.” “Mars,” said Arthur. “Mars,” Seldene agreed. “A poor world of dust and leases, transformed by chemistry into the hinge of policy.” Arthur looked again at the stars beyond the glass. “And I’m the hinge-pin.” “For the moment.” He gave a dry laugh. “I should be flattered.” “You should be cautious.” Silence stretched. The station’s systems breathed softly through hidden vents. Arthur said, “What do you want from me?” Seldene leaned back. “An alliance.” “Those usually begin with consent.” “They often begin,” she said, “with transportation.” “What kind of alliance?” “You are an engineer. More important, you are local. You understand Martian geology, Martian labor systems, Martian people. You are not yet purchased by any combine. Your claim gives you legal standing, however temporary that may prove.” “Temporary?” “Yes. If you return unprotected, your lease will be challenged, your records audited, your workers bribed away, and your life shortened.” Arthur met her eyes. “That’s a threat.” “No, Mr. Neegan. It is the inventory of threats already in motion.” He was silent. Seldene continued, “We want you to retain your claim publicly and cooperate privately with our investigation of the anomaly beneath it.” “Who is ‘we’?” “A consortium.” “That sounds like a corporation in evening clothes.” “It is not. Not exactly.” “Then exactly what is it?” Seldene’s expression cooled. “A group of parties who have concluded that if Azra’s deeper properties are monopolized by any single power, the result will not be a stable peace.” Arthur said, “That implies there are deeper properties.” “It implies,” said Seldene, “that some of us fear there are.” “And if I refuse?” She did not answer immediately. Instead she rose and moved to the viewport. Starlight edged her profile in silver. “At this distance,” she said, “Mars appears serene. A red lamp in black velvet. Yet on its surface men bargain over air, water, and years of human life. Scale does not improve morality; it merely enlarges its consequences.” Arthur said nothing. She turned. “If you refuse, I will have you returned with every courtesy. You will be watched by our rivals and by us. Your choice may remain your own for several days, perhaps weeks. After that, events will overtake it.” “That’s not much of a refusal.” “No,” Seldene said. “History seldom offers those.” She touched the small pin at her breast, almost absently. “Help us, Mr. Neegan, and you may preserve more than your claim. Refuse, and you may still become the most important dead engineer in the System.” Arthur adjusted his collar once more. It felt tighter than before. “What,” he asked, “precisely are you offering me?” Seldene smiled then, very slightly, and for the first time he understood that everything before had been preface. “A future,” she said. “And the truth about what was buried under Mars long before your corporations learned to mine it.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Asimov's style and the classic science fiction genre. The author successfully captures the retrofuturistic atmosphere with details like "luminescence strips," formal diplomatic protocols, and the political machinations surrounding a valuable resource discovery. The worldbuilding feels authentic to the golden age of science fiction, with corporations, diplomatic corps, and interplanetary politics driving the narrative. The characterization of Arthur Neegan is particularly well-executed. His discomfort with formal clothing serves as an effective metaphor for his displacement from his familiar world, and his dialogue consistently reflects his working-class Martian background. The author avoids making him either too naive or implausibly sophisticated, striking a believable balance. Seldene is appropriately mysterious and commanding, though perhaps slightly archetypal as the shadowy power broker. The dialogue carries the story effectively, revealing information naturally through conversation while maintaining distinct voices for each character. Commander Vale's brief interactions add texture without overwhelming the central dynamic between Arthur and Seldene. The pacing builds tension appropriately, moving from Arthur's initial confusion to the revelation of deeper mysteries beneath Mars. The prose style successfully emulates Asimov's clean, precise approach while adding more descriptive flourishes than the master typically employed. Phrases like "rust-colored, immense, and deceptively tranquil" and "accredited coercion at respectable volume" demonstrate skillful writing that enhances rather than overwhelms the narrative. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within the genre conventions. The mysterious consortium, the hints of ancient secrets beneath Mars, and the "bigger than we thought" revelation follow familiar patterns. While executed competently, these don't break particularly new ground. The ending, while appropriately ominous, relies somewhat heavily on the "ancient mystery" trope. The technical execution is strong throughout, with smooth transitions between scenes and natural information delivery. The author successfully balances exposition with action and character development, never allowing any single element to dominate inappropriately. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom always smells like wet paper and coins, like a fountain at the bottom of a train station. Acid, dust, old water. Under the red safelight, everybody looks half-born. Edges soften. Faces become theories. Elliot is standing across from me with a pair of tongs in one hand and a strip of test prints in the other, his black nail polish chipped at the tips. He’s wearing that giant thrifted wool coat he refuses to take off even in overheated rooms, sleeves swallowing his wrists, silver chain at his throat, eyeliner smudged in a way that would look deliberate on anyone else. On him it looks like he forgot to care and somehow that became a style. I’ve been watching him all semester. Not in a creepy way, I tell myself. In the way you watch a photograph develop in the tray—waiting for contrast, for the image to confess what it is. Elliot never fit neatly into anything my eyes knew how to categorize. He had the bones and slightness of a beautiful boy in a medieval painting, but he moved through critiques with this careful, almost apologetic softness that made the loudest people lean in when he finally spoke. And when he did speak, it was always something smart and strange enough to rearrange the room. Normally, I know what I want from people. Especially the people I want. I’m not shy. I flirt like I’m starting an argument. I like confidence, bluntness, the spark of recognition when someone clocks me clocking them and steps forward instead of back. My friends call me bossy like it’s a personality defect; my exes usually called it a relief until they didn’t. With Elliot, everything in me that usually reaches just… paused. Maybe because he was nineteen, which felt both not-younger-enough-to-matter and absolutely younger-enough-to-matter. Maybe because he carried himself like someone whose skin had only recently become negotiable. Maybe because every time I thought I understood what drew me to him, he shifted slightly and the answer changed shape. I slide another sheet of paper into the enlarger easel and glance up. “You’re hovering.” His mouth twitches. “I’m assisting.” “You’re haunting.” “That too.” I grin before I can stop myself. “Set the timer for twelve seconds.” He does, and we work in practiced silence: expose, tray, rock the developer, wait for the image to rise. We’ve done this four nights in a row for our final, a collaborative series on constructed selves. Typical art-school assignment—vague enough to become accidentally autobiographical. The first night he barely looked at me. The second, he started making dry little comments I had to catch like thrown pins. The third, he told me he’d never worked in a darkroom this long before and said it with a kind of reverence, like the room itself had earned his trust. Tonight the silence feels padded, not empty. My phone buzzes once in my back pocket, a blunt little insistence against my hip. Probably Maya asking if I’m still alive, or my landlord, or the guy from the print lab who keeps texting me as if “u up” can become romance through repetition. I ignore it. Elliot is looking at the print in the tray like it’s saying something. “You can tell me if you hate this one,” I say. He shakes his head. “I don’t hate it.” Then, after a second: “I just look… different than I think I do.” I lean on the counter beside him. In the tray, his self-portrait blooms darker: blurred jawline, lace collar under a leather jacket, one hand lifted to his own throat. It’s a gorgeous image. It unsettles me a little, which is probably part of why it works. “Different how?” He keeps his eyes on the paper. “I don’t know.” I wait. That’s new for me too—waiting without pouncing on a gap in conversation. With most people, silence is an opening I know how to take. With him, I’ve been learning it can also be shelter. Finally he says, very quietly, “Do you ever feel like people can see you wrong so consistently that eventually you start performing their version just to make things easier?” There it is: not exactly out of nowhere, but still enough to make something in me straighten. “Yeah,” I say, softer than I expected. “I think everybody queer does, at least a little.” He glances at me then, red light caught in his eyes. “I’m not even sure what I am.” It would be easy—so easy—to step in here with confidence like a blade. To tell him uncertainty is normal, labels can wait, gender is fluid, presentation means nothing and everything, welcome to the club. I know the language. I know how to lead. But his face has that fragile, braced look people get when they’re trying to hand you something breakable without making it your responsibility. So I say, “Okay.” He lets out a breath that sounds almost like a laugh. “That’s it?” “That’s not a bad place to start.” He puts the tongs down. “I think…” His shoulders rise under the coat, then fall. “Sometimes I like being read as a boy. Or I’m used to it. Or maybe I just know how to do it. But then sometimes it feels like I’m wearing somebody else’s heavy winter clothes and pretending they fit. And I don’t know if that means anything, or if I’m making it up because I’m in art school now and everyone is—” “Stop,” I say, gently. He stops. “You’re allowed to feel weird about yourself without needing a thesis statement.” That gets a real laugh, brief and nervous. His fingers tap the edge of the tray. “I don’t know what I want people to call me. I don’t know if I want to change anything. I just…” He swallows. “I think I want room.” Room. The word lands in me harder than I expect. Under the red light, Elliot’s softness doesn’t read as uncertainty so much as courage without armor. He isn’t asking me to define him. He’s asking whether I can stand not knowing. Whether I can be near him without making his unfinishedness into a problem to solve. The answer should be simple. But I am having the deeply annoying experience of discovering that desire can scramble your self-concept just as efficiently as gender can. Because the truth is, part of what drew me to him was that he looked like a boy in a way I could hold onto—a delicate, cinematic kind of masculinity, yes, but still a recognizable shape. A point of orientation. And now that shape is speaking, shifting, blurring at the edges, and instead of making me pull back, it’s making me lean in so hard I almost resent it. What does that say about me? About the stories I’ve told myself about who I want, who I am when I want them? I’ve always liked defined silhouettes: butch girls, pretty boys, femmes who weaponize clarity. People who arrive like a statement. Elliot feels more like a question, and somehow that’s become the most compelling thing in the room. “I can do room,” I say. He looks at me carefully, as if checking for pity. I make sure he doesn’t find any. “I mean it,” I add. “You don’t owe me a conclusion.” The air between us changes. Not dramatically. Not a movie lightning-strike. More like a lens clicking into a finer focus. “Can I ask you something?” he says. “Obviously.” His mouth curls. “See, that’s what I mean.” “What?” “You always sound like you’re already in charge of whatever is happening.” “I usually am.” “I know.” There’s no bite in it. If anything, he sounds curious. I feel heat rise in my face, absurd in a room this dim. “And is that bothering you?” I ask. He thinks about it. “No. I just…” He fidgets with the cuff of his sleeve. “I like talking to you because you make things feel less scary. But I don’t want to just become whatever people around me suggest. I’ve done enough of that.” The honesty of it hits cleanly. I nod. “That makes sense.” It costs me something, admitting that and meaning it. Not because I want to control him exactly, but because my instinct with anyone tentative is to create structure, momentum, safety through direction. I want to tell him what shirt suits him, what pronouns to try privately, how gorgeous he already is, how I’ve wanted to touch the silver at his throat for months. I want to move us somewhere definite. Instead I ask, “What feels good right now?” He goes very still. I realize, half a second too late, how that sounds in a room like this, with chemistry steaming around us and my body angled toward his. The question hangs there, suddenly double-exposed. Elliot’s cheeks darken even under the red light. “You mean with— with all this.” “I did mean gender,” I say, and then, because lying would be pathetic: “But I guess I’m also not taking the other interpretation off the table.” He stares at me. I can hear the soft drip from the rinse sink. My phone buzzes again, muted against my pocket, and I leave it there to die. Then Elliot smiles, small and stunned and a little wicked, and I feel my whole body answer. “That,” he says, “is maybe the most stressful possible way you could have phrased it.” I laugh, helpless. “Yeah, well. Uncharted territory.” “For both of us?” There’s the opening. Usually I would take it with both hands. This time I nod first. “For both of us.” He steps closer, not enough to touch, enough to choose. His coat brushes my wrist. “What feels good right now,” he says slowly, trying the words, “is that you asked.” And because he gave me something clear, because he made the first move in the only way that matters tonight, I let myself answer with my full attention, nothing pushy in it, nothing claimed too soon. “Okay,” I say. We stand over the tray together and watch his image come further into being.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated handling of a complex prompt involving gender identity, attraction, and power dynamics. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive 25-year-old while showing her vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, with the red light creating an intimate, confessional atmosphere that serves the story's emotional needs. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge approach and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Her self-awareness about her typical patterns ("I flirt like I'm starting an argument") contrasts effectively with her uncertainty in this new situation. Elliot emerges as more than just an object of fascination - his vulnerability feels genuine rather than performative, and his tentative exploration of gender identity is handled with sensitivity and realism. The prose quality is consistently high, with memorable imagery ("Under the red safelight, everybody looks half-born") and precise metaphors that enhance rather than overwhelm the narrative. The writing avoids both clinical detachment and overwrought sentimentality, finding an appropriate register for the subject matter. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple functions - advancing plot, revealing character, and building tension. The conversations capture the "tender awkwardness" requested in the prompt without becoming stilted or unrealistic. The pacing allows for meaningful silences and subtext. The story's emotional complexity is perhaps its greatest strength. It explores attraction that defies easy categorization, the discomfort of having one's assumptions challenged, and the courage required for authentic self-expression. The ending avoids false resolution while providing genuine emotional satisfaction - both characters have moved forward without the narrative forcing premature conclusions about identity or relationship outcomes. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious in its literary aspirations, and a few instances where internal monologue could be more concise. However, these are relatively minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
At eleven in the morning, Rome was already shimmering. The stones around the Trevi Fountain threw back heat and light; tourists clustered in bright knots, arms lifted for photographs, guidebooks flapping like startled birds. In the middle of it all, Ben Fletcher stood too still, tall and rumpled in a linen shirt that had surrendered to the day, one hand on a map, the other gripping a small blue guidebook titled *Rome in Seven Walks*. He was reading while walking backward. This was his first mistake. The second was stepping directly into the path of a black scooter that came slicing through the chaos with a growl of engine and a flash of bronze forearm. “Ma sei matto?” the rider shouted, wrenching the handlebars hard enough that the scooter skidded sideways and stopped an inch from his thigh. Ben froze. The rider kicked down the stand in one sharp movement and pulled off her helmet. Dark hair spilled out, flattened at the crown and curling at the ends, and then she was glaring at him with black, furious eyes and a mouth made for trouble. “You want to die under a Piaggio in front of a fountain? Very dramatic. Very Roman.” “I am so, so sorry,” Ben said at once. “Entirely my fault. I was—” “Reading.” She snatched the guidebook from his hand, glanced at the cover, and made a face. “Ah, yes. Englishman. Lost in culture. Blind in traffic.” “I can usually do one thing at a time very well,” he said. “Apparently today I chose the wrong thing.” Her mouth twitched. That was the first opening. He saw it and, emboldened, attempted, “Mi dispiace. Io sono un idiota.” Now she outright laughed. “That was almost charming.” “Almost?” “Your accent is criminal.” He let out a breath he hadn’t realized he was holding. “That seems fair.” She flipped through his guidebook with contempt. “This says the best time to see Trevi is early morning before the crowds.” “Yes, I noticed it may have misled me.” “This book is rubbish.” “It was recommended by a colleague.” “Then your colleague hates you.” She handed it back, then glanced at the insulated delivery case strapped behind her seat. “You are not from here.” “No.” “Obviously. You stand like you’re apologizing to the pavement.” “British,” Ben said. She gave a solemn nod. “Tragic.” He laughed despite himself. “And you are?” “Alessandra.” She swung one leg over the scooter but didn’t put her helmet back on. “Born three streets away. Which gives me the right to nearly run over idiots near my fountain.” “Your fountain?” She tipped her chin. “You have your queen. I have Trevi.” He should have thanked her and moved on. He was in Rome for six weeks of archival research, not spontaneous near-death experiences. But Alessandra was looking at him with bright, assessing amusement, as if she had already decided he was either a waste of time or the most entertaining thing she’d seen all morning. “So, Professor Apology,” she said. “What are you doing today besides being in the way?” He blinked. “How did you know I’m a professor?” “The shoes,” she said. “And that face. You look like you know where medieval manuscripts are kept.” “I’m not sure whether to be impressed or offended.” “Both.” She checked her watch, muttered something under her breath, then looked back at him. “I have three deliveries and forty minutes before the city becomes impossible. Get on.” He stared at the scooter. “I beg your pardon?” “I will show you Rome.” “I don’t think that’s remotely necessary.” “Of course not. That’s why it’s interesting.” “I don’t know you.” “Good. If you knew me, you might say no faster.” Before he could answer, she held out a spare helmet from the hook under the seat. Ben looked at the helmet, then at Alessandra, then at the impossible swirl of sun and water and shouting tourists around them. Something old and careful in him hesitated. Something smaller and much more dangerous said yes. Twenty minutes later he was clinging to the back of the scooter as they shot down a side street too narrow for comfort, his knees tucked in, his pulse somewhere up around his ears. “Are you trying to kill me?” he shouted. “No,” Alessandra shouted back. “If I wanted that, I’d take Via del Corso.” She laughed into the traffic. He could feel it through her shoulders. She delivered a parcel to a tailor near Piazza Navona, another to a florist who kissed both her cheeks and called her *diavola*, and a third to a lawyer’s office where she somehow emerged with an envelope, a clementine, and a silver pen she had not entered with. Ben noticed the pen only because she tucked it behind her ear and caught him looking. “What?” “Nothing,” he said. “Liar.” He considered. “Did you steal that?” She looked scandalized. “Ben Fletcher. In daylight? From a lawyer? I liberated it from a desk where no one loved it.” “That is stealing.” “That is editing ownership.” He should have been appalled. Instead, absurdly, he was trying not to smile. By one o’clock she had abandoned all pretense of working and deposited him on the Spanish Steps with a paper cone of fried zucchini flowers. She stole one from his hand while he was still trying his first bite. “You really do just take things,” he said. “Only interesting things.” “And people?” She tilted her head. “Sometimes.” They walked with no clear direction. Alessandra never seemed to consult a map. She moved through Rome as if the streets rearranged themselves for her amusement—ducking into courtyards, cutting through churches for shade, pausing to argue with a barista she clearly knew and emerging with two espressos, one of which she pressed into Ben’s hand. He learned things in fragments. She was twenty-nine. She lived in Trastevere above her aunt’s old seamstress shop. She had been a courier for three years because she liked speed and hated bosses and loved knowing shortcuts no one else knew. He told her he taught art history in London, specialized in religious iconography, and had come to Rome to work on a chapter he had been avoiding for months. “Why avoiding?” she asked. He looked down into his coffee. “Because if I finish it, I have to decide what happens next.” “Ah,” she said. “You are one of those men.” “What men?” “The ones who build whole lives out of postponing decisions.” There was no sting in it, only unnerving accuracy. “And you?” he said. “I make decisions too fast. It saves time.” By midafternoon she had coaxed, mocked, and finally dragged him onto the back of the scooter again, this time across the river into Trastevere, where laundry hung between buildings and restaurant tables crowded the lanes. She parked illegally with the serene confidence of someone who considered rules advisory. At a tiny market she bought peaches and slipped a packet of candied violets into her pocket while distracting the owner with a debate about football. Ben saw the movement—quick, elegant, practiced. When they stepped outside, he stared at her. She held up the violets. “For later.” “You truly are impossible.” “And yet,” she said, offering him one, “you are still here.” The candy dissolved floral and strange on his tongue. So was she. They ended up on the Janiculum Hill as the afternoon bent toward evening. From there Rome looked less like a city than an idea someone had been elaborating for two thousand years: domes, terraces, pines, heat wavering over stone. Ben stood at the balustrade, wind pushing his hair back, and said quietly, “I haven’t done anything remotely like this in years.” Alessandra sat on the low wall, watching him instead of the view. “That bad?” “No. Just very... scheduled.” She grinned. “You need corruption.” “I think that’s already underway.” For a moment neither of them spoke. A cannon fired in the distance, startling him. Alessandra laughed so hard she nearly slid off the wall. Then the sky changed. Clouds, sudden and heavy, rolled over the city with theatrical speed. The first drops were enormous. Alessandra looked up. “Run.” They were halfway down the hill when the rain truly came—a violent Roman downpour, drumming against cobblestones, bouncing off helmets and shutters. They sprinted the last stretch and dived under the striped awning of a gelato shop, breathless and wet through, while the street blurred silver beyond them. Ben pushed dripping hair out of his eyes. Alessandra’s mascara had smudged faintly at one corner, making her look even less trustworthy and somehow more beautiful. Inside, the gelataio looked at them with pity and handed over napkins. “You planned this,” Ben said. “Of course. I control weather for dramatic effect.” They stood shoulder to shoulder beneath the awning as rain hammered around them. Alessandra licked pistachio gelato from a spoon she had acquired before he’d even seen her order it. “Did you pay for that?” he asked. She widened her eyes innocently, then reached up and touched a spot of rain on his cheek with her thumb. The gesture was so casual it took his breath. “You ask too many ethical questions,” she murmured. “And you avoid all of them.” “Not all.” The space between them changed. Ben was acutely aware of the nearness of her body, the scent of rain on hot pavement, the sweet cold smell of pistachio and lemon, the way her gaze held his just a beat too long to be accidental. He had the absurd sensation of standing on an edge he had not seen himself approach. “Alessandra,” he said. “What?” “I have no idea what’s happening.” She smiled, smaller now, less mocking. “Good.” Then she leaned in and kissed him. It was not gentle, exactly, but it was precise—brief, warm, tasting faintly of pistachio, rainwater, and dare. By the time he answered it, she was already drawing back, eyes bright with something he couldn’t read. The rain eased by degrees. Rome began to reassemble itself beyond the awning: scooters, umbrellas, shouting, life resuming. Alessandra checked the time and made a face. “I should go.” Ben laughed helplessly. “You can’t do this to me and simply disappear into traffic.” “Watch me.” But she didn’t move immediately. Instead she reached into her pocket and pressed something into his palm: the silver pen from the lawyer’s office. “A souvenir,” she said. “I can’t accept stolen property.” “You absolutely can. Consider it your first bad decision.” He looked at the pen, then at her. “Will I see you again?” She put on her helmet, visor still raised. “Tomorrow, maybe. Or never. Rome is full of miracles and administrative failure.” “That’s not an answer.” “It’s the only one I have.” She started the scooter, engine rumbling alive beneath her. Then, just before lowering the visor, she said, “Trevi. Eleven. Try not to walk backward.” And she was gone—out into the damp, glowing street, swallowed by Rome. Ben stood under the gelato awning with rainwater drying on his sleeves, a stolen pen in his hand, and the distinct, disorienting sense that his life had just tilted on its axis.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge naturally through action and dialogue rather than exposition. Alessandra's mischievous energy and Ben's scholarly restraint are well-contrasted, and their chemistry develops believably over the course of a single day. The prose style is polished and engaging, with particularly strong descriptive passages that capture Rome's atmosphere without becoming overly ornate. Lines like "Rome was already shimmering" and "tourists clustered in bright knots, arms lifted for photographs, guidebooks flapping like startled birds" create vivid imagery without excess. The dialogue feels authentic and reveals character effectively - Alessandra's sharp wit ("Your accent is criminal") and Ben's self-deprecating politeness come through clearly. The story adheres well to the prompt's requirements, incorporating the near-collision at Trevi Fountain, Alessandra's kleptomania (subtly woven throughout), and the cultural clash between the characters. The pacing moves naturally from their initial meeting through various Roman locations, building tension toward the kiss scene and ambiguous ending. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The transformation of Ben from cautious academic to willing participant in Alessandra's adventures, while not entirely unearned, happens quite rapidly. Some moments border on being slightly overwrought, particularly in the final scenes where the romantic tension peaks. The ending, while appropriately ambiguous, leans toward conventional romantic optimism that feels somewhat predictable for the genre. The character of Alessandra occasionally risks becoming a manic pixie dream girl archetype, though the author largely avoids this trap by giving her agency and complexity. The theft subplot is handled with appropriate moral ambiguity rather than being romanticized completely. Overall, this is accomplished writing that successfully balances character development, atmospheric description, and romantic tension within the constraints of a short piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The evening shift at The Last Outpost always begins with a lie. “Quiet night,” I say, polishing a glass that is already clean. Three heads lift at once to glare at me from different corners of the bar. Old Parn, who has spent twenty years hauling ice and resents every one of them. Seli, a courier with four silver rings through one ear and a talent for finding offense in atmospheric conditions. And Junie from Dock Control, who isn’t technically off duty so much as hiding from it. “Don’t you put that on us,” Junie says. Seli points a warning finger. “Take it back, Nets.” Parn snorts into his algae stout. “Girl’s trying to kill us all.” I grin and set the glass upside down on the rack. “Fine. Wildly eventful night. Danger at every turn. Better?” The station hums around us, old metal and older grudges. The Last Outpost sits bolted to the slow-spin hub of Rook’s Lantern Station, out where the Andromeda rim thins into dark enough to make you religious. Miners come in dust-coated and hollow-eyed, merchants come in smiling like they invented profit, and everybody comes in wanting something stronger than they can afford. The gravity hiccups. It’s subtle, just a fractional slip in the station’s spin compensation, but every regular feels it. The bottles behind me clink. A line of amber in Parn’s glass domes upward, trembling, then settles. “Again?” Junie mutters, looking toward the ceiling as if Dock Control lives directly above us and she can shame it into competence. “Tell your maintenance crush to stop dating the reactor,” I say. “He’s not my crush.” “Then why do you know his coolant cycle?” Seli groans. “Please don’t start. I’m trying to have a drink, not witness courtship rituals.” “Courtship?” Junie says. “I threatened to file a report.” Parn raises his glass. “That’s how my second wife got me.” I like this part of the night best, when everyone is still enough themselves to pretend they are. There’s a rhythm to it—pour, wipe, listen, interrupt before feelings curdle into fights. I’m good at rhythm. Better at that than honesty, anyway. The door iris shivers open, admitting a wash of corridor light and one frazzled stranger. First thing I notice is the hair—or what passes for it. Thick tentacled dreads, dark and glossy, tied back badly and still escaping in every direction like they’ve been in a personal argument with vacuum seals. Second thing is posture: too still in the shoulders, too fast in the eyes. The kind of stillness people learn when they expect trouble to be looking for them. They take the middle stool, not too close to anyone, not too far from the exit. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say softly. Their voice has a rasp in it, wear and travel and maybe a little fear. “Better make that a double.” I reach for the good bottle automatically, then pause. “Bad night?” Their mouth twitches. “Optimistic question.” I pour. The gravity wobbles again as I set the glass down; the whisky lifts at the edges in a tiny amber swell before settling. The stranger watches it like they expect the laws of physics to betray them personally. “Tab or cred?” I ask. “Cred.” They slide a chip across. Their hand is gloved, fingertips worn shiny. I slot it into the till. A balance flashes up fat enough to be suspicious in this neighborhood. That gets Seli’s attention too. I can feel her glance cut sideways without moving her head. The stranger sees me seeing the balance. “It’s clean.” “I didn’t say otherwise.” “You thought it.” “I think lots of things. Most of them are uncharitable.” That earns me a small, tired huff of laughter. Better than a smile. Smiles can lie outright. Laughter usually has to pass through something true. “What should I call you?” I ask. They wrap both hands around the whisky, though they don’t drink yet. “Tonight? Vale.” “Only tonight?” They look up at me then, eyes a pale gray-violet that catches too much light. “Do you ask all your customers for legal identities?” “Only the ones who arrive looking like they’ve been chased through maintenance ducts.” Parn turns on his stool. “If there’s chasing happening, I’d like notice. At my age I need a head start.” “There’s no chasing,” Vale says quickly. “Mm.” I lean on the bar. “That answer usually means there was recently chasing.” Junie slides off her stool and wanders closer under the pretense of wanting another drink. “Nets, I’ll take a cider.” “You hate cider.” “I’m broadening myself.” Vale clocks her station badge and goes a shade tenser. Not much. Enough. I set a bottle under the tap. “You’re making my new customer nervous.” “I’m naturally soothing,” Junie says. “Like decompression.” Vale finally takes a swallow of the whisky. Their eyes close for half a second. When they open them, they look less frayed around the edges, but only just. “I’m not here to make trouble,” they say. Seli laughs into her own glass. “That’s exactly what trouble says before ordering expensive whisky.” “Enough,” I tell her, but gently. The door opens again. This time four people come in together, and the room shifts so subtly it would be easy to miss if I didn’t make a living off currents. Two in merchant-cut coats too neat for actual docking work. One compact woman with a shock baton hanging visible at her belt, which is a choice. And behind them, broad enough to eclipse the corridor light for a second, a man in station security gray. Junie goes still beside me. “That’s not my shift.” “Congratulations,” I say under my breath. The four spread out in a practiced fan, not threatening exactly, but not not threatening either. Looking. Hunting, if we’re being accurate, and I have a private rule about accuracy. Mostly I only use it when things are getting dangerous. Vale lowers their glass very carefully to the bar. I wipe a dry spot with a cloth. “Friends of yours?” “No.” “Debts?” “No.” “Exes?” That almost gets a smile. “Worse.” The security officer scans the room. His gaze passes over Parn, over Seli, over Junie—and lands on Vale. There it sticks. “Damn,” Junie murmurs. Vale’s hand drifts away from the whisky and beneath the bar line, where I can’t see it. “Don’t,” I say quietly. Their eyes flick to mine. “You don’t know what they want.” “Maybe not. But if you pull a weapon in my bar, I become irritable.” “I’m serious.” “So am I.” The officer starts toward us. Parn mutters, “I hate when Nets gets calm. Means paperwork.” Seli swivels fully on her stool, openly invested now. “Anyone want to tell the rest of us whether we’re about to be murdered?” “Probably not murdered,” I say. Junie shoots me a look. “That is not reassuring.” The baton woman peels off and heads for the back corridor instead, toward the private booths and supply lockup. Searching exits. Efficient. I dislike efficient people on instinct. The officer stops at the bar. Up close he smells like ionized fabric and impatience. “Evening, Nettle.” I hate hearing my full name from people who aren’t regulars. “Officer.” He glances at Vale. “We’re looking for an individual connected to the theft of restricted cargo from Dock Nine.” Vale says nothing. I keep polishing the same spot. “Dock Nine sounds like your department.” “It became station-wide when one of the stolen items turned out to be a bonded relay core.” That lands in the room with a weight all its own. Even Parn straightens. Junie whispers, “A relay core? Here?” “Apparently,” I say. The officer places one palm on the bar. “We have reason to believe the suspect may attempt to fence it here.” The insult in that is almost funny. “At my establishment? Officer, I am wounded. People only fence ordinary contraband here.” Seli coughs into her hand to hide a laugh. His expression doesn’t move. “You understand the seriousness.” “I understand you came in with three plainclothes and a visible baton to make sure everyone else does too.” His eyes narrow. “If anyone matching the circulated profile has entered this bar, you are required to report it.” Vale sits beside that sentence like a live wire disguised as a person. Their face has gone unreadable. Too unreadable. I tilt my head. “Circulated where?” He taps his wrist comp. A description flashes holo-blue between us, angled for me. Species markers partial. Height approximate. Dark cranial tendrils. Gray-violet eyes. Close enough for government work. I don’t look at Vale. I don’t have to. I can feel the whole bar pretending not to. “Nets,” Junie says softly, warning in one syllable. The gravity flickers harder this time. Every glass on the bar shivers. The whisky in front of Vale lifts in a trembling wave, almost cresting the rim. Somewhere in the back a stack of plates clatters. In the half-second everyone glances up, Vale leans toward me and says, so low only I can hear, “If they take me, people on this station die.” I keep my face blank because I learned that trick long before I learned to pour a proper drink. The officer waits. The room waits. And behind my ribs, something old and buried begins, very inconveniently, to wake up.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the requested cozy sci-fi atmosphere. The author successfully establishes the setting of The Last Outpost as a lived-in space station bar through concrete details like gravity hiccups, algae stout, and the station's "slow-spin hub." The world-building feels natural and unforced. The dialogue achieves the requested 60% ratio and flows naturally, with distinct voices for each character. Nets has a dry, observational wit ("I think lots of things. Most of them are uncharitable"), while the regulars each have their own speech patterns - Parn's gruff commentary, Seli's sardonic observations, and Junie's defensive protests about her "maintenance crush." The banter feels authentic to people who know each other well. The narrative voice is consistent throughout, maintaining Nets' first-person present perspective with appropriate bartender observations about reading people and managing social dynamics. The prose strikes a good balance between descriptive and functional, avoiding purple prose while still creating vivid imagery ("thick tentacled dreads, dark and glossy, tied back badly and still escaping in every direction"). The conflict builds effectively from Vale's nervous arrival through the revelation of the stolen relay core to the final tension when Vale warns that "people on this station die" if captured. The stakes escalate naturally without feeling forced, and the chapter ends on genuine suspense without resolving the central conflict as requested. Character actions feel believable within the established world. Vale's cautious behavior, the security team's methodical search pattern, and Nets' protective instincts all ring true. The regulars' reactions to the unfolding drama feel authentic to bar patrons caught in unexpected excitement. The piece shows emotional complexity in Nets' internal conflict between self-preservation and helping someone in trouble, hinted at through her observation that "something old and buried begins, very inconveniently, to wake up." This suggests character depth without over-explaining. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly overwrought ("like a live wire disguised as a person") and a few instances of telling rather than showing, particularly in character descriptions. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls like unearned transformations or incongruent positivity, maintaining appropriate tension throughout. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft with engaging dialogue, solid pacing, and effective world-building. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
