+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By eleven in the morning, New Orleans already felt like it had a fever. Not a sick fever. More like the city had decided sweating was a personality trait and everybody else was just going to have to respect that. The air pressed against my skin like a wet hand. My T-shirt stuck to my back. My backpack strap had dug a groove into my shoulder so deep I was pretty sure it was now part of my skeleton. I stood on a sidewalk that looked like it had seen some things, watching cars slide past, and tried not to look like a sixteen-year-old idiot who'd run away from home with sixty-three dollars, half a pack of crackers, and a phone at 12%. Which, unfortunately, was exactly what I was. A brass band was playing somewhere nearby. Or maybe a trumpet. Or maybe New Orleans just came with a soundtrack, and I was hearing things now because I hadn't slept properly since Tennessee. Two days ago, I told my family I was trans. Forty-eight hours later, I was in Louisiana. Honestly? If somebody had pitched this as a movie, I'd have said the plot was trying too hard. My phone buzzed in my hand and nearly stopped my heart. Mom. I stared at the screen until it went dark. Missed call. Then a text. WHERE ARE YOU Then another. Taylor please And then, because the universe enjoys layering trauma like a trifle, one from my older brother: you are being selfish as hell I locked the phone without answering and immediately hated myself, which was becoming kind of a hobby. My chest felt hollowed out. I wanted my mom. I wanted to never see my family again. I wanted someone to call me by the right name and mean it. I wanted to rewind forty-eight hours and also set them on fire. Conflicting emotions: now available in family-size packaging. A man pushing a cooler on a cart looked me over as he passed. “You lost, baby?” There is no answer to that question that doesn't make you sound pathetic. “Maybe,” I said. He jerked his chin down the block. “Canal’s that way. River’s that way. Trouble’s everywhere.” “Cool,” I said. “Helpful.” He barked a laugh and kept walking. I started moving because standing still felt dangerous. Maybe it wasn't. Maybe everything felt dangerous now because my nervous system had become one of those car alarms that goes off if a leaf touches the windshield. Downtown New Orleans was louder than any place I'd ever been. Not just volume-loud. Life-loud. Music leaking out of open doors. People arguing like they were auditioning. Tourists in matching shirts. A woman in gold heels smoking under a balcony dripping plants. A guy painted silver pretending to be a statue and then winking when I looked at him too long. Nobody knew me here. That thought was terrifying for at least three minutes before it became, briefly, exhilarating. Nobody knew me here. Nobody knew the old name that still felt like a dead bug on my skin every time I heard it. Nobody knew the version of me that sat in church folding and unfolding the bulletin while the pastor talked about God's design in that sweet slow voice people use when they're saying something cruel. I could be anybody. Yeah, except I still had nowhere to go, so maybe let's not get too poetic. I found a convenience store with air conditioning so aggressive it felt religious. I spent four dollars on a bottle of water and the saddest turkey sandwich ever manufactured, then took them outside because if I stayed in there much longer I was probably going to steal a charger, and I was trying to limit the number of crimes in my self-discovery era. There was a little paved square with a bench and a tree doing its best. I sat down and ate half the sandwich. It tasted like wet paper and regret. “You gonna finish that?” I looked up. The kid standing a few feet away looked about my age, maybe seventeen. Skinny in a wiry, feral-cat way. Buzzed dark hair under a faded red bandana. Huge T-shirt, ripped black jeans, one sneaker gray with dirt. Their eyes flicked from the sandwich to my face and then away again, fast but not shy exactly. More like they were used to checking exits. “Depends,” I said. “Are you a seagull?” They snorted. “No. Seagulls got higher standards.” I held out the other half. “Knock yourself out.” They took it, but carefully, like this might be a test. “Thanks.” “No problem.” They sat on the far end of the bench without asking. Close enough to be rude, far enough to run. I drank water and watched them eat. They ate fast at first, then slowed down when they noticed me noticing. “What?” they said. “Nothing.” “You got a staring problem.” “You got a sandwich problem.” That got a tiny smile. “Fair.” For a minute we sat in silence while the city ricocheted around us. Then they said, “You’re not from here.” “Wow. Was it the expression of raw panic or the backpack of poor decisions?” “Both.” They wiped their mouth with the back of their hand. “Name?” The question hit harder than it should have. Name. Such a normal thing. Such a minefield. “Taylor,” I said. It still felt new in my mouth sometimes. Not wrong. Just tender. Like skin under a peeled bandage. They nodded once, no weird pause, no repeat of my old name from some invisible file in their head. “I’m Jules.” “Nice to meet you, Jules.” “Mm. We’ll see.” I laughed before I could stop myself. Jules leaned back, looking me over again. “You ran.” Not a question. I tightened my grip on the water bottle. “What makes you say that?” “You got that look.” “What look?” “The one where every siren sounds personal.” I didn't answer, which was apparently answer enough. Jules tugged at a loose thread on their jeans. “Relax. I’m not gonna narc. Cops don’t care unless you’re making rich people uncomfortable.” “Good to know.” “They'll care if you look too young and too alone in the Quarter, though.” “The Quarter?” Jules gave me a long look. “Jesus. You really just fell off a truck.” “Close, actually.” That made them laugh, and this time it stayed longer. They told me where not to go with my backpack hanging open. Which streets got stupid after dark. Which convenience stores would let you charge a phone if the cashier liked your face. Which shelters were “fine if you don’t mind getting your shoes stolen” and which ones were “absolutely not unless rabies sounds fun.” It should have made me trust them less, how much they knew. Instead it made me listen harder. “So,” Jules said eventually, “what are you running from?” I looked out at the street. A streetcar rattled past like it had all the time in the world. I envied that. “My family,” I said. “Sort of.” Jules waited. “I told them something,” I said. “And they acted like I’d detonated the house.” Jules's face changed, just a fraction. Not surprise. Recognition. “Oh,” they said softly. That one syllable nearly undid me. I swallowed hard. “Yeah. Oh.” “You safe going back?” “No.” That came out too fast. Maybe it was true. Maybe I didn't know what kind of not safe I meant. Nobody hit me. Not exactly. But there are a lot of ways to make a home unlivable. Silence can do it. The wrong name can do it. Your mother crying like you died while you're standing right there definitely helps. Jules nodded like they understood the shape of that. “My turn?” they said after a second. “You don't have to.” “I know.” They picked at their thumbnail. “I’m not from here either.” “Where from?” “Texas.” “That's a big state. Narrows it down zero.” “Good.” Their smile was crooked and brief. “I left a few months ago.” “A few months?” “Yeah.” That did something ugly to my stomach. A few months. The phrase opened like a trapdoor under me. I had been in New Orleans for, what, maybe three hours? I was still thinking in terms of lunch. Jules had whole seasons in this. “How old are you?” I asked. “Seventeen.” “Do your parents know where you are?” Jules looked away. “Not exactly.” There was something in that answer. Something sealed over. Before I could ask, a police car rolled slowly down the street. Not even coming toward us, just existing, but Jules went rigid. Every muscle tightened. Their hand shot out and grabbed my wrist hard enough to hurt. “Don’t look at them,” they said under their breath. My pulse kicked. “Why?” “Just don’t.” So of course I looked a little, because I am apparently committed to surviving through bad instincts alone. The cop in the passenger seat glanced out the window. Jules dropped my wrist and stood up too casually. “Come on,” they muttered. “Now.” I followed, because whatever secret Jules had, it was big enough to put fear in their bones, and because they were the only person who had talked to me like I wasn't a problem to be solved. We turned down a side street under a tangle of balconies and laundry and shade. My phone buzzed again in my pocket. 11%. I didn't check it. Jules kept walking, fast, one shoulder hunched, like they knew exactly how visible a person could be. I fell into step beside them, heart hammering, the city opening wider and stranger around us. I had wanted gone. Now I was here. And here, apparently, came with rules nobody had explained yet.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a blend of vulnerability, defensive humor, and raw emotional honesty that feels genuinely adolescent without being patronizing. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece maintains first person mixed tense throughout, incorporates substantial internal monologue showing Taylor's conflicted emotions, includes meaningful dialogue with Jules (another runaway with clear secrets), and covers the specified timeframe of one day. The setting of New Orleans is vividly rendered through sensory details that avoid tourist clichés. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor's voice feels authentic - the mix of bravado and terror, the self-deprecating humor masking deep pain, and the way they process their family's rejection all ring true. Jules is efficiently but effectively sketched as someone with their own mysterious past, and their interaction with Taylor feels natural rather than forced. The dialogue crackles with personality and subtext, particularly in the exchanges about names and running away. The prose style is confident and engaging, with memorable phrases like "conflicting emotions: now available in family-size packaging" and "my nervous system had become one of those car alarms that goes off if a leaf touches the windshield." These moments of dark humor feel earned rather than forced, providing relief without undermining the serious subject matter. The emotional complexity is well-handled - Taylor's simultaneous longing for and rejection of family, the terror and exhilaration of anonymity, and the careful dance of trust with Jules all feel psychologically realistic. The piece avoids easy answers or unearned optimism while still suggesting possibility. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors feel slightly overworked, and the pacing could be tighter in places. The ending, while appropriately uncertain, might benefit from slightly more concrete stakes or direction. However, these are relatively minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By the time the sun had climbed to that accusatory angle it reserved for bad decisions, Midas was already having a conversation with a stuffed koala. This was not, in the current age, especially remarkable. Chewy sat on an upturned fuel drum in the courtyard of Home Base—which was what they called the roofless, drafty remains of the old municipal library, because “crumbling death trap with useful shelves” was too long to paint on the wall. He was grey once, perhaps. Time had since recoloured him the shade of old dishwater and noble endurance. One button ear hung by a seam. His animatronic eyes, however, still worked after a fashion, flickering faint greens and blues and the occasional alarming red whenever his internal ancient mechanisms found occasion to be expressive. Midas crouched before a map scratched in charcoal on a salvaged bit of signage that still faintly read WELCOME TO LOWER CAR PARK LEVEL B. “Right,” he said, with the determined tone of a man setting out to meet his fate and hoping to be polite about it. “The mission is simple.” Chewy’s eyes glowed a sceptical amber. Midas ignored this. “We go to the old arcade district, we retrieve the copper coils Old Jen swears are still in the ceiling of the Regent Picture House, and we return by dusk. No detours. No heroics. No speaking to strangers with crossbows. No crawling into holes to see where they go.” Chewy blinked bright green, which Midas had come to understand as approval, or perhaps a short in the wiring. “And if,” Midas added, wagging a finger at the koala, “you start flashing red at inopportune moments, I’m putting a sock over your face.” Chewy’s eyes went innocent blue. The world after the Fall had become simpler in the same way a kicked anthill became less bureaucratic. The machines had stopped. The lights had gone out. Towers full of cleverness became weather vanes for crows. People relearned practical things with great enthusiasm and variable success: mending shoes, preserving food, and how to distrust anyone smiling too hard. The remains of the old world were still everywhere, poking up through vines and dust, waiting to collapse under exactly the wrong foot. Midas had a knack for finding that foot. They set out through the weed-cracked avenues of the old town, Midas with a sack, a length of rope, and a spear that looked as though it had started life as three unrelated objects. Chewy rode in the crook of his arm with the patient dignity of a saint being carried to a minor but unavoidable martyrdom. The streets were alive in the post-technological fashion: pigeons nesting in traffic lights, ivy strangling office blocks, and traders setting up in the shells of cafés whose menus still offered things involving avocado. Somewhere in the distance, a wind-bent sign clanged like a blacksmith with no sense of rhythm. “So far,” said Midas, as they crossed what had once been a pedestrian plaza and was now mostly feral mint, “nothing has gone sideways.” Chewy’s eyes flickered yellow in warning. There are, broadly speaking, two ways to invite disaster. The first is to announce that everything is fine. The second is to be Midas. They found the Regent Picture House half-swallowed by a neighbouring building that had collapsed years ago with all the grace of a drunk aristocrat sitting down too quickly. The façade still held a sun-bleached glamour: chipped gilt letters, torn posters advertising films no one remembered, and a pair of doors grand enough to suggest the inside had once sold illusion by the hour. Inside, the air smelled of mould, dust, and very old popcorn—which, if you thought about it, was merely another kind of dust with ambition. Midas stared up into the lobby ceiling. There, above a cracked chandelier and a hanging colony of something best left unnamed, ran a nest of copper cabling. “You see?” he said. “Straight up, quick salvage, and out.” Chewy’s eyes went a doubtful mauve. The ladder they found backstage had lost confidence in itself decades earlier. It creaked under Midas’s weight with the resigned complaint of old joints and public infrastructure. Halfway up, he paused. “Hand me the knife.” Chewy, being a stuffed koala of limited limb articulation, did not. “Right,” said Midas. “Well. Moral support, then.” Chewy’s eyes warmed to green. Midas reached the catwalk, edged across it, and began sawing at the first coil. For one shining moment it seemed possible that the day might permit competence. Then the catwalk, insulted by optimism, gave way. There was a noise like a metal orchestra being pushed downstairs. Midas dropped through rotted planks, grabbed a curtain rope, swung wildly over the old stalls, and collided with the edge of the stage in a burst of dust and language. Chewy sailed through the air in a graceful arc and landed in the conductor’s pit. His eyes flashed a furious red. This would have been merely embarrassing, had the racket not attracted the attention of the Candleboys. The Candleboys were a local gang whose defining features were soot, opportunism, and an almost religious attachment to wax. They filtered in through side doors with clubs, knives, and the expressions of men who regarded other people’s property as a helpful first draft. “Well now,” said their leader, a gaunt fellow in a coat made from curtains. “Treasure hunters.” “Recyclers,” said Midas, hauling himself up and trying to look taller than his biography suggested. Chewy’s eyes flashed red again. “Got company?” said Curtain Coat, peering into the pit. Midas dived for Chewy just as one of the Candleboys lunged. They went over the orchestra rail together in an untidy heap, slid down a bank of collapsed seats, and vanished through a maintenance hatch concealed by the sort of accident no architect had intended but fate had approved. The tunnel beyond was dark, damp, and smelled of history with a fungal aftertaste. Behind them came shouts, then the thump of pursuit. Midas ran bent double, one hand on the wall, the other clutching Chewy. “This,” he panted, “still technically counts as returning by dusk.” The tunnel sloped. The floor became slick. Somewhere ahead water moved with the calm confidence of something that had all the time in the world. Midas’s foot met an old patch of algae, and the laws of motion, delighted at being remembered, took personal interest. He skidded, pinwheeled, and shot down the incline on his back like a toboggan assembled by an idiot. Chewy’s eyes strobe-flashed every colour his antique circuitry could produce. They splashed into a storm drain with all the dignity of laundry. The current took them. There are many ways to return home. Walking proudly through the gate is one. Being delivered half-drowned in a ribbon of brown water through the old drainage culvert is another, and has the virtue of surprise. Home Base’s lookouts heard the shouting first. “Open the grate!” bellowed Midas, emerging from the tunnel mouth clinging to an uprooted shopping trolley. “And perhaps prepare tea! Or a priest!” They hauled him in with poles. Chewy was retrieved from the trolley basket, eyes blinking a weak but offended blue. Midas lay on the stones of the library courtyard and coughed up water of uncertain age while a small audience gathered in the warm spirit of communal concern that mostly wanted details. Old Jen folded her arms. “Where are my copper coils?” Midas sat up slowly. Around his neck, looped by chance and catastrophe, hung a glorious tangle of stripped copper wire torn free during his descent through the theatre and somehow retained through the tunnel, the slide, and the drain. He looked at it. Then at Chewy. Chewy’s eyes glowed smug green. “I meant to do that,” said Midas. A cheer went up, because people are surprisingly willing to celebrate success if they don’t inspect its method too closely. That might have been the end of it, had the Candleboys not chosen that precise moment to locate the outer wall and begin shouting threats through the cracks. At nearly the same time, the swollen storm drain, burdened by afternoon runoff, surged again and flooded the lower stacks, sending three days’ worth of carefully dried firewood floating through the history section. So Midas returned home in multiple ways at once: as a hero through the culvert, as a fugitive by pursuit, and as an accessory to minor hydraulic disaster. By evening he was wrapped in a blanket, bruised in twelve significant places, and helping barricade the gate with a shelf labelled MODERN EUROPEAN ECONOMICS, which finally proved useful to somebody. Chewy sat on the parapet beside him, one ear drooping, fur muddy, eyes flickering gentle blue into green. Midas sighed. “Tomorrow,” he said, “we do something safe.” Chewy’s eyes turned a brilliant, disbelieving red. And because the world still possessed a sense of humour, the shelf collapsed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and philosophical asides woven into adventure narrative. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both desolate and oddly cozy, populated by pragmatic survivors who've adapted to their circumstances with characteristic human resilience. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a genuinely hapless protagonist whose disasters feel organic to his character rather than forced by plot convenience. His relationship with Chewy, the animatronic koala, is cleverly developed through the color-coded eye responses, creating genuine personality for what could have been a mere prop. The dynamic between them feels authentic and endearing. The prose style closely mirrors Pratchett's voice, with well-crafted metaphors ("collapsed years ago with all the grace of a drunk aristocrat sitting down too quickly") and the kind of wry observations that made Pratchett distinctive ("people are surprisingly willing to celebrate success if they don't inspect its method too closely"). The pacing moves smoothly from setup through escalating disaster to resolution. The mission structure follows the prompt perfectly - deliberation, inevitable sideways progression, and return home "worse for wear." The cascade of disasters feels natural rather than contrived, each mishap flowing logically from character actions and circumstances. The Candleboys provide effective antagonists without overstaying their welcome. However, the piece occasionally veers toward trying too hard to capture Pratchett's voice, resulting in some slightly overwrought passages. A few metaphors feel forced rather than organic. The ending, while satisfying, leans perhaps too heavily into feel-good territory given the harsh world established earlier, though this doesn't significantly undermine the overall effect. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and serves character development well. The world-building is economical but effective, painting a clear picture of this post-technological society without excessive exposition. The humor consistently lands without becoming forced or repetitive. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell over the door of Chapter & Quill gave a startled little jangle, and a man in a camel coat, dark glasses, and obvious distress stumbled in from the rain. “Please tell me,” he said, breathing hard, “that you possess either a back exit, a trapdoor, or the common decency to lie on my behalf.” Behind the till, Nia Morgan looked up from a stack of invoices. She took him in: wet hair curling at the temples, scarf half-undone, the sort of face people paid frightening amounts to have projected forty feet high. “You’re standing in Fiction,” she said mildly. “Lying’s one of our core services.” He blinked. Then a grin broke across his face, quick and brilliant enough to seem rehearsed until the anxiety under it gave the performance away. “Excellent. I had hoped Wales might still value literature.” Outside, camera shutters clicked in frantic bursts. Someone tried the door, found it locked, and swore. Nia set down her pen. “You locked my door?” “I saved your afternoon from becoming a tabloid background shot. You’re welcome.” The man tugged off his glasses. Yes, definitely him. Adrian Vale: Cardiff-born, globally famous, collector of magazine covers and spectacularly unconvincing denials about various starlets. He looked less lacquered in person. More tired around the eyes. More dangerous for it. “You’re Adrian Vale,” she said. He put a hand dramatically to his chest. “I am wounded by your tone. Usually there’s at least a tiny gasp.” “I’m trying not to encourage you.” “Cruel.” He glanced back at the frosted glass as another flurry of shutters crackled. “Though if cruelty extends to hiding me behind, say, Welsh poetry until they lose the will to live, I’d be very grateful.” Nia jerked her chin toward the back. “Travel section. Hardly anyone goes there by choice.” He stepped closer to the till instead. “And abandon the only person in Cardiff who’s greeted me like a parking violation? Impossible.” “You assume I’m the only one.” “I’m choosing optimism. It’s vital to my emotional ecosystem.” Nia folded her arms. “Is it?” “Absolutely. If people stop liking me, I’m fairly certain I turn to dust.” “That sounds medically serious.” “It is. Tragic, actually.” She should have pointed him toward the rear and returned to her invoices. Instead she found herself studying the raindrops shining in his eyelashes, the restless, contained energy in him, like a man used to entering every room as an event and thrown off balance by entering this one as prey. From outside came a muffled call. “Adrian! We know you’re in there!” He closed his eyes briefly. “Predators,” he muttered. “You say that,” Nia said, “but I imagine you’ve spent rather a lot of time making sure cameras point at you.” He opened one eye. “That sounded perilously like judgment.” “It was observation.” “And here I was hoping for sanctuary.” “You came to a bookshop, not a chapel.” His smile shifted, less broad, more interested. “You do this on purpose, don’t you?” “Run a business?” “No. Refuse to be impressed.” Nia lifted a shoulder. “I’m impressed by first editions, decent coffee, and customers who don’t bolt my door without asking.” His mouth twitched. “I can make coffee.” “I doubt it.” “I can. Badly, but with tremendous charisma.” “That does seem more your line.” He laughed then, genuinely enough that she felt it low in her stomach, annoyingly. “You know, everyone says the Welsh are warm.” “We are. You’re just being handled with appropriate caution.” “Like a stray dog?” “Like a fox in the henhouse.” He leaned on the till, lowering his voice. “That is, for the record, wildly flattering.” Nia busied herself straightening a display that did not need straightening. “You should probably stay away from the front window.” “Ah. Concern. I knew we’d get there.” “It’s concern for the glass.” “Devastating. Still, I’ll take what I can get.” He moved at last, wandering a few paces into the shop. His fingers skimmed the spines with absent familiarity rather than celebrity performance. “This is a lovely place.” “Mm.” “No, truly. The floors creak as if they have opinions. That chair by the radiator looks like someone has cried in it over Russian novels. There’s a shop cat, isn’t there?” Nia looked up sharply. “How did you know about Dafydd?” “Because there’s fur on that entire shelf, and because every proper bookshop has a vaguely disapproving cat. I’m surprised he hasn’t appeared to judge me.” “Dafydd is asleep in local history. He has his standards.” Adrian glanced back, eyes bright. “I’m beginning to think everyone here does.” “You’re not used to that?” He took a beat too long to answer. Then, lightly: “On the contrary. I’m used to a great many standards. Most of them involve my face under good lighting.” That fragile note flashed and vanished so quickly she might have imagined it. But she hadn’t. Under the flippancy was something rawer: a man who could fill premieres and still panic at disapproval from a stranger in an oversized cardigan. Outside, another rattle at the door. “Do they ever simply go away?” Nia asked. “Sometimes if ignored long enough. Sometimes if fed to a larger scandal.” “And today?” “Today I made the tactical error of leaving a hotel through the front entrance.” “That does sound advanced.” “I never claimed to be wise. Only pretty.” Her snort escaped before she could stop it. He turned at once, triumphant. “There. A laugh. We’re making progress.” “Don’t be pleased with yourself.” “Too late. I survive on traces of approval, remember?” “You really do, don’t you?” He looked at her, and for one startling second all the stage-light wit dropped away. “More than I ought to.” The shop seemed to quiet around them, the rain ticking at the windows, the radiator humming in the corner. Nia became acutely aware of her own hands, of the tea stain on the cuff of her jumper, of how impossible his face was when he wasn’t arranging it. She cleared her throat. “Well. That sounds exhausting.” “It is. Though usually I’m better at disguising it.” “Maybe you’re slipping.” “Or maybe Cardiff makes me reckless.” “That’s a line.” “It was,” he admitted. “Not my best. I’m unsettled. You’ve ruined my rhythm.” “I’ll add it to the customer service policy.” He drifted back toward the till. “Nia Morgan.” She stiffened. “How do you know my name?” He pointed to the little handwritten badge pinned to her cardigan. “I’m charming, not psychic.” “Debatable.” “Mm. Keep doing that and I’ll become insufferably invested.” “As opposed to your current state?” He rested both palms on the counter and leaned in just enough to be outrageous if she acknowledged it. “Nia, in my current state I am a humble fugitive seeking asylum among paperbacks. In another minute I may also buy something overpriced and ask for your expert recommendation.” “You think spending money makes you less irritating?” “Often, yes.” “It doesn’t.” “God, you’re magnificent.” The words landed between them with a weight neither of them had quite invited. Heat climbed up Nia’s throat. She reached for the nearest pile of books and nearly knocked one over. “That sounded accidental,” she said. “Terrifyingly so.” He looked just as taken aback as she felt, but his voice had gone softer, stripped of shine. The cameras outside crackled again, distant now, like another weather system. Nia picked up the fallen novel and set it right. “You should stay until they get bored.” “Should I?” “Yes.” “And if they never do?” “Then I’ll put you to work alphabetising crime.” He smiled, slower this time. “You’d trust me with murder?” “I wouldn’t trust you with the till.” “Fair.” He glanced toward the shelves. “What would you recommend, then?” “For hiding from the world?” “For being told off by a beautiful woman in a bookshop while pretending not to enjoy it immensely.” Nia met his eyes despite herself. “We haven’t a section that specific.” “A gap in the market.” She turned, walking toward the contemporary fiction table before she could do something foolish like smile too warmly. Behind her she heard him follow, slower now, matching her pace. “If I buy two books,” he said, “will you concede that you’re at least a little impressed?” “No.” “Three?” “No.” “A signed photograph?” She shot him a look over her shoulder. “I could get one online.” He pressed a hand to his heart again. “You see? Dust. Immediate dust.” And because he said it with such rueful absurdity, because he looked both ridiculous and unexpectedly earnest under the soft shop lights, Nia laughed properly this time. When she handed him a novel, their fingers brushed. It was nothing. It was catastrophic. “Start with this,” she said, far too evenly. Adrian looked at the book, then at her. “And if I want to know how it ends?” “You read it.” A beat. “Cruel,” he murmured. “Fiction’s one of our core services,” Nia said. Outside, the rain kept falling over Cardiff. Inside, with paparazzi at the door and an actor smiling at her like she’d just invented oxygen, the little bookshop felt suddenly, inconveniently, like the beginning of something.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue. Adrian Vale is portrayed as a charming but insecure celebrity who relies on others' approval for validation, while Nia Morgan serves as his foil - outwardly unimpressed but inwardly affected by his presence. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with each character maintaining a distinct voice. Adrian's speech patterns reflect his performative nature and underlying vulnerability, while Nia's responses are grounded, practical, and tinged with dry Welsh wit. The banter feels natural and serves the dual purpose of revealing character while building romantic tension. The setting is well-established through specific details like the shop cat Dafydd, the creaking floors, and the cozy atmosphere that contrasts with the chaos outside. The Welsh setting feels authentic rather than superficial, integrated into character names and dialogue patterns. The romantic chemistry builds organically through their verbal sparring, with moments of genuine connection breaking through Adrian's practiced charm. The author handles the "immune to charm" dynamic well, showing how Nia's resistance paradoxically draws Adrian in while revealing her own growing attraction. However, the piece occasionally veers toward overly clever dialogue that feels slightly constructed rather than completely natural. Some exchanges, while witty, border on being too polished for spontaneous conversation. The prose, while generally elegant, sometimes indulges in slightly purple passages, particularly in the descriptive moments. The pacing works well, building from Adrian's frantic entrance to the quieter, more intimate moments of connection. The ending strikes an appropriate note of possibility without rushing into unearned romantic declarations. The characterization shows good depth, particularly in revealing Adrian's fragile ego beneath his celebrity persona. Nia's character, while somewhat more archetypal as the "unimpressed local," still feels genuine in her responses and internal conflict. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 77.8
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The latch yielded beneath Aya’s gloved hand with a softness that made her skin crawl. She had expected resistance: old tumblers grumbling in their brass housings, ward-etched iron protesting the touch, some muttered warning from the dark. Instead the underhold door opened with the meek civility of a parlour maid admitting callers. A draught of cold, stale air moved out to meet them, carrying the smells of lamp oil, wet stone, mildew, and something metallic besides—blood, perhaps, or merely the memory of it soaked into the mortar. Aya paused on the threshold. Behind her, Katra shifted her weight and made the leather of her coat creak. “I don’t like it,” she muttered, low as a knife being drawn an inch from its sheath. “Too neat.” “Everything important is hidden beneath something neat,” Simeon said. He tried for dryness, but it came out strained. His spectacles caught a ribbon of gaslight as he peered past Aya into the dark. “That is, generally speaking, the principle of safes, tombs, and certain marriages.” Aya might have smiled, had the silence not pressed so hard upon her ears. The underhold steps descended in a curl of black stone worn hollow by generations of criminal feet. Gas lamps in iron cages hissed from the walls at measured intervals, each one casting a sickly amber halo and a forest of bars across the passage. Their light shook in the draught, and all the shadows it made seemed too long, too thin, too eager. She descended first, gloved fingers brushing the damp wall. Beneath the cuffs of her sleeves, the little tremors had begun in her wrists. Not fear alone. The immateria had a way of stirring where old things were hidden and older sins were layered thick in the earth. It pricked at her senses like cold needles. Something down here had been fed with secrecy for a very long time. Her mother had once told her that forbidden power was seldom loud. It whispered. It waited. Men feared the spectacle of sorcery because they did not understand that its worst aspect was patience. At the foot of the stairs the passage opened into a vaulting chamber, cathedral-like in proportion and funereal in aspect. Stone pillars rose thick as ship masts to support a soot-dark ceiling crossed with ancient beams and newer iron braces. Chains hung from pulleys overhead. Crates and ironbound chests stood in ordered rows. At the far end of the chamber, beyond a circle of warding marks worked in silver and pitch into the flagstones, stood a pedestal of black scythan stone. Upon it rested the artifact. Aya saw only enough to know it was there: a hooked shape under a glass bell, all fossil-dark sheen and whale-bone pallor, as if carved from something dredged from the floor of a dead sea. And before it, in a high-backed chair set with absurd calm at the centre of the chamber, sat Madame Vale. The guild leader crossed one boot over the other and regarded them as though they had arrived late to an appointment. She wore mourning silk under a coat of bottle-green velvet with braid at the cuffs, and around her throat was tied a strip of black lace that made her face seem sharper, almost sepulchral in the unsteady light. A pistol rested loose in one hand. In the other she idly tapped the arm of the chair with a narrow-bladed stiletto. She was not alone. Six bodyguards emerged from the architecture of the room as though the shadows had spat them out: broad men in dock leathers and wool greatcoats, one with a brutal steam-rivet gauntlet hissing softly at the joints, another cradling a whale-gun cut down for close use, all of them armed, all of them already aiming. For one absurd instant Aya thought: So she was never taken. Then the fuller truth landed like an iron weight in her stomach. The arrest had been theatre. A sweep of the board. A net cast for ambitious fish. “Well,” said Madame Vale. “There she is.” Katra swore under her breath and reached half an inch toward her belt before three hammers clicked back in answer. “Don’t,” said Vale, almost kindly. “I should hate to splash the stone.” Simeon lifted both hands at once. “Madame, let us all be rational. We are plainly outnumbered, and I have always held that dying in cellars is bad for one’s complexion.” Aya said nothing. Her pulse had become a hammer-beat against the silk binding beneath her mask. She felt suddenly, hideously, like a child at a masquerade who had wandered into the wrong room and found the adults smiling. Vale’s gaze fixed on her. “You were the one I wanted to see most. Step forward, little revenant.” Aya remained where she was. “You staged your own capture.” Vale smiled without mirth. “You make it sound theatrical. It was accounting.” The pistol angled, not quite at Aya’s heart, but near enough. “Step. Forward.” Katra moved closer to Aya’s shoulder. Simeon, poor fool, did the same on the other side, though he looked as though he expected his knees to resign beneath him at any moment. Aya took one measured step. The chamber answered with the tiny chorus of men tightening grips on metal. “I had wondered,” Vale said, “which of my eager young climbers would fancy herself fit to seize the underhold before the dust had settled. I had half a sovereign on Dren, and lost it. He lacks imagination. But you—ah.” Her eyes narrowed. “You have always had imagination in dangerous quantities.” Aya forced her voice level. “If you intended to root out disloyalty, you might have done so upstairs.” “Upstairs,” said Vale, “one catches rats. Down here one catches the rat-catcher.” The words settled into the stones. Aya’s mind ran, not toward escape—there was none visible—but toward sequence, leverage, delay. Every second won was a thread. Enough threads made rope. Enough rope, perhaps, reached daylight. Then one of the guards was suddenly behind her. She had not heard him move. A hard hand seized her wrist; another twisted her arm cruelly up her back. Katra barked and surged, was checked at once by two men and the round black mouth of the whale-gun. Simeon gave a distressed noise as someone tore his cane from him and struck the backs of his knees. “On your knees,” growled the man behind Aya. She did not comply quickly enough. He forced her down. Stone slammed into her through wool and bone. Katra was thrown beside her, hair shaken loose, eyes bright with murder. Simeon knelt less by choice than by collapse, breathing hard through his nose. Vale rose from her chair. The lamps spat and fluttered. Her shadow stretched monstrously across the warded circle, long and tapering, as though some taller thing stood up in her stead. “You hid yourself cleverly,” she said to Aya, and came close enough for the scent of violet water and gun oil to cut through the underhold damp. “A mask. A modulated voice. Fine gloves. Better manners than anyone here. I said to myself, either she is nobility, or she has robbed nobility very recently.” Aya kept her face tipped away. Vale clicked her tongue. “No. We shall have honesty.” She caught the edge of the mask and tore it free. The cool air hit Aya’s skin like a slap. For a heartbeat nobody spoke. The bodyguards looked first at her features, then at one another with the ugly fascination men reserve for scandals involving money or bloodlines. Simeon shut his eyes. Katra inhaled once, sharp and furious, as if the exposure had been physical violence done to them all. Madame Vale’s brows rose, and then she laughed—a low, delighted sound that left no warmth in the room. “Oh, merciful depths,” she said. “Lady Aya Valcrest.” The name echoed. Aya heard it as if from very far away. It was done, then. The secret was not merely endangered but dead. She saw in a flash the consequences assembling themselves with merciless precision: the family crest dragged through court mud; her father’s face gone white with rage; invitations withdrawn; whispers behind gloved hands; the old charge that had hanged her mother hauled out and nailed now to the daughter. Aristocrat. Thief. Possibly worse. And if they learned what she could do— She thought of the thing that answered her when she reached into the black between worlds. Thought of how quickly these men, who trusted brass and oil and steel because they could hold them, would empty every chamber they carried into her on principle. “Well,” Katra said through clenched teeth, “that’s spoiled the surprise.” “Hold your tongue,” snapped a guard. “Gladly,” Katra said, “once it’s out of yours.” Vale crouched before Aya, velvet pooling around her boots. “This is a jewel beyond price. Do you know what a ransom a house like Valcrest would pay to keep one daughter from the scaffold and one family from disgrace?” Aya made herself meet the woman’s gaze. “Enough, perhaps, that you needn’t sell the artifact.” “Aye,” Vale said. “And more useful besides. An aristocrat in my debt. A fox in a henhouse by birthright. I begin to see providence.” Aya swallowed. Her mouth had gone dry. “Then let us speak as businesswomen.” Katra made a small sound of protest, but Aya pressed on before the other woman could ruin it with courage. “You kill us here, and you gain little save certainty. Coin can be had elsewhere. Names cannot. Influence cannot.” She drew one careful breath. “Alive, I am worth negotiation. Dead, I am merely a corpse in a cellar and a scandal to be managed.” Vale watched her with keen amusement. “You bargain briskly for a prisoner.” “I was trained well.” “By thieves,” said Vale. “By nobles,” Aya replied, unable to keep the bitterness from it. “There is less difference than polite society pretends.” That won a genuine flash of pleasure from the guild leader. Simeon, still kneeling with his hands bound behind him, found enough voice to add, “If I may recommend the hostage arrangement, Madame, it possesses several advantages over murder. Foremost among them being that murder is so final, while extortion may be repeated.” Vale stood and considered the trio as one might consider specimens under glass. “And the other two?” she asked. Katra bared her teeth. “Go to hell.” “There speaks loyalty,” Vale murmured. “There speaks sound advice,” said Simeon. Aya felt the chamber’s oppressive cold settle deeper into her bones. Every flicker of gaslight seemed to animate the carved grotesques upon the pillars; every shadow looked like a mouth beginning to open. Somewhere water dripped with the slow regularity of a counting clock. The artifact under its bell stood silent, patient, listening. She had bought moments, nothing more. Yet moments were still life. “Keep them alive,” Vale said at last to her men. “Separate cells. Search them thoroughly. Lady Aya is to be handled carefully—carefully, do you hear me? She has become valuable. And I mislike damaging merchandise.” A guard hauled Aya to her feet. As they dragged her past the warded circle, she looked once toward the scythan relic. In the wavering light it seemed less an object than an eye, old and lidless, fixed upon her with abyssal recognition. For the first time that night, true fear touched her—not of prison, or exposure, or even the noose, but of what might yet be required of her before dawn.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing ability with vivid atmospheric description and solid character work. The author successfully creates a tense confrontation scene that adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified Victorian gothic aesthetic. The prose is generally elegant and evocative, with particularly strong atmospheric writing in the opening passages. Descriptions like "the underhold door opened with the meek civility of a parlour maid admitting callers" and the detailed rendering of the chamber create an immersive sense of place. The author effectively builds tension through environmental details and the gradual revelation of the trap. Character voices are well-differentiated and authentic to the setting. Madame Vale's calculated menace comes through clearly in her dialogue, while Simeon's nervous wit ("dying in cellars is bad for one's complexion") provides appropriate levity without undermining the tension. Aya's internal struggle between her aristocratic upbringing and criminal activities is convincingly portrayed. The dialogue generally serves the story well, though some exchanges feel slightly stilted or overly formal even for the Victorian setting. The revelation scene where Aya is unmasked carries appropriate weight, and her subsequent attempt to negotiate demonstrates character agency and intelligence. However, the piece occasionally veers toward purple prose, particularly in some of the more elaborate descriptive passages. Phrases like "cathedral-like in proportion and funereal in aspect" and "sepulchral in the unsteady light" sometimes feel overwrought. The writing also shows some tendency toward tell-don't-show, particularly in explaining character motivations and backstory. The pacing is generally effective, building tension steadily toward the confrontation and maintaining it throughout the negotiation. The ending appropriately dark and leaves the characters in genuine peril while setting up future complications. The worldbuilding elements integrate well, with references to whale-guns, steam-rivet gauntlets, and scythan artifacts feeling organic to the established setting. The magic system and its social implications are handled with appropriate subtlety. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 76.6
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A lazy ceiling fan barely stirs the smoky air inside the bar. It pushes heat, cigarette haze, and the smell of spilled beer in slow circles over warped tables and men who know better than to ask questions. Neon from a busted beer sign bleeds red across the bottles behind the counter. Somewhere in the back, an old radio coughs out jazz through static. Spike Spiegel sits on a stool like he has nowhere else to be and no reason to leave. One long leg stretched out, one bent, cigarette hanging from his mouth, suit wrinkled, eyes half-lidded. He looks lazy in the way a knife on a table looks harmless. His glass is cheap whiskey, mostly melted ice, and he’s making it last because he doesn’t have the money for another. He hears her before he really looks at her. Boot heels. Confident. Sharp. Not stumbling, not hesitant. A predator’s walk. Revy drops onto the stool two spaces away and signals the bartender with two fingers without even checking if he’s looking. Black crop top, cutoffs, dragon ink winding over her arm like it might crawl off her skin and bite someone. She smells like gun oil, smoke, and rain-damp pavement. Two pistols ride low on her hips, visible enough to be a statement. Spike glances over. “Bit overdressed for this place.” Revy turns her head slowly, eyes bright and dangerous. “And you look like a thrift store funeral.” Spike smiles around his cigarette. “That bad, huh?” “Worse.” The bartender sets down her drink. She knocks half of it back in one swallow, then notices the folded bounty slip sticking out from under Spike’s glass. Her gaze drops. His follows a second later. The same face stares up from both their papers: Emil Varga, mid-level broker, trafficker in weapons and information, wanted by three syndicates and two governments. Alive pays more. Dead pays fast. Spike sighs. “There it is.” Revy laughs once, without humor. “You’ve gotta be kidding me.” “Afraid not.” She snatches his paper, checks the details, tosses it back. “So which idiot hired you?” “Depends. Which idiot hired you?” “Cute.” “Sometimes.” Her eyes narrow. “I was on this first.” Spike tips ash into an empty bowl of peanuts. “That so? Then where is he?” “Working on it.” “Mm.” He takes a sip. “Sounds a lot like not having him.” For a second the room seems to tighten around them. The bartender suddenly finds something very important to wipe at the far end of the counter. A few locals drift away from the line of fire by instinct. Revy smiles, and it’s all teeth. “I can fix that. Starting with the competition.” Spike finally turns fully toward her. One dark eye amused, the other distant and unreadable. “You always flirt by threatening homicide?” “You always flirt by being broke in public?” “Only on special occasions.” She should hate that the line lands. She definitely hates that she notices his hands—long-fingered, relaxed, never still. A fighter’s hands. She notices the slouch is fake. Notices the way he clocks exits in mirrors, glasses, bottle reflections. Notices the scar under one eye and the sadness he wears like old cologne. Spike notices plenty too. The twitch in her trigger finger when someone enters. The way she sits where no one can come up behind her. The scar at her thigh. The fury under her skin, banked but never gone. He notices her mouth around the rim of the glass and decides that’s a bad direction for his thoughts. “Tell you what,” he says. “We find him together, split the money.” Revy barks a laugh. “Trust you? I’d trust a land mine with a loose wire before I trust some washed-up pretty boy.” “Ouch.” “You’ll live.” “Probably.” She studies him, annoyed by how little he reacts. Men usually puff up or back off. Spike does neither. He just smokes and watches her like he’s already accepted the worst possible outcome. That’s when the front door opens. A skinny man in a brown jacket freezes three steps inside. Sweat. Nervous eyes. Recognition flashes across his face when he sees the two bounty slips on the bar. Emil Varga bolts. Spike and Revy move at the same time. Her stool crashes backward. His glass spins off the counter and shatters. The bar erupts in shouting as they hit the door shoulder to shoulder and nearly jam in the frame. “Move, asshole,” Revy snaps. “Ladies first.” “Eat shit.” They tear into the alley after Varga. Rainwater glistens in potholes, reflecting neon in oil-slick colors. Varga scrambles over a chain-link fence. Revy fires first, twin pistols barking, rounds punching sparks off metal near his head. “Alive pays more,” Spike calls, vaulting a dumpster. “You want a bonus, shoot his legs!” Varga barrels through a market lane, knocking over crates. Fruit bursts underfoot. Spike cuts left, using speed instead of panic, reading angles. Revy goes right, relentless, gunfire driving civilians screaming into cover. Two hired guards appear from behind a delivery truck, submachine guns up. Spike is already moving. He slips inside the first burst, body loose, almost lazy, then whips a kick into one guard’s wrist. Gun flies. Elbow to throat. Pivot. A shot from Spike’s Jericho drops the second guard in the shoulder. Revy doesn’t even break stride. She fires both pistols sideways, controlled and brutal, shredding the tires on the truck as Varga tries to dive behind it. The vehicle slams crooked into a wall. Varga runs again. “Persistent little bastard,” Spike mutters. They corner him near a motel lot where the city gives up pretending to care. A flickering VACANCY sign paints everything sick pink. Varga, panting and cornered, swipes a key from a fallen maid cart and crashes into Room 12. Revy reaches the door first and kicks it in. Inside, Varga grabs a hostage on instinct—a prostitute in cheap makeup and cheaper perfume—and jams a snub-nose revolver against her temple. “Back off!” Spike appears in the doorway a beat later, gun low, expression unchanged. Revy is already aiming center mass. “Alive,” Spike says quietly. “He’s using cover,” Revy says. “He forfeited alive.” The woman is sobbing. Varga shakes so hard the gun wobbles. Spike sees the tremor, the angle, the mirror over the dresser. Revy sees his pulse in his neck, the weakness in his grip. They also see each other. Each knows the other is calculating the shot. “On three,” Spike says. “Why would I count with you?” “Because if you don’t, one of us gets the hostage.” Revy’s jaw tightens. She hates that he’s right. Varga starts shouting. Neither listens. “One,” Spike says. Revy shifts half an inch. “Two.” Varga jerks at the movement. “Thr—” Two shots crack almost as one. Varga drops. His revolver spins under the bed. The woman screams, then realizes she is not dead. Blood spreads through Varga’s shoulder and thigh. Alive enough. Revy exhales. “I had the shoulder.” “I had the leg.” “Liar.” “Maybe.” The woman bolts past them and keeps running. Then the room goes quiet. Too quiet. One stained bed. One buzzing lamp. One dead roach on the windowsill. Rain tapping the glass. Their guns are still raised, but now they’re pointed at each other. Varga groans on the floor between them. Revy steps inside and kicks the door shut behind her. “So now what, cowboy?” Spike’s tie hangs loose, damp with sweat and rain. “Now we argue over custody, I guess.” “With guns?” “You seem comfortable with the format.” She laughs under her breath, still aiming. “You’re either fearless or suicidal.” “Been called both.” The pink motel light catches in his mismatched eyes. One reflects her cleanly. The other seems to look through her, beyond her, into some private graveyard. She hates that she understands that look. Hates even more that she wants to get closer to it and run from it at the same time. Spike watches her chest rise and fall. Her finger is steady on the trigger. Her mouth is parted from adrenaline. She is danger distilled into a woman-shaped miracle, and every instinct he has says keep moving, don’t get attached, don’t stand still long enough for trouble to learn your name. But he doesn’t move. Revy licks whiskey from her lip, maybe left over from the bar, maybe imagined. “You thinking what I’m thinking?” “That this is a stupid way to die?” “That depends.” Her eyes flick to his mouth, then back to his gun. “Could be a fun way.” He smiles, slow and tired and real enough to hurt. “There’s the flirting again.” “Don’t flatter yourself.” “Wouldn’t dream of it.” Neither lowers their weapon. Neither looks away. On the floor, Varga moans louder, forgotten for the moment. Outside, thunder rolls over the motel roof. Inside, the space between them feels sharper than any knife, hotter than the gun smoke still hanging in the room. Attraction, suspicion, challenge—none of it settled, all of it loaded. Spike tilts his head. “Split the bounty?” Revy’s grin comes back, dangerous as ever. “Maybe.” “Maybe?” “Depends who gets to cuff him.” “Competitive.” “Paranoid.” “Fair.” She takes one slow step forward. He doesn’t retreat. The muzzles stay level, the distance shrinks, and the air turns electric with everything neither one of them is stupid enough to trust. Whatever happens, happens. For now, nothing does.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author successfully captures both Spike's fatalistic cool and Revy's volatile aggression while maintaining their distinct voices throughout. The dialogue crackles with authentic tension, particularly in the bar scene where their verbal sparring reveals character depth beneath surface antagonism. The action sequence is well-choreographed, moving fluidly from bar to chase to motel confrontation. The author shows solid understanding of both characters' fighting styles - Spike's fluid martial arts approach versus Revy's aggressive dual-wielding gunplay. The pacing builds effectively toward the final standoff. The atmospheric writing is particularly strong, with the seedy bar and rain-soaked urban setting providing appropriate noir backdrop. Descriptions like "neon bleeds red" and "danger distilled into a woman-shaped miracle" create vivid imagery without becoming overwrought. However, the piece suffers from some predictable beats. The "both hunting same bounty" setup leads exactly where expected, and the sexual tension follows familiar patterns without surprising developments. The ending, while atmospheric, doesn't resolve the central tension in a particularly innovative way. The character work is the strongest element. Both protagonists remain true to their source material while developing believable chemistry. Their mutual recognition of shared damage and professional competence feels earned rather than forced. The author successfully navigates the challenge of maintaining their essential mistrust while building attraction. Some dialogue exchanges feel slightly forced in their cleverness, and occasional descriptions veer toward purple prose territory. The piece also relies heavily on familiar noir and action tropes without subverting them meaningfully. Despite these issues, the execution is generally solid and the character dynamics compelling. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
