+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
Before dawn the ludus breathed like an animal in sleep. Men snored behind thin walls, coughed, muttered in dreams. Somewhere in the dark a chain scraped stone. I woke to the smell before I opened my eyes: old sweat dried into straw, lamp oil gone sour, damp plaster, and the faint iron tang that never left the place no matter how often they sluiced the yard. Rome had its perfumes—rose oil, cinnamon, incense before the temples—but where men were kept for use, it smelled of leather, vinegar, and bodies. I lay still for a moment on my pallet and listened. Beyond the barred slit of my cell, the city had already begun to stir. Cart wheels groaned over paving stones. A mule brayed. From farther off came the long, thin call of a baker's boy and, after it, the answering bark of dogs. Rome never truly slept. Even in darkness she shifted her weight like some vast creature, fed by grain ships and taxes, by slave backs and senators' ambitions. The man in the next cell, Sabinus, was awake too. I heard him clear his throat and spit. “You dreamt again,” he said quietly. “So did you.” He gave a soft laugh at that. Most of us dreamed. The arena taught a man to do that even when he had never seen sand. Mine were not always of blood. More often they were of water: the grey-green sea off Massilia, where I had first shipped as a free man with a cargo of wine jars; or the bright shallows near home, before debt and war and a magistrate’s clever accounting had narrowed my life to this cell. A key turned in the corridor. The lanista’s steward, a narrow-faced Greek named Menon, passed with a lamp, his sandals whispering. “Up,” he said. “The editor’s steward comes today. No limping, no sulking, no philosophy. You are merchandise until noon and heroes after it.” He enjoyed that sort of thing. Menon had a small education and hated us all for having bodies that could not be ignored. Doors opened one by one. We shuffled into the corridor, rubbing sleep from our eyes, tying belts, pulling rough tunics over scarred shoulders. The lamp flame made every face look hollowed and old. In the yard the morning air struck cool against my skin. I drew it in greedily, though it carried stable stink from the alley and smoke from cook fires beginning in the insulae. Above the roofline, the sky was pearl-colored. Swallows stitched it with black arcs. A trainer tossed us wooden practice swords, and the familiar weight landed in my palm. Not my true weapon, only shaped like it. Still my fingers closed with instinctive affection. I was a murmillo. My proper arm was the gladius: short, broad, plain in outline and perfect in purpose. There were men who loved curved blades, flashy Thracian steel that caught the eye. There were retiarii who boasted of the net and trident, all trick and reach. The gladius had no vanity in it. It asked a man to come close enough to smell his opponent’s breath, close enough to feel fear as heat between bodies. It was a soldier’s blade. A citizen’s empire had been built by thousands of such lengths of iron thrust beneath ribs and into bellies from Spain to Syria. Even in the ludus it linked me to something larger and bitterer. Rome had taken my freedom with a contract and a stamp, then put into my hand the same shape of steel by which she ruled the world. We drilled in pairs while the sun climbed. Wood struck wood with a crack like split kindling. Trainers shouted. Sand puffed around our feet and clung damp to our calves. Sweat soon ran down my spine beneath the leather practice harness. Across from me, Hilarus feinted high and grinned through his broken front teeth when I checked him. He was Syrian, quick as a cat, sold after some auxiliary levy was disbanded and unpaid. In the yard we all became types the crowd would understand—Gaul, Thracian, Samnite, murmillo—though outside those masks our stories were as tangled as Rome’s roads. When the whistle blew for rest, we crouched in a strip of shade against the wall, gulping watered posca from a shared jug. It tasted of vinegar and copper, and I loved it in that moment more than Falernian wine. The editor’s steward arrived near the third hour, carried in a litter with purple trim that tried too hard. He was plump, perfumed, and carefully bored. We stood in a line while Menon named us and listed victories, wounds, and present condition as if reading horseflesh. The steward pinched my arm, turned my wrist, and asked if the scar on my shoulder would show beneath the armor. “If he raises the arm, yes,” Menon said. “Good,” the steward replied. “The people like scars. Reassuring.” I kept my face empty. This was politics, no less than anything debated in the Senate. The man who sponsored games bought cheers the way others bought land. Bread for their bellies and slaughter for their eyes, and the mob would hail him generous, worthy, perhaps fit for office. We in the line were part of the language by which power spoke to the city. Somewhere men in white-bordered togas argued over provinces and taxes and the emperor’s favor; here, a scar was an asset and a death a punctuation mark. When the steward had gone, Menon exhaled and snapped his fingers for the kitchen slaves. We received our noon ration: barley porridge thick enough to turn with a knife, beans softened with onion, a handful of dried figs. Gladiators ate better than many poor citizens. Rome prized us too much to starve us and too little to pity us. I sat on an overturned bucket by the wall and ate slowly. The porridge steamed in the warming day, smelling nutty and plain. A fly worried at the rim of my bowl. Through the gate, left open for deliveries, I could see a ribbon of street. A woman passed balancing a basket of eels on her head, scales flashing silver. Two boys ran after a hoop, barefoot and shrieking. Behind them came a senator’s client in a clean tunic, hurrying with the anxious face of a man who belonged to another man’s schedule. Then a file of chained prisoners shuffled by under guard, dust up to their ankles. One looked scarcely older than sixteen. He stared through the gate as if the yard were a place of privilege. “New quarry workers,” Sabinus said around a mouthful of beans. “Or rowers.” “Or worse luck than ours,” I said. He snorted. “There is always worse luck in Rome. That is how she keeps her foundations level.” I laughed, though there was no mirth in it. Sabinus had once taught rhetoric to provincial boys before some patron’s collapse dragged all dependents under. He still spoke in polished little sentences even with bean skins caught in his beard. Later, I was sent to the armory to oil my equipment. This was the hour I liked best, if a man in my condition could be said to like anything. The armory was dim and cool, its shelves lined with helmets, greaves, shields, and blades hanging in ordered rows. Light fell through a high slit and struck the metal so that edges winked like water. The smell was strong and comforting: olive oil, ash used for polishing, leather, old iron. Outside, Rome was noise and appetite; here, everything had a place and purpose. My gladius lay wrapped in cloth on the bench. I unfolded it with care. Its hilt was bone, worn smooth where my hand had learned it. The pommel had a nick from my first real match, not from combat itself but from the gate as I passed through, trembling enough to misjudge the width. I ran oil along the blade with a wool rag, watching the metal darken and then shine. The sword was not beautiful, but it was honest. It did not pretend to spare. It gave me, within narrow bounds, a say. In the arena the crowd saw costumes, pairings, spectacle. What I felt in my hand was choice measured in inches: thrust, step, turn, live. I remembered the first time the weapons-master placed it before me. “Forget what poets say,” he had said. “A sword is not glory. It is timing.” I had hated him for that, because I had still been full of thoughts fit for poetry—justice, return, the gods’ notice. Years had rubbed those away like rough cloth on painted wood. In their place remained smaller things, perhaps truer ones: the warmth of sun on the yard wall in winter, the weight of a fig in the palm, a blade balanced just so. From beyond the armory came a swell of city sound. A vendor cried hot chestnuts. Temple bells chimed faintly. Somewhere a crowd roared—races at the Circus, perhaps, Green against Blue. Men shouted themselves hoarse for teams and tribunes and emperors they would never touch, while grain dole lines lengthened and landlords stacked families in rooms fit for grain sacks. Yet I could not pretend I stood outside it all. If Rome was unjust, she was also magnificent. Her aqueducts strode the horizon like conquered rivers. Her forums glittered with marble from lands I had never seen. Her appetite made monsters of some men and fed genius in others. Even those of us chewed in her jaws could not deny the size of her. I sheathed the gladius and buckled it to my belt for fitting. Its weight settled against my hip with the old familiar pull, almost intimate. For a moment, absurdly, I felt less alone. By late afternoon the sun had turned white and hard over the yard. Tomorrow or the next day we would be marched to the amphitheater, oiled and armed, to help some ambitious man persuade the crowd he loved them. But this day was still ours in fragments. Sabinus was humming under his breath. Hilarus slept on his back in the shade, mouth open. In the kitchen someone burned garlic, and the sharp smell drifted out. Over the wall came the laughter of free people on their way somewhere they had chosen. I put my hand on the hilt of my sword and closed my eyes. Not in prayer. The gods had heard enough from me. I only stood there and felt the heat, the leather grip, the city rumbling around us like distant surf, and understood that my life, narrowed as it was, remained a life: bounded, watched, wagered on, but still threaded with hunger, memory, bitterness, and the stubborn wish to see another dawn over Rome’s roofs.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and literary merit. The author successfully creates an immersive slice-of-life narrative set in ancient Rome, following a gladiator through a single day at his ludus (gladiator school). The piece excels in several key areas. The sensory details are rich and authentic, from the "old sweat dried into straw, lamp oil gone sour" to the "iron tang that never left the place." The author demonstrates solid historical knowledge, weaving in period-appropriate details like the ludus structure, gladiator types (murmillo, retiarii, Thracian), Roman social hierarchy, and political context without feeling forced or expository. The protagonist's voice is consistent and compelling - world-weary but not self-pitying, observant and philosophical without being pretentious. His background as a former free man adds depth, and his relationship with his gladius serves as an effective metaphor for his constrained agency. The supporting characters like Sabinus (the former rhetoric teacher) and Menon (the bitter steward) feel authentic and serve the narrative well. The prose is generally elegant and controlled, with strong imagery and natural sentence flow. Phrases like "Rome never truly slept. Even in darkness she shifted her weight like some vast creature" demonstrate skillful personification. The author successfully integrates larger political and social themes - the bread and circuses dynamic, the complex relationship between power and spectacle, the layers of Roman society - without heavy-handedness. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Occasionally the prose edges toward purple territory with phrases like "Swallows stitched it with black arcs" or overly poetic descriptions. Some philosophical observations, while well-written, feel slightly forced rather than naturally emerging from the character's experience. The piece also relies heavily on atmospheric description at the expense of dramatic tension - while this fits the "slice of life" brief, it makes some sections feel slightly static. The ending strikes an appropriate note of resigned dignity without false hope or unearned transformation. The protagonist's final moment of acceptance feels genuine and hard-won rather than artificially uplifting. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 89.3
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
12 March 1978 Eilean Dubh Light Station Dear Mam, I am arrived safe, though soaked through from the transfer boat and near sick with the pitching of it. The island is no more than a black tooth in the sea. The tower stands white enough against it, but all round is rock and wind and the cries of birds that sound like folk arguing in the dark. The quarters are clean, if bare. Iron bed, table, little range, shelves with old tins left by the last man. I have polished the lens this morning under Mr Fraser’s instruction and learned the clockwork for the lamp. It is work I can do well. There is comfort in a thing that must be done in the right order. You need not fret. The pay is better than the yard and there is less chance of a crushed hand or a back gone wrong before forty. I will send money when I have my first month. If you are passing Mrs Donnelly, tell her I found the scarf she knitted in the bottom of my trunk. I laughed seeing it. Orange enough to guide ships itself. Your loving son, Alec --- 18 March 1978 8 Kirkgate, Greenock My dear Alec, Your letter came yesterday and I read it twice before putting the kettle on. Then I read it again with tea. A black tooth in the sea is a fine thing to call an island, though I wish you were on a softer bit of ground. I am glad the work suits you. You were always steadier with careful things than your brother, God rest him. Do not skip meals because there is no one there to ask if you’ve eaten. Men alone are bad for that. I’ve sent a parcel with oatcakes, two jars of jam, and your father’s old cardigan if the post office women have not laughed me out the door for the size of it. Mrs Donnelly says to wear the scarf and be grateful. She made a face when I told her what you wrote, but she was pleased all the same. I know why you chose the light. You do not need to explain it to me. Quiet can be a kindness after too much sorrow. Still, quiet has its own teeth, so write often. All my love, Mam --- 2 April 1978 Eilean Dubh Mam, The oatcakes arrived broken but no worse for that. The jam did not survive. Raspberry over everything, even my spare socks. The gulls had a feast from the paper at the door before I could sweep it. I have settled to the routine now. Wind measurements at six. Check the mechanism. Scrub salt from the panes. Record visibility. Wind again at noon, lamp at dusk. It is strange how quickly a man begins speaking aloud to himself when no answer is expected. I caught myself saying “there now” to the kettle as if it were a bairn. There is one thing on the island I like beyond the work. In a drawer under the chart table I found a small carved bird, no bigger than my thumb joint. It is made of whalebone, yellowed smooth with handling, and the wings are not spread but tucked close, as if against weather. The eye is a pinprick of black glass. Someone has worked tiny feather marks along the breast with a very patient knife. It sits in my pocket through the day. I do not know why that should help, but it does. Some nights the sea is all steel and order. Other nights it bangs at the rocks like a man locked out. Those are harder. Alec --- 10 April 1978 Greenock Dear son, I like the thought of your little bird. Keep it if no one comes claiming. A thing held by many hands can have company in it. Your grandfather carried a brass button in his pocket all through the war and called it his “spare nerve.” Men make their own medicine where they can. I have washed and re-knitted the cuff of your father’s cardigan before sending it, because moths had made a meal there. It still smells of his pipe if you put your nose to it. That may be comfort or nuisance depending on the day. You say the loud nights are harder. Then say more on them. You needn’t be brave by post for me. I have already seen what bravery costs and I’d rather have honesty. I was at the cemetery Sunday. I brought daffodils for your brother. The ground is greening up. You were right not to come if you could not bear it yet. There’s no shame in grief taking its own road. Love, Mam --- 1 June 1978 Eilean Dubh Mam, I did not answer at once because there was no clean way to say it. The hard nights are the fog ones. When the horn goes and goes, the sound comes through the walls and into the bones. It is like hearing something mournful from inside your own chest. Last Tuesday, after forty hours near without sleep, I thought I heard Jamie on the stair. Not his voice exactly. More the tread of him, the quick uneven way because of the old football knock to his knee. I opened the door and of course there was no one but wet stone and blowing white. I stood there too long in the cold because I could not make myself shut the door on what I knew was not there. I am ashamed writing this. You told me to say more, so I am saying it. Since the accident at the yard there are times I think if I had not asked him to swap shifts, if I had taken the crane run myself, if I had looked up sooner— I know all the answers. None of them help. Do not worry overmuch. I have not neglected the light. If anything I tend it like a man possessed. No ship will come to harm here for want of me. Alec --- 8 June 1978 Greenock Alec, my son, Listen carefully to me. You did not kill your brother. A snapped cable killed your brother. A foreman who hurried men in bad weather maybe helped. A yard that runs old gear till it breaks helped. But not you. Not because you swapped a shift. Not because you were young enough to think one day was like another. I have held my tongue because I thought time would do what words could not. I was wrong. So here it is plain: Jamie chose the overtime because he wanted the extra money for the motorcycle, foolish creature. He kissed my cheek at the door and said if he was lucky he’d have it by August. He was cheerful. He was not sacrificed. He was alive in his own life, making his own daft plans, till a bad machine ended them. You are not honouring him by punishing yourself on a rock. If hearing things grows worse, you must tell your supervisor or the doctor on the mainland. Men break from loneliness as surely as from falls. There is no disgrace in that either. I wish I could box your ears and hold your face both at once. Mam --- 27 August 1978 Eilean Dubh Mam, I have read your last letter many times. I kept it in the lamp room for a week and the paper smells faintly of paraffin now. There was an incident on the 19th. A trawler from Oban lost bearings in a gale after midnight. I saw her light where no light should be, much too near the west reef. The radio was failing in bursts. I kept the beam on and sounded the horn and, against instruction, burned a hand flare from the gallery when she vanished in the spray. I thought of Jamie then, but not in the old way. Just that I would not let another family get the knock at the door if I could help it. They made harbour by morning. Yesterday I had a telegram from their skipper: LIGHT KEPT US OFF THE TEETH STOP BLESS THE MAN ON IT STOP. I stood with that paper in my hand and cried like a fool. Maybe from relief. Maybe from being tired. Maybe because for the first time in months I did a thing and it did not end in blame. I have written requesting one shore leave week in October. If granted, I will come home and fix the loose gate as promised these three years. Your son, Alec --- 3 September 1978 Greenock My dear boy, I am smiling so hard my face aches. Let them call you foolish for crying if they like. Better tears than all that stony silence you’ve been swallowing. Come in October. The gate hangs like a drunk and the back window sticks. I have saved those jobs for you out of fairness and because I am not climbing ladders with this knee. I’ll make stew and a treacle tart. Mrs Donnelly will pretend not to notice if you wear the orange scarf to the shop. One more thing. I took out your brother’s last things from the hall cupboard last week. Among them was the little tin where he kept bolts and oddments. On top was a note in his writing, likely meant for no one: ASK ALEC ABOUT THE LIGHTHOUSE JOB. BETTER HIM THAN THIS RATTING YARD. He was thinking of you. Proud, I’d say. Bring your bird if you come. I want to see the face of the man who carries a bone seabird for courage and still calls himself ordinary. Love always, Mam --- 14 October 1978 Greenock Mam, I am writing from your own kitchen though you are in the front room asleep with the wireless low, because I wanted to put this down while the house is breathing round me. The leave has near undone me with goodness. The sound of cups in a cupboard. Your slippers on the linoleum. Rain on a street instead of naked sea. Today, while fixing the gate, I found myself whistling. I stopped because I did not recognise the man doing it. Thank you for Jamie’s note. I carried it in my pocket beside the bird all day. They made a strange pair: his rough pencil scrawl and that little pale carving, both saying in their fashion the same thing — go on. I have decided not to resign. That may surprise you after all my complaining, but hear me out. The island is lonely, yes. Some nights it opens a hole in me. But it is also plain. It asks what needs done and then lets a man do it. I think I need that for a while longer. Not to hide. To mend. There is a difference, and I am only just learning it. I have also spoken with Mr Fraser about rotating shorter spells and he agreed more readily than I expected. “No use having a cracked keeper,” he said, which is as close to kindness as the man gets. When I go back tomorrow I will take your parcel, the repaired cardigan, Jamie’s note, and the bird. I will keep the light. I will write. I will come home. That is the resolution, if not of all things then of enough. Your loving son, Alec
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills the prompt's requirements while delivering a genuinely moving story about grief, isolation, and healing. The piece demonstrates strong technical craft in several key areas. The Scottish working-class voices are authentic and distinct without relying on heavy dialect or stereotypes. Alec's voice carries the weight of his grief and isolation through understated language, while his mother's letters reveal wisdom, patience, and deep maternal love. The voice consistency is maintained throughout the months-long correspondence. The story's emotional architecture is well-constructed. The revelation of Jamie's death in the crane accident emerges naturally through the letters, avoiding melodramatic exposition. The progression from Alec's initial stoicism through his breakdown confession about hearing his brother's footsteps, to his mother's direct intervention, feels psychologically authentic. The imagery is particularly strong - "black tooth in the sea," the lighthouse as refuge and prison, the carved bone bird as talisman. These concrete details ground the emotional journey without becoming overwrought. The fog horn sequence and the rescue of the trawler provide effective dramatic beats that serve the character development. The mother's voice is especially well-crafted. Her letter breaking through Alec's guilt ("You did not kill your brother") demonstrates perfect timing and authentic maternal directness. Her revelation of Jamie's note about the lighthouse job provides genuine surprise while feeling completely earned. The resolution avoids false optimism. Alec's decision to stay at the lighthouse while arranging shorter rotations feels realistic - he's not "cured" but has found a sustainable path forward. The final letter's tone of quiet determination rather than euphoric healing rings true. Minor weaknesses include some slightly predictable beats (the rescue scene serving as turning point) and occasional moments where the prose edges toward the literary in ways that might not fully align with Alec's character voice, though these are minimal. The piece succeeds as both a character study and a meditation on grief, isolation, and the slow work of healing. It avoids sentimentality while remaining deeply humane. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 1 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t walk into a room without threat-modeling it. Not in the dramatic, movie-montage way where code scrolls over my pupils and every lock clicks open in my imagination. It’s quieter than that. More habitual. More like a second layer of subtitles on reality. Exit signs, camera placement, point-of-sale terminals, the deadbolt on the staff door with the strike plate hanging half a screw loose, the little acrylic sign by the register that says GUEST WIFI PASSWORD: penne2024 in cheerful marker as if “password” still means anything when it’s taped to the counter for all to see. I notice. I catalog. I build attack trees for fun and by accident. Reformed, they say, as if it’s a conversion and not an ongoing maintenance problem. As if my brain got a patch and no longer defaults to exploit vectors. I’m white hat now. Consulting, audits, conference talks where I say things like security is about trust boundaries and human factors and everyone nods and writes it down because it sounds wiser than I actually feel. I disclose. I document. I ask permission. I leave systems better than I found them. People are harder. Especially across a little restaurant table under an amber pendant light, where the objective is not “enumerate vulnerabilities” but “be charming” and “ask follow-up questions” and “do not, under any circumstances, compare a person to a badly configured firewall.” I got here twelve minutes early because punctuality is one of the few social protocols I can execute without packet loss. Long enough to inspect the place in the ambient way I inspect everything. The Wi‑Fi password on the counter. The QR code menu with no domain verification. The tablet at the host stand smudged with fingerprints and left unlocked while the hostess refills water. The waiter reusing the same four-digit code to log into the payment terminal because his fingers tapped it with the weary confidence of muscle memory. A whole ecosystem of trust assumptions lacquered over with rustic décor. And then Maya walks in and all of that recedes enough for me to remember I am here for a reason other than passive reconnaissance. Okay. She is prettier than her pictures, which feels unfair in my favor, like someone undersold an asset before acquisition. No, terrible. Stop. She has that competent stride people have when they know where their body is in space. Denim jacket, black dress, silver ring on her thumb. She spots me, smiles in a way that reaches her eyes, and my pulse immediately starts behaving like a service under load. Say something normal. I stand. I do not salute. Good start. “Hey,” I say, which is operating within acceptable parameters. “Hey. Sorry, parking was chaos.” “Threat model failure in urban design,” I almost say, but reroute in time to “No worries.” We sit. Menus up like little privacy screens. The waiter appears, takes our drink order, vanishes. I am trying to remember all the advice ever given to me by better-adjusted friends. Ask open-ended questions. Listen for understanding, not merely response latency. Don’t monologue. Don’t perform. Don’t mention cybercrime in the first seven minutes. Maya saves me from myself by asking what I actually do all day, and I give her the cleaned-up version. Security assessments. Resilience testing. Helping companies find the holes before someone worse does. I can hear how ominous that sounds and add, lightly, “Legally. These days.” “These days?” she says, and there is the tiniest spark in her grin. There are moments where honesty is a risk worth taking. “I was an idiot with a terminal and poor impulse control in my twenties.” “Only in your twenties?” I laugh, surprised into it. Good. Humor handshake established. It goes better from there. More than better, actually. She works in library systems, which means she is exactly the kind of person who can make metadata sound heroic. She tells a story about migrating a county archive off a catastrophically old database and I fall a little in love with the phrase catastrophically old database in her mouth. Then somehow—beautifully, improbably—we hit Magic: The Gathering. Not just “oh yeah, I played in college” Magic. Not “I know what a planeswalker is” Magic. Actual, dangerous, specific Magic. Commander salt levels. Draft archetypes. The moral legitimacy of counterspells. The fact that she once built a deeply annoying blue-black deck “as a social experiment,” which is both hilarious and a mild red flag in exactly the way I appreciate. “Okay, but answer carefully,” she says, leaning in. “Do you destroy Sol Rings on sight, or do you let people have their happiness for one turn?” “Depends,” I say. “Are we talking table politics or optimization?” Her eyes light up. “Optimization.” “Then happiness is an attack surface.” She laughs hard enough that the couple at the next table glances over. There it is, that little endorphin burst, reward circuitry lighting up because a line landed. Human connection through shared cardboard neuroses. This I can do. This I understand. Structure, rules text, mana curves. A game where everyone admits they are trying to outmaneuver each other and the social contract is explicit enough to parse. We order. Pasta for her, salmon for me. The waiter leaves. We are now in the warm middle phase of a date where possibility starts to feel less theoretical. She tells me about her cat, who is named after a fantasy novelist but behaves like a tax auditor. I tell her about the time I had to explain to a CEO why “password123!” was not transformed into a good password by the addition of punctuation. She snorts water through her nose. I apologize while secretly treasuring the moment because it means she is relaxed. This is going well. This is going so well. And then her phone buzzes. It’s faceup on the table, because of course it is. A bright rectangle of modern compulsion. She glances at the notification and says, “Sorry, my sister,” with the automatic guilt reflex of our age, and picks it up. I look away because I am not a monster. Which would be easier if my peripheral vision weren’t trained like a heat-seeking missile for exactly this class of event. She unlocks the phone with a pattern. Not just a pattern. A pattern. A simple corner-to-corner shape, visible in one smooth movement, with the kind of greasy screen residue that probably reproduces it from orbit. An L-shape with a flourish. I catch it in less than a second, not because I’m trying to steal it but because my brain flags weak authentication the way some people notice off-key singing. It is immediate and involuntary and accompanied by a full internal advisory bulletin. No no no no. Do not say anything. You are on a date, not in an awareness training seminar. She types a quick reply, sets the phone down, and keeps talking about—something about a local game store opening a new Commander night—but I am only half processing because a whole section of my cortex is vibrating at the insecurity of what I just saw. Pattern unlocks are bad enough. Simple visible pattern unlocks are practically a consent form for shoulder surfing. Combined with the smudge trail? Combined with the fact that her phone has probably everything on it? Banking, email, password reset vectors, social graph, two-factor intercept— Do not say anything. What I should do is let it go. This is what normal people do. They do not turn every intimate interaction into an impromptu penetration test. They do not confuse concern with compulsion. They certainly do not interrupt a flirtatious debate about whether Cyclonic Rift should be a bannable offense to critique someone’s endpoint security hygiene. I open my mouth. “That unlock pattern is really guessable.” There is a silence so sudden and complete it feels like the restaurant has lost power. I actually hear the clink of silverware from two tables over. Maya blinks at me. Once. Twice. “I’m sorry,” she says, in a tone of voice people use when they’re giving you a chance to reverse into a less stupid statement. Abort. Roll back. Patch now. “I just—” No, that’s already bad. “I noticed—” Worse. Catastrophic. “Occupational hazard,” I say, which is true and also sounds deranged. “I wasn’t trying to look.” Her face changes. Not dramatically. If I weren’t calibrated to microexpressions I might miss it. A slight withdrawal around the eyes. Her mouth flattening by a degree. Not anger, exactly. Alarm. Recalculation. She is rerunning the model of me with new data and the confidence score is dropping. “You noticed my phone password,” she says. “Pattern,” I say automatically, because apparently I am committed to dying on technical distinctions. Excellent. Bury yourself with precision. She leans back. “That’s… kind of weird.” Correct. Accurate assessment. Vulnerability confirmed. I can feel multiple response strategies presenting themselves, all flawed. Minimize. Joke. Explain. Apologize cleanly. Double down into educational concern. Pretend to receive an urgent text and launch myself bodily through the taped Wi‑Fi sign. The waiter appears at exactly the wrong moment with our food, setting plates down into the blast radius. “Can I get you anything else?” A new identity, I think. “We’re good,” Maya says, not looking at him. When he leaves, I choose honesty because it is the only tool left that isn’t also an exploit. “You’re right,” I say. “That was weird. I’m sorry. My brain catches stuff like that before I can stop it, and sometimes I mistake noticing for needing to comment. That’s not fair to you.” She studies me. I force myself not to fill the silence. Silence is where people authenticate. “I get noticing,” she says finally. “I don’t get saying it out loud on a first date.” “Neither do I, apparently.” That earns the smallest exhale through her nose. Not quite a laugh. But maybe not a total shutdown either. I continue, carefully. “For what it’s worth, I wasn’t trying to show off or make you feel stupid. It’s more like… if someone came in with their backpack unzipped and their wallet sticking out, and you spent your whole life noticing unsecured bags, your brain would scream. Mine just has terrible timing and worse social skills.” “And you think my phone is an unzipped backpack.” “I think modern phones are entire lives with glass screens.” She cuts a piece of pasta, thinking. “That was almost smooth.” “Accidental. I don’t recommend relying on it.” There. A real smile, brief but extant. System partially recovered. I let her eat before I say anything else. Important lesson from incident response: stop making changes during active instability. Inside, though, I am still triaging. Did I torpedo this? Maybe. Maybe not. There is a version of tonight where this becomes a story she tells friends—funny, exasperated, weirdly endearing. There is another where I become “that cybersecurity guy who clocked my unlock pattern over appetizers,” and honestly I would deserve it. She twirls pasta, then says, “So if my phone security is terrible, what’s the damage?” The worst possible thing for me is an opening. I take a breath. “Do you want the answer as a person who’s trying not to be insufferable, or as a person whose entire job is evaluating worst-case scenarios?” She points her fork at me. “See, this is your problem. You think those are different people.” That lands cleanly because it is true. I smile despite myself. “Fair.” She sets her fork down. “One sentence. Maximum.” I can work with constraints. Constraints are kindness. “Use a six-digit PIN or a better alphanumeric password, because visible patterns are easy to shoulder-surf and easier to reconstruct from screen smudges.” “Two sentences.” “Semicolon.” She laughs. Genuine this time. The knot in my chest loosens by a measurable percentage. “Okay,” she says. “I’ll change it. But if you quiz me on two-factor authentication before dessert, I’m leaving.” “I would never,” I say, while mentally deleting at least four pending observations about the restaurant’s payment flow. For the next ten minutes I behave. I ask about her favorite set. She says Innistrad, which is objectively defensible. I confess a weakness for artifact synergies and she accuses me, rightly, of being the sort of person who says the phrase value engine without irony. We recover the thread. Maybe not fully, maybe with a visible patch, but functional. Still, some tiny malicious goblin in the back of my head wonders if this is what sabotage looks like when you’re trying to quit it. Not attacking systems anymore, so instead you stress-test moments. Probe boundaries. Introduce failure into anything that starts to matter, just to prove you can predict the collapse. There’s a comfort in broken things. They make sense. They can be mapped. Connection is the real zero-day. No documentation. No guaranteed rollback. Every disclosure a gamble. When dessert menus arrive, Maya glances at the counter and says, “By the way, their Wi‑Fi password is literally taped up over there. Does that upset you too?” I follow her gaze to the cheerful PENNE2024 sign. She caught it. Of course she caught me catching things. “It does,” I admit. She smiles over the candle between us. “You don’t have to tell them.” I look at her, at the amused challenge in it, at the possibility she’s offering me: notice, and choose. For once, maybe that’s the harder trick. Maybe that’s the whole reform. Not losing the instinct, but deciding where it ends. So I leave the sign on the counter. I leave the host stand tablet alone. I leave her future phone settings to her. I cut into tiramisu and ask what commander she’d build if budget weren’t a constraint, and when she answers without hesitation, I listen like it’s the only signal in the room worth capturing.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging narrative premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout - a reformed hacker whose technical mindset creates a compelling lens through which to view both digital and social interactions. The internal monologue feels authentic and consistent, blending cybersecurity terminology with genuine emotional vulnerability. The characterization is particularly strong. The protagonist emerges as genuinely three-dimensional - someone whose professional expertise has become both a superpower and a social liability. Their self-awareness about their own awkwardness ("I am not a monster" followed immediately by detailed observation of the security flaw) creates effective dramatic irony. Maya is also well-developed despite limited direct dialogue, coming across as intelligent, patient but not infinitely so, and genuinely interested in connecting. The technical elements are handled with impressive authenticity without becoming inaccessible to general readers. The author clearly understands both cybersecurity concepts and Magic: The Gathering culture, using both to create believable shared ground between characters. The hacker mindset is portrayed realistically - not as Hollywood dramatics but as a habitual way of processing information. The central conflict - the protagonist's inability to suppress their professional observations during an intimate moment - feels organic and well-executed. The aftermath is handled with emotional sophistication, showing both characters navigating the awkwardness in believable ways. The resolution doesn't feel forced or overly optimistic; instead, it suggests cautious progress. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with particularly strong metaphorical language that maintains consistency with the protagonist's technical background ("pulse behaving like a service under load," "silence is where people authenticate"). The pacing is well-managed, building tension around the central incident and then working through its consequences. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors feel slightly forced, and the ending, while earned, pushes toward optimism in a way that feels just slightly convenient. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
When Mr. Garza says, “Okay, lab groups of two,” the whole room erupts into that scraping-chair, half-yelling chaos that always makes my skin go hot. Everyone starts swiveling around and calling names and acting like this is normal and easy, like it doesn’t require an advanced strategy and three backup plans. I’m already doing the usual thing where I pretend to look calm while my brain begins sprinting in seven directions at once. Then Mr. Garza looks at the paper in his hand and says, “Maya, you’re with Eli.” I have exactly one second to react, which is one second too few. Eli. My face probably does something humiliating and visible, like widening or freezing or becoming a human stop sign. I hope not. Maybe I’ve trained myself out of obvious reactions by now. Maybe years of trying not to look weird have finally produced at least one useful skill. He glances over at me and smiles, quick and easy, like this is fine, like I am fine, like pairing with me is not some kind of social test I’m definitely going to fail. “Cool,” he says. Cool, I think back, because apparently my internal voice is a person who can answer normally. What comes out of my mouth is: “Uh—yeah.” Amazing. Incredible. A truly memorable opening. “Uh—yeah,” said in the tone of someone who has just been informed of a minor plumbing issue. I gather my notebook and pencil and move to the lab table trying to walk in a way that suggests I have walked before. Not too fast, because then I’ll look overeager. Not too slow, because then I’ll seem reluctant, which is worse, because what if he thinks I don’t want to work with him? But if I smile too much, that’s weird. Neutral pleasant. Friendly but not intense. Casual shoulders. Is there such a thing as casual shoulders or did I just invent a new thing to fail at? He sits across from me and unfolds the assignment sheet. Photosynthesis and environmental variables. We have to design a mini experiment with aquatic plants and light exposure and then present our results next week. This is actually the kind of thing I’m good at. This should be the part where I become a person who can function. I should say something first. People who are socially normal say something first. Option one: Hi. No, too late for hi. We have already crossed the threshold where hi would be natural. If I say hi now it’ll sound like my brain rebooted. Option two: So, do you want to read through it or should I? Too controlling? No, not controlling. Efficient. But also maybe weirdly formal, like I’m a substitute teacher. Option three: I think this one looks kind of interesting. Too vague. What one? We are both literally looking at the same paper and yet somehow I’d still find a way to be unclear. Option four: Have you ever done this lab before? No, because that sounds like I think he got held back, which is not only rude but insane, and now I can never say any sentence with the phrase “ever done this before” again. I am still deciding among the least bad openings when he says, “Do you want to test different light colors? That might be easier to measure.” “Yes,” I say immediately, too quickly, with enough force to suggest I have been held hostage by my own silence and finally released. He doesn’t seem to notice. “Like maybe white light versus blue and red?” I nod. Then I realize nodding without words is not ideal because it can come off as me being blank or uninterested or incapable of speech, all of which are, unfortunately, partially true at the moment. “That makes sense,” I add, aiming for intelligent and calm. Good. Fine. Actual words. Maybe this will be okay. My internal voice, meanwhile, is giving a TED Talk. It would like to note that blue and red wavelengths are more strongly associated with photosynthetic activity, and that if we keep all other variables controlled, the data could be fairly clean. It would also like to make one casual joke, something light and not overdone, maybe about our plant becoming a tiny celebrity under stage lighting. The internal version of me is witty but restrained, informed without showing off, maybe even charming in a quiet way. External me is trying not to hold the pencil like it’s a distress flare. He says, “Do you want to write, or should I?” This is dangerous. Too much depends on the answer. If I say I’ll write, then my handwriting will be visible, and what if it suddenly looks childish today for no reason? What if my hand shakes? But if I let him write, then I look passive, like I’m just sitting here contributing oxygen. Also if he writes, I’ll have to look at him more, which is a whole separate issue because eye contact has to be measured in exact fractions of a second or else I become either rude or creepy. “I can write,” I say. Not bad. Simple. Useful. “Okay,” he says, sliding the sheet toward me. I write our names at the top. His first, then mine, because that feels less self-centered, although now I’m worried that was too noticeable, like maybe only someone who had spent weird amounts of time thinking about him would consciously decide name order. Maybe normal people also think about that. Maybe no one has ever thought about that except me. Fantastic. He leans in a little and points to the procedure section. “We should probably decide how long each plant gets exposed.” He smells like laundry detergent, faintly, except underneath that there’s still this sharp ammonia scent hanging in the room from whenever the custodians cleaned up after last period. It keeps sneaking into my nose at the worst times, slicing right through my thoughts. Ammonia always smells like something too clean, if that makes sense, like cleanliness with an attitude problem. Every time I start constructing a sentence in my head, the smell barges in and wipes part of it out. Maybe thirty minutes, I think. No, say it aloud. “Maybe thirty minutes?” I manage. “Yeah, that should work.” He says it so easily. He says things and they just arrive in the air properly formed. He doesn’t seem to inspect them first from twelve angles. He doesn’t appear to think, If I suggest thirty minutes, will that sound too eager? Too arbitrary? What if he already thought thirty and now it sounds like I’m trying to impress him by agreeing first? What if thirty is wrong and I’ve revealed myself as secretly incompetent in both science and being alive? I know this is irrational. That’s one of the worst parts. I know, intellectually, that no one is analyzing me this hard because most people are busy being themselves. I know if someone else said “Maybe thirty minutes,” I would think, yes, normal sentence, thank you. But when it’s me, every word feels like it has to pass through customs. He taps the paper with the eraser end of his pencil. “Do you want to be the one who talks during the presentation, or do you want me to?” Absolutely not. Also, if I say absolutely not too quickly, then I’ll sound dramatic and incompetent and like a terrible partner. If I say “I can do it,” then I will spend the next week sick with dread and maybe die in front of the class. There must be a middle option. There is always a middle option. Find the middle option. “We could both do part of it,” I say. That is, annoyingly, a very good answer. Balanced. Cooperative. I should be proud of that, but pride is hard to access when I’m already replaying the sentence to check if my voice sounded weird on the word both. “Sure,” he says. “I’m fine with that.” Of course he is. Because he is one of those people who can just be fine with things. I write down our variables carefully, making my handwriting as neat as possible without making it obvious that I’m trying to make my handwriting neat. There’s a narrow band of acceptable effort. Too sloppy and I look careless. Too neat and I look like I care too much, which is somehow also embarrassing. Existing as a person in public is basically a nonstop calibration problem. Say something casual, I tell myself. This is your chance. Not about the project. Something normal classmates say when they are not malfunctioning. I could ask if he watched the game last night, except I did not watch the game last night and if he says something back I’ll be trapped in a conversation built on ignorance. I could mention the math quiz, but that sounds like I’m trying to be school-only interesting, which is not interesting. I could say I liked his presentation from history last week. No. No, that sounds like I have been paying specific attention to him, which I have, but the point is not to reveal that like some kind of deranged documentary narrator. I could make a joke about our plant. The tiny celebrity under stage lighting. That was good. That was actually funny in a modest, intelligent way. I open my mouth. Nothing. Not exactly nothing. More like traffic. Too many possible tones, too many outcomes. What if he laughs because it’s funny? Good, obviously. But then what if I get too pleased too fast and my face does something enormous? What if he doesn’t laugh and there’s that tiny pause where a joke dies and both people have to stand over the body pretending not to notice? What if it comes out sounding rehearsed, which it is? So instead I say, “Do you think we need three trials?” He answers right away, “Probably, yeah, just to make the data better.” And there it is again, the little stab of disappointment that comes from choosing safe over real. Safe keeps me from embarrassment, but it also keeps me from being known. Which sounds way too dramatic for lab class, except it’s true. Every time I take the safest possible sentence, I leave the better one trapped in my head, and then people meet this edited version of me made almost entirely of caution. He smiles suddenly, looking at the row of colored plastic filters in the supply bin. “If we use the red one, our plant is going to look evil.” I laugh before I can evaluate it, which is probably the most honest thing I’ve done all hour. “Like it has a secret plan,” I say. There. That one made it out. He grins. “Exactly.” And for one whole second I forget to monitor my face. I forget my hands and my posture and whether my voice was the right volume. I’m just there, at the table, with the ammonia smell fading into the background and the paper between us and a stupid joke about an evil plant hanging in the air, and it feels weirdly simple. Then of course I ruin it by thinking about it. Did I laugh too loud? Was “secret plan” too much? Should I have stopped at “yeah”? Was I leaning forward weirdly? Do I look too happy now? Fix your face. Not too much. Relax your shoulders. Casual shoulders. There it is again, the impossible assignment. But still. He’s writing down materials now, and I’m filling in the hypothesis, and every so often one of us says something and the other answers, and even though each sentence still has to drag itself past my internal security system, they are at least happening. Not the perfect ones. Not the ones from inside my head, polished and effortless and bright. Just the real ones, awkward and ordinary and enough to keep the conversation alive. Maybe that counts for something. Maybe overthinking everything doesn’t actually save me from embarrassment as much as it just guarantees I experience it in advance, over and over, like a preview nobody asked for. I write, If aquatic plants are exposed to different light colors, then plants under blue and red light will produce more oxygen bubbles than plants under white light, and Eli says, “That sounds good,” and I say, “Okay,” except this time okay comes out like an actual word a normal person might say. Which, for today, feels almost enormous.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of adolescent social anxiety and delivers an authentic first-person narrative that successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of an overthinking teenager. The writer excels at creating a believable internal voice that contrasts sharply with the protagonist's limited external expression, fulfilling the core prompt requirements effectively. The characterization is particularly strong. Maya feels genuinely like an intelligent 8th-grader - her concerns are age-appropriate (handwriting neatness, name order on papers) while her internal voice shows real intellectual depth. The gap between her articulate thoughts and halting speech is convincingly portrayed through specific examples like her elaborate internal TED talk about photosynthesis versus her simple "That makes sense" response. The prose style effectively mirrors the protagonist's mental state through run-on sentences, rapid topic shifts, and cascading anxieties. Phrases like "my brain begins sprinting in seven directions at once" and the extended metaphor of words passing through "customs" demonstrate creative language use without becoming overwrought. The writing maintains consistent energy and voice throughout. The dialogue, while minimal, serves its purpose well. Eli's casual responses ("Cool," "Sure") effectively contrast with Maya's internal complexity, and their brief exchange about the "evil plant" provides a genuine moment of connection that feels earned rather than forced. The piece shows strong adherence to instructions, maintaining almost entirely internal monologue while incorporating the science project setting naturally. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Maya experiences a small moment of authentic interaction without any dramatic transformation of her anxiety, which would have felt unearned. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive phrasing around the anxiety themes and a few moments where the internal voice becomes slightly too sophisticated for the age, though these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls like purple prose or melodrama while maintaining genuine emotional engagement throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
