+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
Morning began in the dark, before Rome had fully decided to be awake. In the ludus the air was always thick at that hour, stale with old sweat soaked into straw, lamp smoke, leather left damp from the day before. Men breathed around me in uneven measure—snorts, coughs, one soft whimper from a dream turned bad. Somewhere beyond our barred windows a mule brayed, and farther still I heard the city beginning itself: cart wheels striking stone, a woman calling for bread, the bark of a dog answered by another across the street as if all Rome passed messages before dawn. I lay for a little while on my back, looking up at the beams blackened by years of smoke, and touched the small bone charm I kept on a cord beneath my tunic. A wolf, crudely carved. Not Mars, though most men in the school favored him, nor Hercules, whose strength every fool wanted to borrow. Mine was for Diana, lady of the hunt, swift through forests no city tax collector could measure, cold-eyed and free. I had never seen a forest like the poets described. I was born among olive trees and dust in Samnium. Still, when I thought of freedom, I thought of her. “Up,” came the lanista’s steward, striking the bars with a stick. “Unless you’d rather starve.” That woke the dead. We rose with the grumbling obedience of chained dogs. The floor bit with cold through my soles. As I stood, my scarred shoulder gave its usual morning protest, and I rolled it until the joint settled. Beside me, Hilarus scratched at his beard and spat into the rushes. “You smiled in your sleep,” he muttered. “I was dreaming of your mother.” He gave a tired laugh. Such jokes were old and poor, but old and poor things often lasted longest in Rome. In the yard the sky had gone from black to iron-gray. The training posts stood in rows, dark and patient, smelling of split wood and oil. The cookhouse sent out steam that carried the scent of barley porridge, onions, and the sharp comfort of vinegar. We ate from rough bowls with wooden spoons, standing because benches slowed a man’s morning. Barley stuck to my teeth. A fly, stupid from the chill, crawled on the rim of my bowl. Across the yard the new men huddled together, still shaped by whatever lives they had worn before this one. One had the cropped hair of a soldier. Another’s ears were pierced in the eastern fashion. They looked at us older fighters as provincials looked at the Forum for the first time—with awe, envy, and the first stirrings of disgust. They had not yet learned that fame had an odor. It smelled like liniment, old blood washed imperfectly from sandaled feet, wool drying too slowly, and men packed too close under a patron’s profit. After breakfast we trained. Not to kill—that was for the crowd’s imagination—but to move as if death were a rhythm one could learn. The doctore paced among us with his staff, correcting stance, cursing lineage, praising no one. My shield arm burned. Sweat ran under my tunic though the day remained cool. Each blow struck the post with a dull wooden thud, and all around me the yard answered in a hundred different beats: crack, grunt, scrape, breath. There was a strange peace in it sometimes. A man narrowed to body and task could forget the rest of the world until the world insisted on being remembered. Rome insisted loudly. By midmorning the street outside had swollen with traffic, and from a high slit in the wall I could see a stripe of life passing. Senators’ litters with their curtains drawn against dust. Beggars lifting scarred hands. Boys driving goats. A painted prostitute in saffron stopping to haggle with a fish seller, as fierce over coins as any general over a frontier. News moved along with them all. News always did. A baker’s apprentice shouted that grain ships had been delayed. Someone cursed the tax collectors. Someone praised the emperor. Someone else did both in the same breath, which was perhaps the truest Roman prayer. At the water trough Hilarus leaned near me and lowered his voice. “There’ll be games next month in honor of a dedication on the Palatine. Bigger than usual.” “Bigger means cheaper seats and louder poor men,” I said. “And richer magistrates smiling with the emperor’s profile stamped on their gifts.” He dipped his cup and drank. Water ran through his beard. “They say he wants the people happy. There’s talk of unrest in the provinces again. Judea, perhaps. Or Dacia. There is always somewhere Rome is teaching obedience.” I said nothing. I had once been one of the places Rome taught. That was the thing no one in the seats cared to remember: the empire was built first in places without marble. In villages where men knew one another’s fathers. In fields measured by weather, not by law. Then came tax, census, soldiers, roads straight as judgment, and the language of command. Some prospered under it, and many boasted they did. Roads made trade; aqueducts made fountains leap in city squares; peace, of a sort, let merchants sleep easier. But peace in Rome often meant someone else had laid down his name. The training ended at last. We were rubbed down with oil so cheap it smelled half rancid, then scraped clean with strigils. Gray rolls of sweat and dust curled off my skin and fell at my feet. In the bath chamber steam clouded the walls. Men talked more freely there, as if nakedness made us honest. One complained of a creditor who had sold himself to avoid prison and now regretted the bargain. Another boasted that a noblewoman had sent him figs with a ribbon tied around the basket. Hilarus said he preferred figs without politics attached. I sat on the stone ledge and let heat loosen me. Above us, someone had painted Neptune in faded blues, his trident raised over dolphins. The plaster had cracked through one eye. I thought again of Diana, not because she belonged in baths or barracks, but because I had seen a little shrine to her once beyond the city, where cypress trees held the afternoon still and women had hung ribbons from the branches. That stillness came back to me whenever the noise of Rome pressed too hard. In the afternoon our owner received visitors. This happened whenever games approached. Men with rings and scented beards came to inspect us the way cooks inspected eels. They spoke of pairings, wagers, crowd taste. One wanted “a Thracian with a story” because the people liked bravery if it came in an accent. Another asked whether my old shoulder would hold for another season. I was present only as horseflesh is present while men discuss its gait. “Good scars,” one said of me. “An intelligent face,” said another, meaning perhaps that I looked tragic enough to die well. I kept my eyes lowered. Rage was expensive, and I had little coin. When they had gone, the yard felt fouled in a different way. Even the sun seemed meaner, glaring off the whitewashed wall. I sat near the shade line and repaired a strap on my greave. From beyond the school came the smell of hot garum from a cookshop, horse dung baking in the street, fresh bread from somewhere richer than ours, and the Tiber’s faint muddy rot beneath it all. Rome never smelled of one thing. It was always many lives packed together until they fermented. A child’s laughter rang outside the gate. I looked up despite myself. Through the crack as it opened for a delivery, I glimpsed a little girl riding on her father’s shoulders, her fingers sticky with purple from crushed mulberries. She stared at me with frank interest, not fear. To her I was not a man or a slave or an enemy made useful. I was simply one of the famous creatures of the city, like the bronze horses or the elephants in triumphal processions. I wondered what stories she had been told. That we were heroes? Criminals? Brave volunteers seeking glory? Rome liked all its lies polished. Toward evening the light softened to gold. The noise of the city changed key. Vendors called the last of their wares. Temple bells sounded faintly. Smoke from cook fires drifted above the roofs in blue veils. We were given bread, beans, and a little cheese. I ate slowly, sitting against the wall while swallows turned over the yard. For a little while no one spoke. Silence among men like us was rarely empty. It was crowded with absences. I took out my wolf charm and rubbed it with my thumb until the edges warmed. I thought of Diana walking beyond torchlight, untouched by applause, owing nothing to any crowd. I thought of the emperor in his palace, of senators flattering him, of magistrates buying love with spectacles, of citizens demanding bread and blood as if either could fill them for long. All of Rome seemed built upon hunger—hunger for power, for safety, for land, for cheering, for one more day. Mine was simpler. I wanted to remain myself in a place devoted to turning men into symbols. Night gathered. The barred windows darkened one by one. In the cell Hilarus settled onto his pallet with a groan and said, “If Diana favors hunters, perhaps she also favors the hunted.” I smiled into the gloom. “Then she has had ample chance to know me.” Outside, Rome kept on with its feasting, its praying, its plotting. Somewhere a senator dined from silver. Somewhere a thief slipped through an alley. Somewhere at a household shrine a woman touched flame to incense and asked the gods to guard her son. In our room the straw rustled, a man muttered in sleep before sleep had fully claimed him, and the smell of oil lamps thickened as the wicks burned low. I lay down and closed my hand around the little wolf. The city murmured beyond the walls like a sea I would never cross. Tomorrow would bring the same stick on the bars, the same porridge, the same blows struck at wood in rehearsal for fate. Yet the day had passed, and I with it. In Rome, that counted as a kind of victory.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric writing, successfully creating an immersive portrayal of gladiatorial life in ancient Rome. The author adheres closely to the prompt requirements, delivering a first-person, past-tense narrative of approximately 1000 words that avoids combat while exploring the protagonist's inner life and the broader social context. The characterization is particularly well-executed. The unnamed gladiator emerges as a complex figure - neither romanticized hero nor simple victim, but a man maintaining his humanity within dehumanizing circumstances. His devotion to Diana rather than the expected Mars or Hercules reveals thoughtful character development, as does his Samnite background, which adds historical authenticity and personal stakes to Rome's imperial expansion. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative sensory details that ground the reader in the ancient world. Descriptions like "air was always thick at that hour, stale with old sweat soaked into straw, lamp smoke, leather left damp" create vivid atmosphere without becoming overwrought. The author demonstrates restraint, allowing details to accumulate naturally rather than forcing poetic language. The piece successfully integrates larger historical themes - imperial expansion, social stratification, the politics of entertainment - without heavy-handed exposition. The gladiator's reflection that "peace in Rome often meant someone else had laid down his name" elegantly captures the empire's contradictions. The visiting patrons who evaluate fighters "the way cooks inspected eels" effectively illustrates the dehumanizing aspects of the gladiatorial system. The narrative structure works well as a "slice of life," moving naturally from dawn to dusk while maintaining reader engagement. The pacing feels authentic to the rhythms of institutional life. Minor characters like Hilarus provide believable companionship and exposition without feeling contrived. Some weaknesses emerge in the dialogue, which occasionally feels slightly stilted or overly literary for casual conversation between gladiators. The philosophical reflections, while generally well-integrated, sometimes border on being too articulate for the character's established background, though this remains within acceptable bounds. The ending strikes an appropriate tone - neither falsely optimistic nor gratuitously dark. The protagonist's small victory of surviving another day feels earned and authentic to his circumstances. The final image of him holding his wolf charm while Rome "murmured beyond the walls like a sea I would never cross" provides effective closure while maintaining the sense of ongoing struggle. The piece succeeds in creating emotional engagement through understated moments - the child's innocent stare, the memory of Diana's shrine, the quiet companionship among the gladiators. These touches humanize the protagonist without sentimentality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 6.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 87.0
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**14 January 1978** North Crovie Light Station Banffshire Dear Mam, I am here safe. The keeper before me left the place swept but smelling of paraffin and damp socks. It’s a white tower on black rock, with the little house hunkered beside it like it’s ashamed of itself. At low tide you can see the skerries stretching out like broken teeth, and inland there’s the long back of **Bennachie** on a clear morning, though mostly I’ve had haar and gulls. The boatman that brought the stores said, “You’ll speak to yourself in a fortnight,” and laughed as if it was a kindness. My duties are straightforward enough. Trim the wick, polish the lens, note the weather, wind the mechanism, keep the fog signal in order. There’s decency in work that tells you exactly what wants done. Don’t let Mairi tell folk I took this post because I’d nowhere else to go. I took it because it’s wages, and because after the yard shut there was no point standing about Macduff pretending ships build themselves. How’s your chest? Keep the scarf on indoors if the fire’s poor. Your son, Alec --- **20 January 1978** 47 Union Road Buckie Dear Alec, Your letter came with fish scales on it, which means Tam the postman had it in his pocket with somebody’s haddock order. We are all very grand here. My chest is my chest. If I die, I’ll let you know. Mairi says no such thing, only that you’ve always had a talent for choosing the loneliest seat in any room. It will suit the lighthouse, then. I am glad of the wages. I am more glad you are not lying in bed till noon with that black face on you. Work is a rope. Take hold of it. I sent a parcel with socks, a tin of tablet, and your father’s old blue pullover. Before you say it, yes, it still has the elbow patches. Things do not stop being useful because they are ugly. Write properly next time and tell me if you are eating enough. Mam --- **3 February 1978** North Crovie Light Station Mam, I am eating enough. More than enough if it’s tinned stew and my own baking. I made bannocks fit for paving, but they fill the gap. You asked for proper writing, so here it is. The days have a shape now. I rise before light, though of course the light is what I tend, and that’s a joke fit for an old man. I sweep soot, scrub salt from the windows, and carry up oil till my shoulders sing. If I stand at the lantern after dusk and look seaward, there is nothing to tell me where the water stops and sky starts. The beam goes round and round as if searching for somebody sensible enough to stay ashore. There’s a cave under the cliff at the north side. The men call it the Piper’s Mouth. When the swell’s from the east the sound comes up through the rock like somebody trying to tune a fiddle with wet hands. It’s queer but not unpleasant. I’ve not spoken aloud much. The first week I was glad of the quiet. The second week I found myself saying “there now” when I set the kettle down, as if there was another body in the room. Don’t worry. I know the difference between quiet and trouble. Alec --- **11 February 1978** Buckie Dear Alec, The difference between quiet and trouble is that trouble answers back. Your father used to talk to his tools. Men alone always find a way of making company. Better the kettle than the bottle. I enclose a clipping from the *Press and Journal* where they are begging for men at Peterhead works. Not to push you from your post, only to remind you the world is still there if ever you want back into it. Mrs. Findlay asked if the light can be seen from Aberdeen and I told her only by saints and drunks. Eat the tablet before it goes soft. Mam --- **28 March 1978** North Crovie Light Station Mam, You can tell Mrs. Findlay the light can be seen exactly as far as weather and the curvature of the earth permit, which I know because there is a handbook and because she annoys me from twenty miles away. It’s been rough. Three nights of gale. The spray came over the lower rocks and struck the windows hard enough to make me duck, though I knew full well glass was between us. One pane in the store shed went. I’ve boarded it till the next supply. I had a foolish turn on Sunday. I woke from a sleep and couldn’t at first mind where I was. The room was shaking with wind and I thought I was back at the yard the day the crane failed, with metal screaming and men running. It took me longer than I care to admit to settle myself. That’s the first I’ve written of it. You’ll say I should have sooner. Maybe. I know you think Da died years before he was buried. Some men shrink in on themselves. I have been watching for signs of that in me, and there are hours I fear I’ve his making. Then there are other hours. Yesterday after the storm, the sea was green as bottle glass and three gannets came down arrow-straight beyond the stack. On the headland a fox stood as if painted there. I thought: if a man must be alone, let it be in a place that at least tells the truth. I don’t know if that sounds mad. Alec --- **5 April 1978** Buckie Dear son, It sounds tired, not mad. Your father had sadness in him but he was not the inventor of all your troubles. Stop trying to inherit every ill from the dead as if it were a bit of furniture. You never wrote of the crane because you thought if you did then the noise of it would come into the room. But writing is not inviting. It is airing. Damp things rot in the dark. I will tell you something I have not told you because mothers are idiots for pretending they sprang from the sea fully made. When I was seventeen and in service at Cullen House, I was sent to sleep in an attic room while the family was away. The wind off the Moray Firth got under the slates and moaned all night. I cried every evening for a week from pure loneliness and shame of being a girl away from home. Then one morning I was too hungry to be tragic and I got up and put my feet in my boots and carried on. There is no glory in endurance. It is mostly breakfast after misery. So: breakfast after misery. Write as often as you need. Mam --- **16 June 1978** North Crovie Light Station Mam, Summer makes liars of lonely places. Everything looks manageable in the long evenings. I have had company of a sort. There’s a schoolteacher from Gardenstown, Miss Fraser, who comes out with the relief boat when there are papers for me or stores to count. Freckled, no nonsense, laughs from the chest. She brought me two books because the station library consists of tide tables, a Bible with mould at the edges, and a detective novel missing the last chapter. Before you sharpen your pen, I know exactly what you are thinking and you can keep it to yourself for once. We stood by the rail and looked toward Troup Head. She said she envied me the view. I told her she was welcome to it for a fortnight and I’d take her classroom and all the bairns in exchange. She said, “At least your lamp can be lit by method,” which is maybe the finest thing anyone’s said to me this year. I am sleeping better. Alec --- **24 June 1978** Buckie Dear Alec, I am doing no such thing with my pen. Though if a freckled schoolteacher with books appears in a story, mothers are allowed a small amount of interest. You sound more yourself. There is your ordinary grumble in the letter and I have missed it. Mairi’s David has taken a job on the buses and now thinks himself king of Elgin because he wears a tie. Your uncle Rab has trapped his thumb in the mincer again. Life proceeds by the usual dignified stages. If Miss Fraser returns, offer her tea in a clean cup. Mam --- **2 November 1979** North Crovie Light Station Mam, I have put off this letter three days because I wanted to write after the feeling passed, but it has not. There was a boat in trouble east of the skerries during the squall on Monday night. Not a big vessel, just a local creel boat, but she’d lost power and was driving wrong. I saw her lamp when it was half-swallowed by spray. I sounded signal, set rockets, and got word through when I could, but the sea had first claim. The rescue got two men. The third was young Ewan Buchan from Macduff. Nineteen. I did all that was to be done. The superintendent said so by wireless, twice. Yet I stood in the lantern after and thought, if the lens had been cleaner, if I’d seen them six minutes earlier, if I’d prayed, if I were another kind of man. I know this arithmetic. There is no end to it and no answer that satisfies. Miss Fraser wrote a kind note. I have not replied. The thought of another person’s decency is more than I can carry just now. I am tired clear through. Alec --- **8 November 1979** Buckie My dear boy, Listen carefully. A lighthouse is not God. It cannot command weather, sea, engines, youth, or bad luck. It can only shine. You shone. If a boy is dead, you do not honour him by punishing yourself for not being the shore, the lifeboat, the calm, and Providence all in one body. You will answer the schoolteacher because kindness refused turns sour in the giver and the receiver both. I have enclosed the minister’s card, not because I think ministers mend everything, but because talking to somebody with no family stake in your silences may help. If you throw it in the fire, at least do it after reading. And if this post is costing more than it pays, then come home. There is no disgrace. I would rather have you in my spare room snoring like a sawmill than brave and broken on a rock. Mam --- **17 November 1979** North Crovie Light Station Mam, I went to see the minister on shore leave and found I disliked his carpet less than expected. He said very little and that was useful. I think I wanted permission not to be all made of iron. I also wrote back to Miss Fraser. Then I wrote again. We have discovered that letters are easier than speech for certain matters. She says children can smell pretence and that adults only pretend they cannot. She asked once, not unkindly, whether I took the post to be solitary or to be punished. I did not answer at once because it hit close. Maybe both. At the yard, after the crane, everybody was kind in the wrong way. You know the sort: soft voices, hands on shoulder, not saying Jamie’s name because they thought I’d break. I was the one nearest him. I saw his boot after. I had no room left in me for their carefulness. Out here the weather does not lower its voice. But a man cannot live forever by choosing only what does not ask anything back. I have put in for transfer to shore duty when a place opens. Maintenance, maybe. There’s talk of a vacancy at Kinnaird Head. Near enough to sea for work, near enough to town for ordinary life. This is not surrender. I want you to know that. Alec --- **26 November 1979** Buckie Dear Alec, Who said a change of post was surrender? Only fools think staying put is the same as strength. Kinnaird Head would suit. You could come Sundays and mend the gate you’ve ignored for two years. I am proud of you, which I do not say often because I dislike cheapening words by spending them like pennies. But there it is. As for Miss Fraser, invite her for New Year if matters continue in this improving fashion. I promise to wash the good cups. Mam --- **4 April 1980** Kinnaird Head Lighthouse Cottages Fraserburgh Dear Mam, This will reach you before I do, because I am coming on Saturday and bringing a guest if the buses and her courage both hold. My transfer came through in February. The work is different here—more repairs, inspections, less of that endless circling alone with one’s thoughts. I miss North Crovie at odd times: the black rock after rain, the cry from Piper’s Mouth, the way dawn used to pull itself over Bennachie like a blanket being dragged by cold hands. But I do not miss becoming smaller there. Her name is Isla Fraser, though by now you have likely guessed all but the baptism lines. She says she will not be interrogated like a criminal and I said that is optimistic where you are concerned. One more thing. Last week I went back out to North Crovie with the relief crew to help close a fault in the old mechanism. I stood in the lantern room alone for a minute before we left. I thought I might feel accused by it, or grateful, or sentimental. Mostly I felt this: it kept me for a time when I had nowhere else to put myself. Then it let me go. I think that is enough from one place and one life both. Put the kettle on for four, not two. Your loving son, Alec
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity. The author successfully creates two distinct voices - Alec's working-class Scottish dialect feels natural and unforced, while his mother's voice carries the practical wisdom and restrained affection typical of her generation and background. The correspondence spans nearly two years, allowing for genuine character development. The story's structure is well-crafted, moving from Alec's initial isolation through crisis to eventual healing. The lighthouse setting serves both literal and metaphorical purposes effectively. The author avoids clichéd treatments of isolation and depression, instead presenting Alec's struggles with psychological realism. His trauma from the shipyard accident and subsequent survivor's guilt from the drowning incident create believable motivations for his self-imposed exile. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery that captures the harsh beauty of the Scottish coast without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "skerries stretching out like broken teeth" and "the sea was green as bottle glass" feel organic to Alec's voice rather than imposed by the author. The mother's voice is equally well-realized, with her practical wisdom delivered through memorable lines like "breakfast after misery" and "Things do not stop being useful because they are ugly." The emotional arc feels earned rather than manipulative. Alec's gradual healing occurs through multiple factors - work, correspondence with his mother, the relationship with Isla, and professional counseling - rather than through a single transformative moment. The introduction of Isla Fraser is handled with restraint, avoiding romantic clichés while still providing hope for connection. The story's resolution strikes an appropriate balance. Alec's transfer to shore duty represents growth rather than defeat, and his final reflection on the lighthouse - that it "kept me for a time when I had nowhere else to put myself. Then it let me go" - provides satisfying closure without false optimism. Minor weaknesses include some predictable elements in the romantic subplot and occasionally heavy-handed symbolism around light and darkness. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall achievement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 84.8
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I don’t do this on purpose. That’s the first lie I tell myself. I notice things. That’s the cleaner version. Other people see a lock and think privacy. I see a lock and think implementation. I see habits, defaults, lazy assumptions, human patches over structural flaws. Years ago, I used to turn that noticing into access. Now I turn it into reports, invoices, and sternly worded slide decks with titles like **Attack Surface Review Q3**. White hat, above board, taxes paid. Reformed, if you like the religious framing of a career pivot. But reform doesn’t uninstall the mindset. It just adds paperwork. So when I sit down across from Mara at a little dimly lit restaurant with Edison bulbs and reclaimed wood tables, I’m trying—actively, consciously trying—not to threat-model the evening. Don’t scan the room. Don’t note the camera angles. Don’t clock the server balancing the POS tablet in one hand with the login PIN stuck in muscle memory. Don’t look at the QR code menu and wonder whether it resolves through a third-party analytics domain with six trackers and a typo-squatted fallback. Look at her. Mara is laughing before she’s fully sat down, apologizing for being two minutes late like she’s confessing to a felony. Curly hair tucked behind one ear, silver ring on her thumb, black nail polish chipped at the edges in a way that suggests an actual life rather than a curated one. She smells faintly like rain and bookstore. My brain, traitorously, notices that too. This is a date, I remind myself. Not reconnaissance. The first five minutes go surprisingly smoothly. That should have been my second clue I was in danger. Good dates are destabilizing because they bypass all the defensive architecture. You can’t hide behind cynicism if the other person is funny in exactly the frequency your nervous system recognizes as home. We do the opening exchange. Jobs, neighborhoods, the mutually agreed-upon ritual complaint about parking. She asks what I do, and I say, “Cybersecurity,” in the tone of someone admitting they own several disappointing jackets. Her face lights up. “Oh no,” she says. “You’re one of those people who tells everyone to use a password manager at parties.” “I am exactly one of those people.” “I knew it. You have the vibe.” “What vibe is that?” “Like if I said I reuse passwords, you’d physically recoil.” I smile. “Only internally.” Good. Easy. Banter with load-bearing beams. Then the server asks if we want drinks, and Mara glances at me and says, “Can we make this easier? I looked you up a little.” Normal people hear flirtation in that. I hear open-source intelligence. “LinkedIn only,” she says quickly, and I can feel the exact shape of my expression because she laughs. “Relax, Mr. Cybersecurity. You make it sound illegal.” “It’s not illegal,” I say. “Just efficient.” And then somehow, through pathways I won’t even pretend to understand, we discover that we both played Magic: The Gathering in high school, left for years, and came back in adulthood with enough disposable income to make the hobby meaningfully more dangerous. This is where the evening turns. Not bad. Just vivid. “Wait,” she says, leaning forward. “Don’t tell me you were a blue player.” I put a hand to my chest. “I was a control player because I was a lonely child with trust issues.” She points at me like she’s called a bluff. “I knew it.” “And you?” “Gruul, mostly. Then I had a phase where I thought black-green made me mysterious.” “Did it?” “No,” she says. “It made me lose to counterspells.” That gets me, actually gets me, and I laugh loud enough that the couple at the next table glance over. The walls come down not all at once but in little packets. We trade commander stories, bad beat stories, the names of cards that still trigger emotional responses years later. We bond over the absurdity of spending real money on illustrated cardboard because some goblin has haste. For twenty solid minutes I am just a person. Not an auditor of weak points. Not a catalog of exploit chains wrapped in a button-down shirt. Just a man on a date with a woman who understands why “I had lethal on board” can be spoken with the gravity of a eulogy. And because the universe despises unmonitored optimism, that’s when her phone buzzes. She pulls it from her bag and glances at the screen. The notification preview reflects in her eyes for a moment. “My sister,” she says. “She’s checking whether I got murdered by my internet date.” “Tell her to wait until dessert before making the call.” She snorts, then with no hesitation at all traces her unlock pattern on the screen. I see it once. That’s the problem. People think the skill is in brute force or scripts or custom tooling. Sometimes the skill is just in seeing. The pattern is one of those painfully common ones, starting top left, dragging down, across, a right angle with all the elegance of a child drawing a chair. Worse, she does it high, visible, wrist turned outward. No shielding. No decoy movement. And because the glass catches the light just so, I can see the residual smudge too, a ghost of access. My mouth moves before governance can convene. “That pattern is really guessable.” Silence. Not a dramatic, cinematic silence. Worse. A small human silence. A silence with blink rate. Mara looks up slowly. “I’m sorry?” And there it is: the point of failure. User-facing. Catastrophic. I should backspace immediately. Laugh it off. Say I was joking. Say I notice these things professionally and then, crucially, stop talking. Instead I say, “I just mean if someone saw you do that once, they could probably reproduce it.” Abortive process. Unauthorized thread continues running. Her eyebrows lift. “Did you just watch me unlock my phone?” No good answer exists because the true answer is yes, reflexively, involuntarily, the same way other people notice eye color or whether someone has an accent. “Not intentionally,” I say, which is true and sounds absolutely false. “Wow.” I can practically watch her permissions narrow. The evening had open ports a minute ago. Now every service is behind a firewall. “I know that sounded bad,” I say. “It did.” “I work in security. I notice insecure things.” “Including my phone. On a first date.” I want to explain that there are categories in my head, that the world constantly presents itself to me as layers of trust assumptions, that seeing a weak unlock pattern is like hearing a smoke alarm chirp in an otherwise pleasant room. But people generally don’t enjoy being informed that their casual gestures have become entries in your internal risk register. She sets the phone face-down on the table. And then, because fate is lazy and likes to pile on, she says, “My mom’s the same way. Her password for everything used to be my birthday.” I close my mouth so hard my teeth almost click. Don’t say it. Don’t say anything. She is telling a family anecdote, not offering challenge questions like a game show host. Mara takes a sip of water. “I had to make her change it after someone got into her email after that whole thing with our dog’s name.” I stare at my own drink like it contains the correct social behavior dissolved in ice. Dog’s name. Mother. Birthday. Sister. All evening she’s left a breadcrumb trail without knowing it. Earlier she mentioned growing up in Cedar Rapids. She referenced her first school when telling a story about getting detention for drawing a Black Lotus on her math homework. She named the street she learned to drive on because it “still gives me parallel parking trauma.” To me they land with the sharp little clicks of tumblers aligning. Not because I want them to. Because my brain has spent too many years learning how identity is stitched together from trivia. It is a grotesque intimacy, this involuntary mapping. She notices my expression and leans back. “You did it again.” “What?” “That look. Like you’re connecting dots.” I exhale through my nose. Honesty is high risk, uncertain yield. “Yes,” I say. “At least you’re honest.” “I’m trying to be.” She studies me for a second, not unkindly. “Okay. Explain it to me without making me want to leave.” Fair. More than fair. So I do triage. “I spent a long time training myself to see where systems fail,” I say. “And people are part of systems, which is uncomfortable but true. Weak passwords, predictable habits, answers to security questions hidden in ordinary conversation—that stuff jumps out at me. Not because I’m trying to invade anyone’s privacy. Mostly because I’ve seen how little it takes.” “Mostly?” I wince. “That was poor phrasing.” “A little.” “I’m not trying to profile you,” I say. “I’m trying not to. There’s a difference, even if it’s currently not doing me any favors.” She laughs once despite herself, which feels like a server coming back online after an outage. “So what, I’ve been accidentally doxxing myself over appetizers?” “A bit,” I say, then immediately regret the precision. Her eyes narrow in amused horror. “Oh my god.” “I can help you lock things down,” I say. “Not now. I mean, unless this date is going so catastrophically that your consolation prize is a personal security audit.” “That is a truly deranged offer.” “It’s what I have.” The server arrives with our food at exactly the right moment, setting plates between us like a diplomatic intervention. We both thank him with the excessive sincerity of people grateful for a protocol break. For a minute we focus on eating. I try to let the conversation die with dignity. Let it reset. People recover from awkwardness all the time. Dates are stress tests in nicer clothes. Then Mara, who either has courage or terrible instincts, says, “So how bad is the pattern? Like, statistically.” I look up. “You want the truth?” “I absolutely don’t, which is why yes.” “It’s bad.” She groans and covers her face. “But fixable.” “Please never say ‘attack surface’ to me during dinner.” I hadn’t realized I was about to. That gets another laugh out of her, and this one opens something. Not all the way, but enough. We negotiate a truce. I agree to stop live-commenting on her vulnerabilities. She agrees that if she ever asks, I can tell her the top three things that make normal people easiest to compromise. We return, improbably, to Magic. She tells me her favorite card art of all time. I tell her about the deck I built around an idea so stupid it circled back to genius exactly once. The rest of the night balances on that edge between salvage and sabotage. Every time she mentions a detail from her life, I force myself not to sort it by exploitability. Every time her phone lights up, I look pointedly at my plate. This is what reform actually is, I think. Not purity. Restraint. The decision, repeated until boring, not to turn access into action. When the check comes, she picks up her phone again and catches my eye before unlocking it. “Don’t,” she says. “I wasn’t going to.” “Good.” Her finger hovers, then she smiles, changes the angle so I can’t see, and unlocks it shielded in her palm. It is, absurdly, one of the most attractive things I’ve ever seen. Outside, under the restaurant’s too-warm patio lights, we linger in that awkward, loaded way people do when deciding whether a night has earned a sequel. “So,” she says. “If there is a second date, are you going to spend it harvesting my childhood memories for account recovery?” “No,” I say. “I’ll bring a privacy policy.” She rolls her eyes. “That was bad.” “But not disqualifying?” She considers me. “Tentatively patched,” she says. I laugh. I can’t help it. Then she kisses me, brief and amused, and walks toward her car with her phone in her hand and, I notice with private relief, her screen timeout set very short. I notice things. That’s the truth. The better truth is that noticing isn’t the same as taking. The best truth—the one I’m still learning—is that intimacy is not access, and trust is not a puzzle someone leaves on the table for me to solve.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a challenging premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout—a reformed hacker whose technical mindset creates both humor and genuine interpersonal friction. The internal monologue feels authentic, capturing someone genuinely trying to suppress ingrained habits while being unable to turn off their analytical nature. The technical metaphors are particularly well-executed, never feeling forced or overly clever. Phrases like "permissions narrow" and "tentatively patched" emerge naturally from the character's worldview. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest provides genuine bonding that makes the relationship feel earned rather than convenient. The central conflict—the protagonist's inability to stop noticing security vulnerabilities—creates real tension without being melodramatic. The phone unlock pattern incident feels like a genuine social misstep that could derail the evening, and the protagonist's internal struggle between honesty and social appropriateness rings true. The character development is subtle but effective. The protagonist shows genuine self-awareness about their problematic tendencies while still being unable to fully control them. Mara emerges as more than just a foil—her responses feel realistic, showing both reasonable concern and eventual understanding. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with particularly strong moments like "intimacy is not access, and trust is not a puzzle someone leaves on the table for me to solve." The technical language never overwhelms the emotional core of the story. The resolution strikes an appropriate balance—neither completely redemptive nor pessimistic. The protagonist hasn't magically overcome their nature, but has found someone willing to navigate it with them. The final kiss and "tentatively patched" exchange feels earned through the evening's genuine connection and mutual understanding. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors feel slightly strained, and some descriptions that edge toward being overly polished. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.7
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The projector squeaks every time Ms. Hernandez changes the slide, like a rubber shoe twisting on a gym floor. It happens every forty seconds or so, and every time it does, my shoulders jump a little even though I know it’s coming. Squeak. New bullet points. Squeak. Another diagram of the water filtration model we’re supposed to build with sand, gravel, charcoal, and whatever else is in the supply bins at the side of the room. I should be thinking about the project. I am thinking about the project. Sort of. Mostly I am thinking about the fact that I am sitting next to Eli Mercer, who is leaning over our lab sheet with his pencil tucked behind one ear like he is the kind of person who naturally knows where to put a pencil, which seems like the kind of thing people are just born understanding. His handwriting is slanted and fast and messy in a confident way, as opposed to mine, which is neat in a nervous way, like every letter is afraid of being judged. Okay. Say something normal. Normal things: “So, do you want to do the hypothesis first?” That is fine. That is an academically reasonable sentence. I could say that. Except I already know what could happen if I say it. He could say, “Sure,” and then there would be a silence while he waits for me to continue, and I would have already used up my prepared sentence and would need another one immediately, which is like stepping off one stair and realizing there are eight more and everyone is watching how your feet work. Alternative: “Do you think the charcoal will make the biggest difference?” That sounds smart. Slightly scientific. Not too eager. But what if he says, “I don’t know,” and then I have to either explain why I asked, which risks sounding like I’m trying too hard, or laugh, which I never do correctly on command. My fake laugh always comes out half a second late, as if my body has to file a request first. He glances at me. Just a glance. Normal. Human. Not loaded with meaning. Probably. “Should we get the materials?” he says. This is my moment. An actual opening. A very simple question with several possible normal answers, most of them one word long. “Yeah,” I say. Good. Fine. Great. The word leaves my mouth in a voice that sounds smaller than the one I used in my head. In my head I sound calm and dry and sort of witty in a subtle way. Out loud I sound like a person apologizing for existing near a locker. He stands up and I stand up too quickly, banging my knee into the desk. Great. Not horribly hard. It barely makes a sound. Except to me it sounds like a drum solo announcing: SHE HAS LEGS AND DOESN’T KNOW HOW TO USE THEM. Don’t touch your knee. If you touch your knee, that acknowledges the knee incident. If you limp, that’s worse. Walk normally. Not too fast. Not too stiff. Arms natural. What do natural arms do? Swing, probably. But not too much. Too much swinging looks cheerful, and cheerful would be suspicious because why am I cheerful? Because I’m carrying gravel with Eli? That would be pathetic. The projector squeaks again as Ms. Hernandez flips to the safety slide, and half the class groans because she always includes the safety slide even when our most dangerous equipment is a plastic spoon. The squeak cuts through the room and gives me one second where no one can hear my pulse. At the supply bins, Eli picks up the charcoal container and says, “This stuff looks like fish food.” I have exactly one chance to respond in a way that suggests I am a person capable of banter. Option one: “Yeah, if you had a goth fish.” No. Too weird. Maybe funny, but weird-first funny. He might think I spend all day preparing jokes about hypothetical fish subcultures, which unfortunately is close enough to my real personality to be dangerous. Option two: laugh and say, “Totally.” That is safest, but also forgettable. Bland. Beige. If I am beige now, I will be beige forever. Option three: “I think fish food smells worse.” This is fact-based and contributes to the conversation, but it also sounds like I have recently smelled fish food on purpose. He is still looking at the charcoal. I say, “Yeah, kind of.” Kind of. The loneliest phrase in the English language. A phrase with all the flavor of wet paper towel. He nods as if this was an acceptable contribution, which almost makes it worse. If he looked disappointed, then at least I would know I had failed in a measurable way. But he just keeps being nice and normal, and nice normal people are the hardest to talk to because they leave so much room for you to ruin things yourself. Back at the table, he starts filling in the procedure section. I sit down carefully this time. My knee and I have come to an understanding. I should help. I am helping. I am looking at the worksheet with tremendous intensity. The problem is that my brain is split into twelve tabs. Tab one: science. We need to layer the materials from coarsest to finest. Gravel first? Or cotton balls first? No, cotton last, maybe. Tab two: posture. Are my shoulders weird? If I hunch, I look insecure. If I sit up straight, I look like I’m trying to look confident, which is another kind of insecurity wearing better clothes. Tab three: face. What is my face doing right now? My neutral face has been called “upset” by multiple relatives, which is rude but not inaccurate. Should I soften it? How do people soften their faces? Think of puppies? No, then I smile to myself for no visible reason and look unhinged. Tab four: Eli’s hand is very close to mine on the table and I need to act like that fact has no effect on my nervous system, which currently appears to believe we are being hunted. He says, “Do you want to pour or write?” This is another test disguised as a question. Not really. Probably not. But maybe a little. If I say write, do I seem controlling and boring? If I say pour, do I risk spilling something and dying on the spot from shame? If I say “either one is fine,” that sounds flexible, but also passive, like I am one step from saying “whatever you want” in a tragic voice while disappearing into wallpaper. “Um, I can write,” I say. Why did I say um? I didn’t need um. The sentence worked perfectly without um. Um is verbal tripping. Um is hanging a sign on your words that says THESE WEREN’T READY YET. But he just slides the paper toward me. “Cool.” Cool. Cool is good. Cool is not “weird.” Cool is not “why are you acting like that.” I write down the steps while he starts arranging the cup and filter. My handwriting gets extra neat when I’m anxious, which means every letter I make now looks like it belongs on a sign in a historical museum. He probably thinks I’m a perfectionist. Which I am, except not in the glamorous way where you win awards and color-code binders. In the bad way where you spend ten minutes deciding whether adding an exclamation point to a text makes you look enthusiastic or unstable. Say something else. You need to say at least one more thing or this whole partnership will pass and he’ll remember you as Silent Girl Who Knows How to Form Cursive Capital Letters. Maybe ask about his weekend. No, too personal? Not personal-personal, but what if he says he went somewhere with friends and then I have to respond in a way that doesn’t reveal I spent most of Saturday rereading old messages to make sure none of my replies were secretly embarrassing. Maybe mention his drawing from art class, the one everyone liked. Too obvious. That sounds like I noticed him specifically, which I did, but there are laws against letting that be visible. Maybe keep it project-related. Safer. Boring, but safe. I clear my throat, then instantly regret the throat-clearing because now it seems like an announcement, like Attention everyone, she has manufactured a comment. “What do you think we should put first?” I ask, pointing at the materials. There. Good. Functional. Low risk. He looks at the bins. “Probably gravel, then sand, then charcoal? And cotton at the bottom?” “I thought cotton at the top,” I say, and the words come out before I can overedit them. He pauses. That pause contains my whole life. Then he says, “Oh, yeah, that makes more sense.” For one bright microscopic second, I feel almost normal. Better than normal, actually. Useful. Like a person participating in a conversation instead of trying to sneak through one undetected. I should build on that. This is where socially capable people naturally continue. Maybe: “Because it catches the bigger particles first.” Good addition. Smart, concise. But now that I’ve had one decent moment, the pressure doubles. If I speak again and stumble, it will cancel the first success. I know that is not mathematically true, but my brain doesn’t care about math unless it’s using it against me. So I just nod like this was always my plan. He smiles a little and starts rearranging the materials. “Good catch.” Do not overreact to good catch. Do not treasure it and replay it later seventeen times. Do not imagine what it would be like if he said your name like that. Too late. The projector squeaks again. Ms. Hernandez is explaining contamination variables to the class, but her voice feels far away, like it’s coming through water. Around us everybody is talking in easy overlapping bursts. Chairs scrape. Someone laughs loudly at the back table. There are girls in my class who can say anything. They can walk up to anyone and make a joke and lean on a desk and exist with their whole bodies like they’ve signed some permission slip I never got. I always feel like I need clearance. Like before every sentence, some invisible committee has to review it for possible humiliation. Don’t twirl your pencil. You’re twirling your pencil. Stop. I stop so suddenly the pencil slips from my fingers and rolls toward Eli. He catches it. “Thanks,” I say, too fast. “No problem.” No problem. Of course no problem. For him, things really are no problem. Dropped pencil, normal. Group project, normal. Sitting next to another person without mentally drafting and redrafting every sentence, probably normal. I wonder what it would be like to just say what I mean when I mean it. Not all of it, obviously. I’m not insane. I’m not going to turn to him in the middle of third-period science and say, Hi, sometimes when you answer questions in class I think about that answer for an entire day because you looked happy while explaining it. I’m aware there are levels of honesty that get you escorted into a new school. But maybe some smaller version. Something real. I look at the filter cup we’ve built together, all these separate layers that are supposed to catch what doesn’t belong and let the acceptable parts through. It feels a little too on the nose, honestly. He taps the worksheet. “Can you write that the cotton goes first?” “I already did,” I say. He leans in to look, and his shoulder brushes mine for less than a second. My whole body immediately becomes a government facility after a security breach. He grins. “Nice.” Say something playful. This is the opening. Tiny, but real. I could say, “I’m occasionally ahead of the curve.” Too cocky? Maybe charming-cocky, but only if delivered by someone with a functioning personality. I could say, “Miracles happen.” No. Too self-insulting. Don’t do that thing where you act like you’re kidding but actually hand people a script for how to underestimate you. I could just smile. I do. A small one, probably uneven, but real. And for once I don’t immediately audit it afterward. Not much, anyway. The projector squeaks again. Ms. Hernandez tells us we have fifteen minutes left. Eli starts pouring the dirty water into the filter, and we both lean forward to watch it drip through the layers, slower than I expected, clearer than before. I want to say something meaningful and effortless and exactly the right amount of interesting. I want to be the version of me that exists perfectly in my head. Instead I hear myself say, quietly but not inaudibly, “It’s kind of working.” He glances at me and smiles. “Yeah. It is.” Kind of, I think. Yeah. It is.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative that captures the authentic voice of an anxious 8th-grade girl with remarkable precision. The author successfully constructs the entire piece almost entirely through internal monologue, creating a vivid portrait of social anxiety through the protagonist's overthinking patterns. The characterization is particularly strong. The narrator's voice feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending or cartoonish. Her internal sophistication contrasted with her external awkwardness is expertly rendered through specific details like her "neat in a nervous way" handwriting versus Eli's "confident" messiness, and her observation that her fake laugh "always comes out half a second late, as if my body has to file a request first." These details demonstrate sophisticated psychological insight. The prose style effectively mirrors the character's mental state through fragmented thoughts, rapid topic shifts, and the clever "twelve tabs" metaphor that perfectly captures how anxiety fractures attention. The writing maintains consistent quality throughout, with particularly strong moments like the description of socially capable girls who "exist with their whole bodies like they've signed some permission slip I never got." The dialogue, though minimal as requested, feels natural and age-appropriate. The interactions between the narrator and Eli are believable and serve the story's emotional arc effectively. The author avoids the trap of making dialogue overly clever or unrealistic for middle schoolers. The piece succeeds in showing rather than telling the protagonist's anxiety through concrete actions and thoughts rather than abstract descriptions. The recurring projector squeak serves as both a realistic detail and a structural device that grounds the reader in the scene while highlighting the narrator's hypervigilance. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - the small moment of connection feels earned rather than artificially positive, and the protagonist's growth is minimal and realistic rather than transformative. The final exchange about the water filter "kind of working" serves as a subtle metaphor without being heavy-handed. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal voice becomes slightly too articulate for an 8th grader, though these instances are rare. The piece also occasionally borders on being slightly overwrought in its anxiety descriptions, but this generally serves the character rather than detracting from it. The writing demonstrates strong technical control, with sentences that flow naturally and maintain reader engagement throughout. The piece successfully balances humor and pathos, creating an emotionally complex portrait that avoids both cynicism and false optimism. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
My phone is at three percent, which feels like a verdict. I keep tapping the side button just to make sure the screen still wakes, as if the battery might have changed its mind and decided to die while I wasn't looking. Three percent. His last text is still there, stupidly normal. Running ten behind. Parking is hell. Don’t leave, okay? Want to see you. Want to see you. I read that part over and over until it starts to sound staged, like something a person says because they know they should say it. Maybe he added it because he could tell from my last message—No worries, take your time—that I was trying too hard to sound unbothered. Maybe he heard the strain in it even through text. Maybe he knows me better now, enough to start managing me. Don’t leave, okay? As if I would. As if I’m the unreliable one. As if he’s already framing the story in a way where I’m the difficult, dramatic person who might storm off because he’s ten minutes late. Which, to be fair, is exactly the kind of thing I would do in my head twenty times before doing absolutely nothing in real life except sit here and peel the edge of my thumbnail until it stings. Two percent. Of course. Of course my phone is dying tonight, the one night I need a lifeline. The one night when if he cancels, or ghosts, or sends some careful, devastating text from his car—You’re great, I just think maybe we moved too fast—I’ll have to receive it in a burst of cold light and then vanish off the grid before I can answer. Maybe that’s for the best. Maybe a dead phone would save me from saying something humiliating. Maybe he’d think I’m ignoring him and then I’d become, in one tiny and efficient move, exactly what I’m always afraid people think I am: punishing, manipulative, unstable. He is not going to say that, I tell myself. And immediately another voice answers, Not in those words. He doesn’t have to. I can hear it anyway, the conversation that hasn’t happened. Look, I care about you, but this is intense. I’m not asking for intensity, I’d say back, already stung. I’m asking for consistency. There’s a difference. And he’d sigh in that patient way people do when they think they’re dealing with someone unreasonable. I’ve just been busy. Busy enough not to text all day? I was at work. People who want to hear from you make time. Then there it is, his face changing, the small withdrawal, the invisible door closing: I can’t do this. That one gets me in the chest every time, even though it’s entirely mine. My own ventriloquism. My own private puppet show where everyone I love eventually speaks in the same voice. A bus exhales at the curb. Someone laughs too loudly. I look up every few seconds expecting to see him approaching with that half-apologetic smile that usually melts me before I can decide to be angry. I hate that it works. I hate that I need it. The truth is he has only been “distant” in the way normal people are distant when they are tired, or working, or distracted, or existing outside of me. He did say this week was bad. He did call me two nights ago from the grocery store just to ask whether I still hated cilantro and then stayed on the phone the whole way through checkout. He did say, last weekend, forehead pressed to mine, I really like this. He did fall asleep with his hand curled in my shirt like he was afraid I’d leave the bed. And still. Still there’s this cold administrative office in my head processing evidence for a case against him. Exhibit A: fewer texts. Exhibit B: a longer pause before “miss you too.” Exhibit C: tonight he is late. I know what I’m doing. That’s the worst part, maybe. I can watch the machinery operate while still being dragged through it. I can say, This is your abandonment stuff. This is you trying to outrun hurt by predicting it first. And the thought barely slows anything down. It just adds another layer, because then I get to feel ashamed of being predictable in my pain. One percent. I should put the phone away. Save it for when he gets here. Save it in case something actually happens. But if I put it away, I can’t keep checking whether he’s texted. If I can’t check, then anything could be happening. He could be typing right now. He could be deciding how to phrase it kindly. He could be sitting in his car thinking about whether sleeping with me was a mistake. Stop. I actually say that one silently but sharply enough that my shoulders jump. He is parking. Parking is hell. You know this part of town. You know he is always late and always embarrassed about it. You know this. Yes, I know. And I know a hundred true things that somehow do not weigh as much as one imagined one. The battery dies in my hand. There’s a moment of pure naked panic, ridiculous and total. The black screen feels personal. I stare at my own faint reflection and have the insane thought that now if he doesn’t come, I won’t even know why, and that uncertainty is somehow worse than certainty. Maybe that’s a lie. Maybe certainty is the thing I can’t survive. Fine. Fine. Maybe this is better. No more checking. No more evidence. No more— “Hey.” My whole body flips. He’s there, a little breathless, hair wind-messed, scarf half off one shoulder. He looks worried. Not distant. Worried. “I’m so sorry,” he says, and reaches for my arm before he even fully stops moving, thumb brushing once over my sleeve. “I couldn’t find parking and then I saw your phone go dark and I thought, great, she’s going to think I died or stood her up.” She. Not you in the abstract. Me. I laugh, but it comes out thin. “I was considering several catastrophic options.” “I know.” He smiles, but softly, not mockingly. “Come here.” And this is the part that should fix it. This should be the part that fixes everything. He opens his coat and folds me into him on the sidewalk like I’m someone he’s relieved to reach. I feel the cold in his jacket and the heat underneath it. His chin touches the top of my head. “I missed you,” he says into my hair, simply, with no performance in it at all. Something in me surges toward him so fast it almost hurts. Need, relief, grief, love, hunger—it all lights up at once. I want to crawl inside his voice and live there. I want to believe him so completely that I stop having to do this. I hold on too tightly and then immediately become aware that I’m holding on too tightly. He leans back to look at me. “You okay?” No, I think. I am being hugged by someone who wants me and already half preparing for the future version of this scene that proves I imagined all of it. I am thirsty in the middle of water. I am so relieved I could cry, which means in five minutes I will distrust the relief and start looking for the trick. What I say is, “Yeah. My phone died.” “I figured.” He rubs my knuckles. “I brought that charger you left at my place, by the way. You always think I’m trying to steal your stuff.” I did think that. Not seriously-seriously, but enough to feel the pinch of it. Enough to make a joke last week about how maybe he liked collecting traces of me because traces were easier than the whole thing. He’d looked confused, then kissed my forehead and said, “Or maybe I wanted an excuse to see you again.” I had felt adored for exactly twelve seconds before wondering if that line had been too smooth, too ready. Now he is handing me the charger from his bag like evidence for the defense. I almost laugh at how badly I want this tiny practical kindness. How quickly my chest fills with it. He remembered. He brought it. He thought ahead to my dead phone and me stranded in my own mind. He knows me. He cares. Unless, a voice starts, he’s doing this because— Enough. Just for one second, enough. I look at him, really look. At the crease between his brows from hurrying. At the way he is still touching me like contact is a sentence he hasn’t finished. At the uncomplicated warmth on his face. None of it matches the courtroom in my head. None of it matches the speech I rehearsed where I accuse him of drifting and he calmly confirms my worst fears. This doesn’t mean those fears are gone. They’re not. They’re crouched right there, waiting to reinterpret everything. He missed you because he feels guilty. He hugged you because he could tell you were fragile. He brought the charger because he is careful with difficult people. I know the pattern. I know how greedily it feeds. Love enters and the disorder asks for documents. But for a moment—small, selective, fragile—I know this too: he is here. He is late and apologetic and kind. He wants to see me. He said so, and then he came. “Hey,” he says again, quieter this time. “Where’d you go?” Nowhere good, I think. I let out a breath. “Can we go inside?” “Yeah,” he says, smiling. “I’ve got you.” And I hate how much I need to hear that. And I need to hear it again.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of borderline personality disorder and the internal experience of anticipating rejection. The writer successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone caught between desperate need for connection and protective cynicism. The narrative structure is well-executed, moving seamlessly between catastrophic internal predictions and the contrasting reality of genuine care from the love interest. The characterization is particularly strong. The protagonist's voice feels authentic and consistent throughout - the way they analyze every micro-interaction, construct elaborate defensive scenarios, and then immediately question any positive validation rings true to the psychological condition being portrayed. The cognitive dissonance is expertly rendered, especially in moments like "I am thirsty in the middle of water" and the recognition that "Love enters and the disorder asks for documents." The prose quality is generally high, with effective metaphors like the "cold administrative office in my head processing evidence" and "my own private puppet show where everyone I love eventually speaks in the same voice." These images capture complex psychological states without becoming overwrought. The writing maintains an intimate, immediate quality that draws the reader into the protagonist's mental state. The piece adheres well to the prompt's requirements, incorporating the imagined dialogues, the cycle of anticipation and resentment, and the contrast between feared rejection and actual acceptance. The limited self-awareness is portrayed convincingly - the protagonist can identify their patterns but cannot escape them. However, there are some areas where the execution could be stronger. The ending, while not unrealistically positive, does lean slightly toward resolution that feels somewhat neat given the complexity of the condition being portrayed. The protagonist's moment of clarity ("Just for one second, enough") and ability to "really look" at their partner suggests a level of breakthrough that may be too tidy for someone in the throes of such intense abandonment fears. The dialogue, while minimal, serves its purpose effectively. The partner's words feel natural and caring without being unrealistically perfect. The contrast between the imagined harsh conversations and the actual gentle interaction is well-executed. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the psychological insight becomes slightly clinical rather than purely experiential, and a few instances where the metaphors, while generally effective, border on being too polished for the raw mental state being depicted. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
